Howard  Carlson  writes  he  was  unable  to  attend 
his  60th  reunion,  but  recalls  when  he  and  sev- 
eral other  WPI  students  residing  in  a  rooming 
house  saved  a  mother  and  child  overcome  by 
gas  fumes.  "We  called  ourselves  the  'Gas  House 
Gang'  after  that  near-fatal  event,  and  as  alumni 
we  often  got  together  on  a  yearly  basis." 

Charles  Clarkson  from  California  could  not 
attend  the  reunion  either.  He  has  warm  mem- 
ories of  "two  of  the  finest  teachers  that  I  ever 
met,  Dr.  Masius  and  Dr.  Morley."  According 
to  Clarkson,  they  could  solve  problems  both 
inside  and  outside  the  classroom. 

George  Walker,  WPI  trustee  emeritus,  was 
unable  to  make  reunion  because  of  his  busy 
schedule,  but  alumni  activities  have  been  "al- 
most a  full-time  extracurricular  program  for  50 
years."  In  1962  he  received  the  Taylor  award 
for  distinguished  alumni  service  to  WPI.  He 
still  walks  four  miles  a  day  or  pedals  20  miles 
a  day  on  his  exercise  bicycle.

seasons,  from  1962  to  1982.

Milton  Bradley's 
Idea  Man  Retires 

If  your  youngsters  put  a  Pac-Man  board 
game,  a  Smurf  card  game  or  the  ever- 
popular  electronic  game,  Simon,  on  their 
Christmas  lists,  you  can  thank  Jim  Hou- 
lihan, '42,  who  retired  in  June  as  vice 
president  of  R&D  at  Milton  Bradley  Co. , 
East  Longmeadow,  MA. 

As  his  career  with  MB  began  to  unfold 
nearly  40  years  ago,  Houlihan  never 
dreamed  he'd  become  the  firm's  chief  idea 
man.  "I  wanted  to  be  a  chemical  engi- 
neer," he  says.  "Following  World  War  II, 
I  joined  MB's  art  department  developing 
crayons.  Before  long,  I  transferred  over 
to  R&D  when  I  discovered  I  was  more 
interested  in  making  games  than  making 
crayons." 


For  Houlihan  the  move  was  a  good  one. 
Developing  new  games  is  big  business, 
and  he  has  the  knack  of  knowing  what  the 
game  players  of  the  world  want.  What's 
the  hottest  name  in  arcades  today?  Pac- 
Man,  of  course.  And  MB  had  the  fore- 
sight to  snare  the  contract  to  produce  the 
Pac-Man  board  game,  which  should  be  a 
hit  come  Christmas. 

What's  more,  before  any  other  toy 
company  was  interested,  MB  picked  up 
the  rights  to  produce  the  little  people 
known  as  Smurfs.  Currently,  "The  Smurfs" 
is  TV's  most  widely  watched  Saturday 
morning  cartoon  show,  and  spin-off  games 
are  now  in  the  works. 

Houlihan  also  helped  bring  out  such 
trendy  games  as  Electronic  Stratego,  Dark 
Tower,  Domination,  Knock  Knock,  and 
The  Fall  Guy — all  part  of  Milton  Brad- 
ley's latest  line  of  best-sellers. 

"I  was  responsible  for  riding  herd  on 
the  staff  as  they  created  new  games,  toys 
and  puzzles,"  he  reports.  "At  the  time  of 
my  retirement,  there  were  80  employees 
developing  non-electronic  games  and  80 
more  working  on  electronic  versions." 

Because  the  game  market  is  so  com- 
petitive (another  firm  pirated  ideas  from 
MB's  Simon  and  called  their  toy  Copy 
Cat),  Milton  Bradley  has  a  locked  security 
wing,  opened  only  to  those  who  know  a 
secret  code.  Jim  Houlihan,  of  course, 
knows  that  code  and  a  host  of  MB  trade 
secrets. 

With  his  inside  knowledge,  did  he  have 
to  go  through  a  debriefing  program  when 
he  left  Milton  Bradley? 

He  grins.  "I  guess  we  have  some  sort 
of  program  like  that." 


Frederick  Curtis,  Jr.,  holds  the  position  of 
vice  president  of  General  Dynamics,  Ft.  Worth 
Division. 

Bob  Hubley,  who  recently  retired  as  a  divi- 
sion manager  for  Western  Union  iri  Washing- 
ton, DC,  is  now  involved  with  video  advertising 
and  videotaping  weddings,  anniversaries, 
birthdays  and  reunions.  He  also  has  made  sev-

construction,  conversion  and  modification  plans.

Geza  Ziegler: 

A  Voice  For  Freedom 

In  1957,  after  the  Soviets  tightened  their 
grip  on  Hungary.  Geza  Ziegler,  '59,  a 
Hungarian  student  at  Budapest  Polytech- 
nic Institute,  fled  to  the  United  States. 
He  entered  WPI,  where  he  later  received 
both  his  BSEE  and  MSEE. 

Away  from  the  nightmare  of  Soviet 
domination,  Ziegler  became  a  willing  par- 
ticipant in  the  American  dream,  which  he 
viewed  as  the  right  to  define  personal  goals, 
the  right  to  pursue  those  goals  and  the 
right  to  receive  recognition  for  personal 
success.  In  May,  he  was  elected  a  fellow 
of  the  Bridgeport  (CT)  Engineering  In- 
stitute (BEI),  the  highest  internal  honor 
bestowed  by  the  Institute. 

Ziegler,  who  has  also  been  named  di- 
rector of  the  Danbury  branch  of  the  eve- 
ning college,  has  been  with  BEI  as  an 
instructor  and  administrator  since  1963. 
His  fellowship  award  cited  him  for  his 
"long,  honorable  and  selfless  service  to 
the  students,  faculty  and  administration." 

It  also  stated:  "Geza  Ziegler  is  an  ex- 
cellent example  of  the  advantages  af- 
forded by  the  U.S.  to  its  newly  adopted 
citizens.  However,  such  opportunities  are 
only  attainable  by  those  who  know  how 
to  grasp  and  use  them." 

During  his  19  years  with  BEI,  Ziegler 
has  served  as  assistant  chairman  of  math- 
ematics, dean  of  the  faculty  of  the  Stam- 
ford branch  and  dean  of  college  exten- 
sion. In  his  capacity  of  dean  of  college 
extension,  he  was  the  prime  mover  in  the 
establishment  of  the  BI*J  Danburv  branch 


Named  Engineering  Fellow  at  BEI 

on  the  Western  Connecticut  State  College 
campus. 

Along  with  his  BEI  post.  Ziegler  serves 
as  director  of  manufacturing  for  Cogni- 
tronics  Corporation  in  Stamford.  He  is 
vice  president  of  Stamford's  Long  Ridge 
Volunteer  Fire  Company  and  city  repre- 
sentative to  the  Area  9  Cable  Council, 
whose  operations  committee  he  chairs. 

Actively  involved  with  educational  and 
community  activities,  as  well  as  with  his 
professional  engineering  duties,  Geza 
Ziegler  represents  what  is  good  about 
America.  Thus  it's  no  mere  coincidence 
that  he  does  voluntary  free-lance  broad- 
casting for  Radio  Free  Europe  extolling 
the  American  way  of  life.

Tee  for  Two — 
And,  Maybe,  Five! 

His  oldest  son,  Joey,  5,  can  already  hit  a 
golf  ball  50  yards,  Jack  Gale,  "70,  reports 
with  parental  pride.  His  twin  sons,  Mi- 
chael and  Daniel.  3.  cannot  match  their 
big  brother's  swing,  but  it's  a  safe  bet  that 
they  will  take  a  liking  to  the  little  white 
ball.  If  golf  genes  can  be  inherited,  surely 
the  sons  of  Jack  and  Mary  Carr  Gale  have 
them  in  generous  supply. 

Take  Jack,  for  instance.  Golf  has  been 
his  life.  A  member  of  the  Professional 
Golfers'  Association,  he's  served  as  a  pro 
at  Worcester's  Green  Hill  Country  Club, 
Holden  Hills  and  the  Rochester  (NH) 
Country  Club.  This  year  he  became  pro 
at  Tatnuck  Country  Club,  introducing  the 
club  to  its  first  pro-member  tournament. 

Take  Mary.  She's  won  the  New  Hamp- 
shire women's  championship  twice.  This 


summer  she  was  champion  of  the  Worces- 
ter County  Women's  Golf  Association. 
Her  brother  is  Joe  Carr,  the  current  New 
England  PGA  champion. 

When  Jack  invited  Mary  to  join  him  for 
the  Pro-Lady  Championship  in  1978  and 
1980,  they  won  both  times.  In  1973  he  led 
his  team  to  victory  in  a  pro-member  club 
tournament  in  Melbourne,  FL.  Since  that 
time,  he  has  often  played  the  winter  golf 
circuit  in  Florida. 

Jack,  the  son  of  Joe  Gale.  ME  labo- 
ratory technician  at  WPI,  caught  the  golf 
bug  late.  "But  once  I  started  playing  in 
my  mid-teens,  I  was  hooked."  At  WPI  he 
captained  the  golf  team.  "When  I  grad- 
uated, there  weren't  many  jobs  for  me- 
chanical engineers.  I  loved  golf.  I  decided 
to  turn  pro." 

Teenage  boys  often  dream  about  be- 
coming professional  athletes.  Jack  Gale 
dreamed,  too.  The  difference  is,  he  made 
his  dreams  come  true.

COMPLETED  0\REERS 


Correction:  We  reported  erroneously  in  the 
August  Journal  that  A.  Hugh  Reid,  '11,  and 
James  K.  Healy,  '35,  had  passed  away  recently. 
Both  are  alive  and  well.  In  fact.  Mr.  and  Mrs. 
Hugh  Reid  recently  celebrated  their  70th  wed- 
ding anniversary!  We  sincerely  regret  any  dis- 
tress or  inconvenience  which  this  misprint  may 
have  caused  these  alumni,  their  families  or 
friends. 

Mrs.  Esther  Kisk  Coddard,  widow  of  Dr.  Rob- 
ert H.  Goddard,  '08,  America's  pioneer  rocket 
scientist,  died  at  her  home  in  Worcester  on 
June  5,  1982.  She  was  81  years  old. 

Dr.  Goddard,  who  launched  the  world's  first 
liquid  fuel  rocket,  died  in  1945.  Following  his 
death,  Mrs.  Goddard,  a  Worcester  native,  ed- 
ited his  papers,  obtained  patents  on  many  of 
his  inventions  and  lectured  and  traveled  world- 
wide to  receive  honors  on  his  behalf. 

After  attending  Bates  College,  Mrs.  God- 
dard graduated  from  Johns  Hopkins  University 
and  Clark  University.  Later  she  became  sec- 
retary to  the  president  of  Clark,  where  she  met 
her  future  husband  and  assisted  him  during  his 
earliest  experiments  in  rocketry.  In  1966  she 
was  honored  as  the  "first  missile-tracking  pho- 
tographer" by  a  chapter  of  the  Society  of  Photo- 
Optical  Instrumentation  Engineers. 

Others  honors  included:  "Woman  of  the 
Year"  (Worcester  Business  and  Professional 
Women's  Club),  "Mrs.  Goddard  Day"  (WPI). 
honorary  membership  in  the  American  Rocket 
Society  and  degrees  from  Clark  University, 
Anna  Maria  College  and  the  University  of 
Maryland.  In  1969.  she  became  the  first  woman 
ever  awarded  an  honorary  doctor  of  science 
degree  from  WPI 

Charles  F.  Goldthwait,  '09,  one  of  the  coun- 
try's premier  textile  chemists,  died  in  Pitts- 
burgh. PA.  on  April  29,  1982.  He  was  96. 

Dunng  his  career  he  was  with  Klearflax  Linen 
Looms  and  Baltimore  Processing  Co.  and  served 
as  a  senior  industrial  fellow  at  Mellon  Institute, 
Pittsburgh  Also,  he  was  with  the  South  Re- 
gional Research  Lab.  USDA,  in  New  Orleans 
and  a  visiting  professor  at  North  Carolina  State 
College  in  Raleigh. 


Until  four  years  ago,  he  performed  research 
on  virtually  all  natural  and  man-made  fibers. 
His  final  articles  appeared  in  the  official  jour- 
nal of  the  American  Association  of  Textile 
Chemists  and  Colorists.  of  which  he  was  a 
charter  member.  He  developed  a  semi-elastic 
cotton  gauze  bandage  for  military  use  which  is 
now  commercially  available. 

Four  times  he  was  honored  for  his  work  with 
the  USDA.  He  was  the  inventor  of  the  differ- 
ential dye  test  for  maturity  and  processing  qual- 
ity of  cotton  fiber  in  the  boll.  In  "retirement" 
at  North  Carolina  State,  he  developed  stretch 
cotton  fabric — of  considerable  importance  to 
cotton  manufacture  in  this  country. 

John  J.  Desmond,  '14,  of  Rochester,  NY.  passed 
away  on  April  28,  1982  at  the  age  of  91. 

Graduated  as  a  chemist  from  WPI,  he  worked 
for  the  Henry  Souther  Engineering  Co.  and 
Bridgeport  Brass  Co.  and  served  in  the  U.S. 
Army  from  1917  to  1919.  From  1919  to  1955 
he  was  with  North  East  Electric  Co.,  where  he 
rose  to  chief  chemist  and  metallurgist,  staying 
with  the  firm  as  Delco  appliance  division  di- 
rector when  General  Motors  acquired  North 
East. 

Mr.  Desmond  was  a  life  member  of  the 
American  Society  for  Metals,  and  a  past  pres- 
ident of  the  Rochester  chapter,  as  well  as  a 
past  member  of  the  American  Chemical  So- 
ciety. He  had  served  as  president  of  the  Roch- 
ester chapter  of  the  WPI  Alumni  Association 
and  as  WPI  Council  representative  from  1953 
to  1958.  A  native  of  Fall  River.  MA,  he  was 
born  on  May  3,  1890. 

John  M.  Bond,  '15,  of  Lakewood,  NJ,  died  on 
March  3,  1982. 

A  native  of  Providence,  RI,  "Jig"  was  born 
on  April  23,  1892.  Graduated  as  an  electrical 
engineer,  he  worked  a  short  time  for  American 
Steel  &  Wire,  Worcester.  Other  employers  were 
National  Carbon  Co.,  Westinghousc  and  the 
New  York  City  Transit  Authority. 

During  his  career,  he  developed  and  pat- 
ented an  electric  snow  mcltcr  for  track  switches 
which  became  a  standard  device  in  subway  sys- 
tems. In  1918  and  1919  he  served  with  the  U.S. 


Naval  Reserve  and  was  assigned  to  the  Exper- 
imental Station  in  New  London,  CT.  devel- 
oping anti-submarine  devices. 

A  member  of  Phi  Sigma  Kappa,  Mr.  Bond 
also  belonged  to  the  New  York  Railroad  Club, 
AIEE  and  the  American  Electric  Railway  As- 
sociation. 

Warren  W.  Parks,  '17,  WPI  trustee  emeritus, 
died  in  Cincinnati,  OH,  on  August  5,  1982. 

Born  in  Russell,  MA,  on  February  14,  1896, 
he  received  his  BSCE  in  1917.  Later  he  was 
with  Durkee,  White  &  Towne,  Boston  &  Al- 
bany Railroad  and  Fay,  Spofford  &  Thorndike. 
From  1943  to  1966,  he  was  village  manager  of 
Indian  Hill.  OH. 

As  a  former  resident  engineer  of  the  town 
of  Mariemont,  he  was  responsible  for  the  build- 
ing of  one  of  the  first  English-Norman  style 
churches  erected  since  the  12th  Century. 

Besides  serving  as  a  church  trustee,  Mr.  Parks 
provided  leadership  in  many  professional  so- 
cieties. In  1961  he  was  named  Engineer  of  the 
Year  by  the  Technical  and  Scientific  Societies 
Council  of  Cincinnati.  He  was  a  past  director 
of  ASCE  (life  member)  and  past  president  of 
the  Ohio  Municipal  League.  He  was  an  hon- 
orary member  of  Tau  Beta  Pi  and  the  father 
of  Russell  Parks,  '41. 

Arnold  H.  Woods,  '18,  a  former  insurance  ex- 
ecutive, died  on  June  3,  1982  in  Allentown, 
PA,  at  the  age  of  85. 

A  civil  engineer,  he  joined  the  State  of  Il- 
linois Highway  Department.  Later  he  was  em- 
ployed by  the  Stock  Co.  Inspection  Bureau  and 
Improved  Risk  Mutuals.  In  1945  he  founded 
the  former  Arnold  H.  Woods  Insurance  Agency 
in  Allentown. 

Mr.  Woods  belonged  to  the  Society  of  Fire 
Prevention  Engineers  and  Tau  Beta  Pi.  He  was 
a  past  president  of  the  Lehigh  Association  of 
Insurance  Agents  in  Allentown  and  an  original 
member  of  the  Certified  Property  and  Casualty 
Underwriters.  During  World  War  I  he  served 
as  an  officer  in  the  U.S.  Navy.  He  was  born 
on  May  16,  1897  in  Lunenburg,  MA. 

Percy  G.  Sharpe,  '20,  died  on  April  18,  1982 
in  Atlantis,  FL.  He  was  83  years  old. 

A  native  of  Ware,  MA,  he  was  employed 
for  42  years  by  the  Marlboro  Savings  Bank, 
which  he  served  as  assistant  treasurer.  He  re- 
tired in  1968.  He  belonged  to  the  Baptist  Church 
and  the  Masons. 

Lawrence  K.  Hyde,  '22,  of  Gladwyne.  PA,  for- 
merly the  assistant  to  the  vice  president  of 
Baldwin-Lima-Hamilton  Corp.,  died  suddenly 
on  June  3,  1982.  He  was  born  on  April  6,  1901 
in  Westfield,  MA. 

During  his  career,  he  was  with  the  National 
Bureau  of  Standards  and  O.S.  Peters  Co. 
(president),  Washington,  DC,  and  McCoUum 
Exploration  Co.,  Houston,  TX.  For  30  years 
he  was  employed  by  Baldwin-Lima-Hamilton 
Corp.,  Waltham,  MA.  After  retirement  from 
the  latter  firm,  he  served  as  manager  of  the 
Testing  Machine  Division  at  Wiedemann  Ma- 
chine Co.  in  King  of  Prussia.  PA. 

Mr.  Hyde  belonged  to  ATO  and  earned  his 
BSME  at  George  Washington  University.  Other 
memberships  included  Sigma  Tau,  ASM, 
ASTM,  ISA  and  the  Washington  Society  of 
Engineers.  He  was  a  registered  professional 
engineer  in  Washington,  DC.  He  was  a  former 


44  WPI  JOURNAL 


vice  president  of  Civitan  International  (DC), 
a  past  president  of  the  Washington  chapter  of 
the  WPI  Alumni  Association  and  the  father  of 
John  Hyde,  '56. 

Donald  McAllister,  '23,  of  Palos  Verdes  Es- 
tates, CA,  a  former  employee  of  Robertshaw 
Controls,  died  on  March  28,  1982. 

He  was  born  on  May  8,  1902  in  Adams,  MA, 
and  received  his  BSME  from  WPI,  where  he 
belonged  to  ATO  and  Skull.  He  worked  for 
T.K.  McAllister  &  Co.,  and  General  Fibre  Box 
Co.,  both  of  Springfield,  MA,  as  well  as  Wor- 
thington  Pump  of  Holyoke.  Other  employers 
were  North  American  Aviation  and  Grayson 
Controls  in  California.  He  was  retired  from 
Robertshaw  Controls  of  Long  Beach.  An  Army 
veteran,  he  rose  to  the  rank  of  major. 

Harold  P.  Latimer,  '25,  a  long-time  employee 
of  Simonds  Saw  &  Steel,  Fitchburg,  MA,  passed 
away  on  January  31,  1982. 

A  graduate  mechanical  engineer,  he  joined 
Simonds,  where  he  retired  as  a  sales  engineer 
in  1960.  Later  he  was  with  Industrial  Supphes, 
Inc. 

Mr.  Latimer,  a  32-degree  Mason  and  Shri- 
ner,  belonged  to  Phi  Sigma  Kappa  and  Skull. 
He  resided  in  Laguna  Hills,  CA,  and  was  born 
on  February  12,  1903  in  Leominster,  MA. 

Paul  R.  Nelson,  '30,  a  former  control  engineer 
at  Norton  Co.,  died  on  June  15,  1982  in  Cape 
Cod  Hospital,  Hyannis,  MA.  He  was  79. 

He  received  his  BSEE  from  WPI.  For  35 
years,  until  his  retirement  in  1960,  he  was  with 
Norton  Co.,  Worcester,  which  he  served  as  a 
manufacturing  control  engineer. 

Mr.  Nelson  was  born  on  November  27,  1902 
in  Worcester.  Besides  attending  Bates  College, 
Lewiston,  ME,  he  did  graduate  work  at  Boston 
University.  He  belonged  to  Phi  Sigma  Kappa, 
the  Norton  25-Year  Club  and  the  Norton 
Foreman's  Club.  He  was  past  master  of  Mon- 
tacute  Masonic  Lodge. 

Warren  N.  Doubleday,  '31,  Quabbin  Reservoir 
historian,  died  at  his  home  in  New  Salem,  MA, 
on  May  2,  1982  at  the  age  of  72.  He  was  born 
in  North  Dana,  MA,  on  February  8,  1910. 

Graduated  as  an  electrical  engineer,  from 
1934  to  1937  he  was  an  engineer  for  the  Quab- 
bin Reservoir  project,  in  which  his  hometown. 
North  Dana,  was  flooded.  Prior  to  his  retire- 
ment in  1972,  he  was  a  civilian  employee  for 
the  Department  of  Defense  Air  Force  Eastern 
Communications  Region. 

Mr.  Doubleday  was  a  well-known  Quabbin 
Reservoir  historian.  He  often  lectured  about 
pre-flood  days  in  the  area  and  had  memorabilia 
which  he  displayed.  A  past  president  of  the 
Swift  River  Valley  Historical  Society,  Mr. 
Doubleday  had  also  been  a  trustee  of  New  Salem 
Academy  and  a  member  of  the  Congregational 
Church,  the  Masons  and  Lambda  Chi  Alpha. 

Manning  HolofT,  '33,  president  of  Manning 
Holoff  Co. ,  manufacturers  of  Magna-Lite,  Los 
Angeles,  CA,  passed  away  on  March  28,  1982. 
A  Worcester  native,  he  was  born  on  March 
27,  1911.  After  graduating  as  a  mechanical  en- 
gineer from  WPI,  his  employers  included  Pratt 
&  Whitney  and  Ranger  Aircraft.  In  World  War 
II  he  was  chief  production  engineer  with  Rolls 
Royce,  then  works  manager  at  Indian  Motor- 
cycle in  Springfield,  MA,  and  production  man- 


ager with  McCulloch  Motors  in  California. 

Later  he  formed  Holoff  Construction  Co., 
and  built  custom  homes  in  the  Los  Angeles 
area.  In  the  1970s  he  developed  Magna-Lite, 
an  improved  lighted  magnifying  glass,  and 
founded  his  own  firm  to  manufacture  the  prod- 
uct. Recently  he  designed  a  large  reader  now 
sold  worldwide. 

William  F.  Atwood,  Jr.,  '36,  formerly  of  Wal- 
tham,  MA,  died  recently. 

He  was  born  on  July  27,  1913  in  Waltham. 
During  his  career  he  was  with  General  Dyestuff 
Corporation,  Boston,  D.E.  Cummings  Co., 
Newport,  ME,  and  Pacific  Mills,  Lawrence, 
MA.  Also  he  worked  for  the  Boston  Naval 
Shipyard  and  the  Philadelphia  Naval  Shipyard. 
He  received  his  BS  from  WPI  in  1936. 

Marvin  Handleman,  '41,  a  retired  chief  engi- 
neer for  the  American  Forces  Network  in  Ber- 
lin, West  Germany,  died  in  Hyannis,  MA,  on 
June  17,  1982.  He  was  63  years  old. 

Mr.  Handleman  was  born  on  August  6,  1918 
in  Paxton,  MA.  In  addition  to  WPI,  he  at- 
tended the  RCA  Institute  in  New  York  City. 
During  World  War  II,  he  was  in  the  U.S.  Army 
Signal  Corps  in  Europe.  From  1946  to  1948  he 
was  with  his  family's  real  estate  management 
business.  Later  he  joined  Radio  Free  Europe 
as  an  electronics  engineer  and  then  the  Amer- 
ican Forces  Network  in  Berlin  as  chief  engi- 
neer. He  belonged  to  the  Institute  of  Radio 
Engineers  and  the  IEEE. 

Stanley  S.  Ribb,  '41,  the  recently  retired  pres- 
ident of  Blackstone  Valley  Electric  Co.,  died 
in  Pawtucket,  RI,  on  June  4,  1982  at  the  age 
of  64. 

An  electrical  engineer,  he  joined  General 
Electric,  staying  with  the  firm  until  1959,  when 
he  went  with  Blackstone  Valley  Electric.  In 
1961  he  was  named  chief  engineer,  in  1968, 
vice  president  and  in  1971,  president. 

Mr.  Ribb  was  a  native  of  Worcester  and  a 
member  of  Sigma  Xi.  He  belonged  to  the  Ro- 
tary Club,  the  Masons,  the  Rhode  Island  Com- 
modores, the  Providence  Engineering  Society 
and  the  Electrical  League  of  Rhode  Island. 

Active  with  the  Boys'  Club  and  Community 
Library  Association,  he  was  also  a  past  chair- 
man of  the  Providence  AIEE  section  and  a 
former  vice  president  of  the  Rhode  Island 
chapter  of  the  WPI  Alumni  Association.  Dur- 
ing World  War  II  he  was  with  the  U.S.  Navy. 

John  H.  Quinn,  Jr.,  '42,  of  Santa  Rosa,  CA, 
passed  away  recently. 

He  was  born  on  July  19,  1920  in  Fitchburg, 
MA.  After  receiving  his  BSME,  he  joined 
NACA,  Langley  Field,  VA,  as  an  aerody- 
namics research  scientist.  Later  he  was  em- 
ployed by  Chance  Vought  Aircraft  and  Temco 
Aircraft.  After  leaving  Lockheed  Missiles  & 
Space  Co.,  Sunnyvale,  CA,  and  Martin  Co., 
Denver,  he  went  with  McDonnell-Douglas 
Astronautics  from  which  he  retired  in  1976  fol- 
lowing 34  years  of  continuous  employment  in 
aerospace. 

One  of  the  highlights  of  his  career  came  when 
he  helped  load  the  first  Polaris  submarine. 

Frank  S.  Jurczak,  '50,  died  unexpectedly  on 
June  10,  1982  in  New  Britain  (CT)  General 
Hospital.  He  was  58  years  old. 
Before  retiring  in  1981,  he  had  been  em- 


ployed by  United  Technologies  Corp.  and  its 
subsidiaries  for  31  years.  While  with  the  firm, 
he  served  in  various  capacities,  including  test 
engineer  for  Pratt  &  Whitney  Aircraft,  East 
Hartford  and  as  manager  of  control  systems  at 
Turbo-Power  &  Marine,  Inc.,  Farmington. 

He  was  a  past  grand  knight  for  the  Knights 
of  Columbus.  Besides  being  involved  with 
Scouting,  he  belonged  to  Phi  Kappa  Theta, 
Tau  Beta  Pi,  Sigma  Xi  and  the  ASME.  He  was 
a  World  War  II  Army  veteran,  was  born  on 
December  16,  1923  in  New  Britain,  CT,  and 
received  his  BSME  from  WPI. 

John  E.  Leach,  '53,  class  head  agent,  died  of 
an  apparent  heart  attack  at  his  home  in  Fort 
Madison,  lA,  on  May  29,  1982. 

He  had  been  plant  manager  for  Du  Pont  in 
Fort  Madison  for  the  past  six  years,  having 
originally  joined  Du  Pont  in  1953  in  Philadel- 
phia. During  his  career  with  the  company  he 
held  numerous  manufacturing  and  technical  as- 
signments. He  was  mainly  involved  with  mak- 
ing paint  and  allied  polymers. 

Recently,  Mr.  Leach  was  instrumental  in  Du 
Pont's  donating  land  for  a  proposed  Fort  Mad- 
ison branch  library.  He  was  also  a  member  of 
Sigma  Phi  Epsilon,  the  Elks  and  the  local  coun- 
try club.  A  native  of  Rockingham,  VT,  he  was 
born  on  October  3,  1931.  He  received  his  BSCE 
from  WPI  in  1953  and  then  served  with  the 
U.S.  Army. 

Lt.  Col.  Thomas  J.  Merchant,  SIM'62  U.S. 
Army  Ret.,  died  June  23,  1982  in  Worcester 
at  age  60. 

He  was  born  in  Westboro,  MA,  graduated 
from  Wentworth  Institute  and  did  graduate  work 
at  WPI,  Holy  Cross  and  MIT.  Since  1949  he 
had  worked  for  Bay  State  Abrasives,  West- 
boro, MA,  where  he  was  an  environmental  and 
safety  coordinator. 

Mr.  Merchant  was  a  veteran  of  the  U.S.  Army 
Corps  of  Engineers  and  saw  action  in  World 
War  II  and  Korea.  He  belonged  to  the  Amer- 
ican Society  of  Safety  Engineers,  the  Reserve 
Officers  Association  (DC)  and  several  other 
military,  church  and  community  organizations. 
He  had  belonged  to  the  Westboro  town  advi- 
sory board  and  had  served  as  chairman  of  the 
board  of  selectmen. 

Roger  W.  Williams,  '65,  a  long-time  employee 
of  General  Electric  Co.,  died  at  his  home  in 
Lunenburg,  MA,  on  May  21,  1982  at  the  age 
of  44. 

After  graduating  from  the  University  of 
Maine,  he  received  his  MSME  from  WPI.  He 
worked  for  22  years  as  a  mechanical  engineer 
at  the  GE  plant  in  Fitchburg  and  was  the  re- 
cipient of  the  GE  Electric  Power  Systems  Sec- 
tor Award  in  1979. 

Mr.  Williams  was  also  affiliated  with  Tau 
Beta  Pi,  the  ASME  and  the  Elfun  Management 
Association  and  was  a  registered  engineer  and 
land  surveyor  in  Massachusetts.  He  was  born 
in  Westbrook,  ME. 

Joel  S.  Schoenholtz,  '68,  of  Upper  Saddle  River, 
NJ,  passed  away  in  May. 

A  native  of  New  York  City,  he  was  born  on 
March  24,  1945.  He  graduated  as  a  civil  engi- 
neer from  WPI.  During  his  career,  he  had  been 
with  Bethlehem  Steel  and  Custom  Chemical 
Co.,  Elmwood  Park,  NJ,  where  he  was  plant 
manager.  He  belonged  to  AEPi  and  Skull.

Equation  Fluency  Test 


This  test  does  not  measure  your  iritelli- 
gence,  your  fluency  with  words,  and  cer- 
tainly not  your  mathematical  ability.  It 
will,  however,  give  you  some  gauge  of 
your  mental  flexibility  and  creativity.  In 
the  years  since  the  test  was  developed, 
we've  found  few  people  who  could  solve 
more  than  half  the  24  questions  on  the 
first  try.  Many,  however,  reported  get- 
ting answers  long  after  the  test  had  been 
set  aside— particularly  at  unexpected  mo- 
ments when  their  minds  were  relaxed, 
and  some  reported  solving  all  the  ques- 
tions over  a  period  of  several  days.  Take 
this  as  your  personal  challenge. 

Instructions:  Each  equation  contains  the 
initials  of  words  that  will  make  it  correct. 
Finish  the  missing  words.  For  example: 
26  =  L.  of  the  A.  would  be  26  =  Let- 
ters of  the  Alphabet.  Correct — at  least 
plausible — answers  appear  below

A  MESSAGE 


The  WPI  Alumnus: 
A  Special  Breed 

A  message  from  Peter  H.  Horstmann, 
'55,  President,  WPI  Alumni  Association 

It  isn't  every  day  that  an  engineer-turned- 
businessman  gets  an  opportunity  to  au- 
thor an  article  for  the  WPI  Journal,  and 
it's  a  double  blessing  when  the  subject  is 
one  of  my  favorites — the  WPI  Alumni 
Association.  Let  me  share  with  you  our 
objectives  for  the  current  year  and  my 
sense  of  strength  and  vitality  in  our  alumni 
organization. 

An  integral  part  of  the  excellence  that 
surrounds  WPI  is  its  alumni  body.  At 
15,000  strong  and  growing,  we  are  by  far 
the  largest  group  within  the  college  com- 
munity. We  are  amazingly  diverse  and 
ever-changing.  More  than  300  women  now 
call  WPI  their  alma  mater.  Engineers — 
or  technological  humanists,  as  the  Plan 
now  defines  them — still  predominate,  but 
our  breadth  extends  to  doctors  and  den- 
tists, presidents  and  professional  athletes, 
consultants  and  legislators. 

Judging  by  our  achievements.  WPI  has 
indeed  educated  us  well  for  professional. 
as  well  as  community  and  personal,  life. 
Our  alumni  not  only  design,  develop  and 
build  around  the  world,  we  also  start  suc- 
cessful businesses,  make  scientific  discov- 
eries, lead  major  corporations,  and  gen- 
erally display  our  talents  in  all  sorts  of 
ways.  We  are  a  group  of  contributors — 
to  business  and  industry,  to  science  and 
technology,  to  our  communities  and  vol- 
unteer agencies,  and,  of  course,  to  our 
college.  It's  a  record  to  be  proud  of. 

The  Alumni  Association  exists  to  serve 
the  needs  of  alumni,  and  in  so  doing  to 
benefit  the  college.  These  needs  range  from 
a  general  desire  to  remam  mvolved  and 
in  touch  with  college  affairs,  to  more  spe- 
cific expressions  of  interest  and  commit- 
ment. 

The  annual  objectives  and  long-range 


goals  of  the  Association  are  designed  to 
meet  these  very  needs.  For  1982-83,  your 
Association  has  established  the  following 
objectives: 

1.  To  promote  growth  and  develop- 
ment of  regionally  oriented  programs, 
including  Regional  Clubs  and  the 
Corporate  Contacts  and  Alumni  Ad- 
missions programs.  This  focus  on  re- 
gional programs  is  a  direct  result  of 
widespread  alumni  interest  and  is  in- 
tended to  exploit  the  regional  com- 
monality among  the  several  programs 
to  their  collective  benefit. 

2.  To  enhance  the  image  and  profile 
of  WPI  alumni.  For  many  years,  and 
in  many  ways,  our  alumni  have  ex- 
pressed a  feeling  that,  as  WPI  alumni, 
we  do  not  always  experience  the  same 
degree  of  respect  and  recognition  of 
excellence  that  the  college  itself  does. 
In  fact,  the  general  public  is  not  al- 
ways as  aware  of  WPI's  excellence  as 
it  could  be.  Our  intention  is  to  help 
shine  a  brighter  light  on  both  our 
alumni  and  our  college. 

3.  To  study  the  organization  of  our 
Alumni  Office,  identifying  staff  and 
organizational  needs  for  the  future. 

4.  To  surpass  the  $1 -million  mark  in 
this  year's  Alumni  Fund. 

5.  To  maintain  the  excellence  of  all 
Association  programs  and  to  evaluate 
objectively  the  contributions  of  each. 
By  any  standard,  these  are  ambitious 

objectives.  They  demand  our  best  efforts 
in  order  to  succeed.  I  have  no  cioiiht.  how- 
ever, that  we  are  dealing  from  a  position 
of  strength  as  we  endeavor  to  reach  these 
goals.  Our  Association  aiul  its  |irourams 
have  made  tremendous  strides  in  recent 
years,  to  the  point  where  we  now  play  in 
the  major  leagues  ot  alumni  organiza- 
tions. 

Well  over  2,00(1  aliiinni  \oliinteers  work 
actively  on  behalf  ol  the  Association.  A 
total  of  6.000  of  us  either  volunleer  or 
participate  in  one  or  more  of  our  pro- 


grams. Last  year  alumni  contributions  to 
the  college  exceeded  $800,000,  and  we  are 
responsible,  directly  and  indirectly,  for 
distributing  more  than  $150,000  in  un- 
dergraduate scholarship  aid.  Our  Alumni 
Admissions  Program  reached  out  across 
the  country,  making  local  contact  with 
more  than    1,200  prospective  students. 

Our  Alumni  Office  staff  is  exceedingly 
talented  and  conscientious.  Their  contri- 
butions are  immense  and  invaluable.  As 
to  our  alumni  volunteers,  I  am  continually 
delighted  and  impressed  by  the  quality 
and  capabilities  of  the  men  and  women 
who  enthusiastically  and  effectively  do  the 
work  of  our  Association. 

You  should  know  that  the  efforts  and 
results  of  our  programs  have  been  widely 
heralded.  The  WPI  Alumni  Fund  is  one 
of  only  three  in  the  nation  to  be  recog- 
nized by  the  Council  for  the  Advance- 
ment and  Support  of  Education  (CASE) 
and  the  U.S.  Steel  Foundation  for  four 
consecutive  years  of  sustained  excellence 
in  college  fund  raising.  Our  alumni  pub- 
lications have  been  recognized  nationally 
for  excellence  in  design  and  presentation. 
Such  widespread  attention  is  important, 
to  be  sure,  but  our  most  gratifying  rec- 
ognition comes  from  WPI  itself.  From  the 
top  down,  the  college  is  strongly  com- 
mitted in  its  appreciation  and  support  of 
the  Alumni  Associaticm  and  our  pro- 
grams. 

Happily,  such  commitment  works  both 
ways.  As  we  work  to  benefit  WPI,  wc  gain 
a  continuing  sense  of  involvement  with  an 
institution  of  excellence,  the  pride  that 
comes  from  accomplishing  something 
worthwhile  for  our  alma  mater,  and  the 
pleasure  of  associating  with  the  marvelous 
people  found  in  the  WPI  community. 

We  as  alumni  are  a  key  element  of  the 
excellence  that  is  WPI.  .lust  as  WPI  is  a 
special  institution,  so  our  alumni  body  is 
truly  a  special  breed,  and  we  can  be  jus- 
tifiably proud  of  our  role  in  the  growth 
and  success  of  our  alma  mater.

The  WPI  Journal  (ISSN  0148-6128)  is  pub- 
lished quarterly  for  the  WPI  Alumni  Associ- 
ation by  Worcester  Polytechnic  Institute  in  co- 
operation with  the  Alumni  Magazine  Consor- 
tium, with  editorial  offices  at  the  Johns  Hop- 
kins University,  Baltimore,  MD  21218.  Pages 
I-XVI  are  published  for  the  Alumni  Magazine 
Consortium  (Franklin  and  Marshall  College, 
Hartwick  College,  Johns  Hopkins  University, 
Rensselaer  Polytechnic  Institute,  Worcester 
Polytechnic  Institute)  and  appear  in  the  re- 
spective alumni  magazines  of  those  institu- 
tions. Second  class  postage  paid  at  Worcester, 
MA,  and  additional  mailing  offices.  Pages  1- 
12,  29-40  ©  1983,  Worcester  Polytechnic  In- 
stitute. Pages  I-XVI  ©  1983,  Johns  Hopkips 
University.

Editor,  Elise  Hancock;  Business  Manager, 
Robert  Hewes;  Production  Coordinator,  Wendy 
Williams-Hauck;  Managing  Editor,  Mary  Ruth 
Yoe;  Designer,  Allen  Carroll;  Magazine  Fel- 
low, Pat  Rushin;  Senior  Writer,  Robert  Kanigel; 
Editorial  Assistant,  Edward  C.  Ernst. 

Advisory  Board  for  the  Alumni  Magazine  Con- 
sortium: Franklin  and  Marshall  College,  John

lege, Philip  Benoit  and  Merrilee  Gomillion; 
Johns  Hopkins  University,  B.  J.  Norris  and 
Elise  Hancock;  Rensselaer  Polytechnic  Insti- 
tute, Lynn  Holley  and  Robert  M.  Whitaker; 
Worcester  Polytechnic  Institute,  Thomas  J. 
Denney  and  Kenneth  L.  McDonnell. 

Acknowledgments:  Typesetting,  Foto  Typeset- 
ters, Inc.;  Printing,  John  D.  Lucas  Printing 
Company;  Mailing.  Circular  Advertising  Com- 
pany.

presented  in  the  magazine.  These  views  do  not

or  official  policies  of  WPI.  Address  corre-

NEWS  FROM  THE  HILL 


A  Legacy  of  Excellence 

Pictured  above  are  students — a  portion  of 
the  WPI  Class  of  1986— who  gathered  with 
their  families  for  Freshman  Orientation 
Day  on  August  28.  Incidentally,  each  of 


these  incoming  students  has  one  parent 
who  is  a  WPI  grad.  Congratulations  and 
best  wishes  to  these  present  and  future 
alumni  and  their  families! 

Besides  the  12  freshmen  pictured,  an- 
other 14  sons  and  daughters  of  alumni 


entered  WPI  in  September.  And  of  the 
alumni  below,  one  gets  special  mention: 
Helen  G.  Vassallo.  With  a  1981  MBA 
from  WPI.  Vassallo  (who  also  has  her  MD) 
is  now  associate  professor  of  management 
at  WPI.

FEBRUARY  1983 


When  Isaac  Asimov,  author  of 
more  than  260  science-fiction 
books,  recently  addressed  a 
spellbound  WPI  crowd  on  technology's 
role  in  history,  he  reminded  us  that  it  was 
he  who  coined  the  term  robotics.  To  take 
such  things  a  step  further,  the  term  robot, 
from  the  Czech  robota.  or  serf,  was  pop- 
ularized a  half-century  ago  by  the  Czech 
playwright  Karel  Capek. 

Today  robot  is  a  household  term,  and 
robotics  is  a  concept  ballyhooed  in  count- 
less board  rooms.  But  in  the  same  way 
that  the  uninitiated  may  conceive  of  ro- 
bots only  in  the  likeness  of  Star  Wars's 
R2D2,  executives  bent  on  bringing  to  their 
assembly  lines  what  they  may  consider  to 
be  state-of-the-art  gadgetry  may  be 
astounded  at  the  complexity  of  bringing 
to  their  plants  true  robotics — systems  of 
computerized,  reprogrammable  machin- 
ery that  can  move  parts  or  manipulate 
tools  through  pre-specified  sequences. 

The  stakes  involved  in  getting  up  to 
speed  in  today's  robotics  can  be  equally 
astounding.  Time  and  again,  robots  have 
been  shown  to  improve  productivity,  re- 
lieve workers  of  tedious,  dangerous  tasks, 
and  contribute  to  the  company's  compet- 
itive health.  But  all  for  a  price:  starting 
at  $30,000  and  soaring  into  the  millions 
for  hardware  alone,  not  to  mention  costs 
like  installation,  interfacing  with  existing 
machinery  and  maintenance. 

Of  robotics,  says  Professor  Donald  N. 
Zwiep,  Head  of  Mechanical  Engineering 
and  Director  of  WPI's  Manufacturing  En- 
gineering Applications  Center  (MEAC), 
"A  revolution  is  occurring  on  the  plant 
floor,  requiring  top-down  commitment  to 
rapid  deployment  of  existing  technol- 
ogy." The  manufacturers  first  to  gain  a 
foothold  in  this  emerging  field,  he  con- 
tends, will  get  a  head  start  in  domestic, 
and  possibly  foreign,  markets.  It  is  widely 
acknowledged  that  the  U.S.  is  hard  pressed 
to  compete  successfully  with  Japan  in  sales 
of  manufactured  goods,  in  productivity, 
and  in  profits.  In  large  part,  say  experts, 
this  state  of  affairs  is  due  to  Japan's  wide- 
spread application  of  robotic  technolo- 
gies— technologies  available,  and  often 
developed,  in  the  U.S.  "Developing  a 
corporate  critical  mass  in  robotics  is  es- 
sential to  effective  applicati(5n  of  robot- 
ics," says  Zwicp.  That,  and  exposing  stu- 
dents to  real-world  robotics  problems,  is 
MEAC's  mission. 

Enter  Hartford-based  Emhart  Cor- 
poration,  a  $1.8-billion  multina- 
tional giant  engaged  principally  in 
metalworkmg  manufacturing — shoe  ma- 


WPI  JOURNAL 


Getting  a  Gripper 
on  the  Future 


Robots  are  revolutionizing  the  plant  floor, 
forcing  industry  to  advance  quickly  on  the 
robotics  learning  curve.  WPI's  response  to 
this  challenge  starts  with  a  no-nonsense 
corporate-academic  bond. 

By  Kenneth  McDonnell 


terials  and  machinery,  glass  container  ma- 
chinery, rubber,  plastics  and  gear  ma- 
chinery, as  well  as  hardware  and  chemicals 
production.  Many  of  these  are  ideal  for 
robotics  applications. 

Walter  L.  Abel,  '39  ME,  is  Senior  Fel- 
low, Technology,  at  Emhart.  Abel  came 
to  Emhart  with  United  Shoe  Machinery 
when  Emhart  acquired  that  firm  in  1976. 
He  brought  with  him  ideas.  He  also 
brought  the  hard  realization  that  the  ro- 
botics revolution  would  surely  leave  the 
mighty  Emhart  foundering  in  the  wake  if 
the  conglomerate  didn't  act  quickly  and 
decisively. 

"We  built  a  robot  back  then,"  Abel 
recalled  recently,  "but  that  was  before 
microcomputers  came  along  and  enabled 
greatly  expanded  memories  and  flexibility 
in  robots."  Leave  robot  building  to  the 
robot  builders,  he  decided — Unimation, 
Cincinnati  Milacron,  GE,  ASEA,  and  so 
on. 

Concentrating  on  applying  existing  ro- 
botic technology,  Abel  realized,  made 
more  sense.  But  a  major  obstacle  re- 
mained, of  course:  finding  and  tapping 
the  resources  capable  of  actually  bringing 
these  advanced  technologies  onto  the  plant 
floor — "a  tricky  problem  all  users  are  going 
to  experience,"  he  contends.  He  ap- 
proached several  colleges  with  the  idea  of 
an  applications  center  before  it  became 
clear  to  him  that  WPi  possessed  the  unique 
balance  of  needed  resources. 

Abel  sits  on  the  Board  of  WPI's  Man- 


agement Department.  "Literally  over 
a  cup  of  coffee,  as  is  often  the  case," 
says  Arthur  Gerstenfeld,  Department 
Head  and  MEAC  founder,  "we  real- 
ized that  by  bringing  Emhart  engineers 
and  state-of-the-art  robots  into  the  neu- 
tral college  environment  and  conduct- 
ing student  projects,  faculty  research  and 
cooperative  WPI-Emhart  exercises,  we 
could  achieve  several  joint  objectives. 

"We  wanted  to  help  Emhart  get  its  ro- 
botics program  up  and  going,  establish  a 
growing  body  of  robotics  expertise  at  WPL 
and  prepare  future  engineers  for  the 
booming  growth  expected  in  robotics."  In 
reaching  these  goals,  he  says,  MEAC  has 
managed  to  avoid  the  classical  industry- 
academic  relationship,  where  industry 
provides  monies  to  the  college,  to  be  ap- 
plied toward  some  study  resulting  in  a  re- 
port seldom  read.  "No,  we  wanted — and 
have  achieved — a  true  partnership,  where 
each  side  gives  and  gains." 

In  March  1981 ,  MEAC  became  a  real- 
ity, when  Emhart  robots  were  wheeled 
into  WPI's  Washburn  Shops.  Now, 
located  in  an  expanded  Iliggins  Labs  set- 
ting, pending  Washburn  renovations,  the 
Center  provides  just  the  kinds  of  re- 
sources envisioned  in  those  earlier  Board 
coffee  breaks.  "Industry  needs  applica- 
tions quickly."  says  Abel,  "but  not  by  sac- 
rificing security."  Students  are  risk-tak- 
ers, he  believes,  forever  coming  up  with 
new  ideas.  "MEAC  enables  us  to  road 


Robots'  reach,  speed,  agility  and  cost 

help  determine  their  manufacturing 

applications.  ME  AC  engineers,  faculty 

and  students  match  company 

needs  with  specific  robotic 

capabilities.  Drawing  by 

Charlotte  Wharton. 


test  these  and  our  own  ideas  before  they 
reach  the  production  line,  thus  ensuring 
that  the  bugs  are  out  before  the  chips  are 
down." 

Several  projects  have  been  completed; 
others  are  under  way  or  planned.  The  first 
was  an  ideal  robotic  application:  endur- 
ance testing  of  pop  rivet  guns.  Normally 
a  human  task,  it  is  a  monotonous  and 
mentally  fatiguing  proposition,  requiring 
up  to  a  million  manual  loadings,  firings, 
and  ejections  of  spent  rivets.  Working 
closely,  WPI  faculty,  students  and  tech- 
nical advisors  combined  expertise  with 


Emhart  engineers  to  define  the  problem 
at  hand,  and  then  to  develop  an  automatic 
rivet  feeder  system,  a  loading  and  firing 
mechanism  (employing  a  Unimate  600  ro- 
bot) and  a  counter,  as  well  as  a  monitor 
to  record  precisely  what  takes  place 
(something  humans  do  not  perform  so 
carefully). 

A  second  project  used  a  Unimate  robot 
to  automatically  combine  two  press  op- 
erations in  the  production  of  doorknobs. 
Fetching  rough  parts,  feeding  them  into 
a  drawing  and  then  a  trim  press,  as  well 
as  simultaneously  lubricating  the  parts. 


were  the  Unimate's  appointed  tasks.  De- 
livery of  both  the  tool-tester  and  the  door- 
knob systems  was  achieved  four  months 
ahead  of  schedule.  Needless  to  say,  this 
experience  has  left  Emhart,  WPI  and  many 
in  the  industrial  world  very  pleased. 

Once  WPI  and  Emhart  had  decided  that 
such  a  close  academic-corporate  inter- 
change was  possible,  a  third  project  was 
undertaken.  This  project  addressed  ro- 
botized lock  assembly,  encompassing  ma- 
chine loading  and  materials  handling,  a 
ubiquitous  activity  in  industry  today,  but 
one  that  can  often  benefit  greatly  from 
the  use  of  robotics.  Here,  not  one  but  two 
robots  were  programmed — to  work  as  a 
team.  "The  beauty  of  this  project,"  says 
Gerstenfeld,  "was  that  the  actions  of  one 
robot  instructed  those  of  another.  We  were 
moving  away  from  islands  of  robots  work- 
ing independently  toward  networks  of  au- 
tomation— to  true  robotics,"  where,  he 
says,  the  output  of  one  robot  becomes  the 
input  of  another,  all  monitored  by  a  com- 
mon host  computer. 

Currently,  Professor  Robert  L.  Norton 
(ME)  supervises  some  25  students  en- 
gaged in  qualifying  projects  focusing  on 
several  aspects  of  Emhart's  operation. 
"They  call  themselves  the  'shoe-shankers' 
and  the  iock-polishers,'  "  he  says.  They 
work  in  what  he  terms  competitive  teams, 
with  each  team  approaching  the  same 
problem  from  a  different  viewpoint. 

The  shoe-shankers,  he  explains,  are  ap- 
plying existing  robotics  to  the  task  of 
wrapping  shoe  shanks,  thin  metal  plates 
that  stiffen  the  shoe  with  tape  before  they 
are  inserted  between  layers  of  sole  leather. 
Correct  taping  is  crucial,  he  says.  "Next 
time  your  shoes  creak  and  squeak  as  you 
walk,"  he  adds  wryly,  "you'll  know  that 
someone  got  careless  on  the  shoe  shank 
line."  And  no  wonder,  considering  how 
boring  the  taping  job  can  be  for  the  worker. 

Each  student  team  is  examining,  much 
as  a  consulting  engineer  would  do,  the 
feasibility  of  robotizing  the  taping  pro- 
cess: The  teams  visit  the  USM  plant,  iden- 
tify the  problem,  and  conduct  extensive 
background  research — including  an  over- 
view of  the  shoe  shank  industry,  Emhart's 
place  in  that  business,  shoe  manufactur- 
ers' needs,  and  robots  available  for  the 
task.  Finally,  each  team  specifies  the  tasks 
necessary  to  solve  the  problem. 

Similarly,  the  lock-polishers  investigate 
how  robots  can  provide  more  consistent, 
economical  results  in  a  traditionally  labor- 
intensive,  physically  demanding  task.  Both 
projects  will  include  post-implementation 
analysis  of  the  robots"  effectiveness,  and 
revision  and  improvement  of  the  systems.

Says  Norton.  "Emhart's  receptiveness  to 
these  projects  has  been  fantastic." 

Last  year."  according  to  Professor 
Ben  Gordon,  "65  ME.  former 
Technical  Director  of  MEAC. 
"WPI  graduated  18  mechanical  engineer- 
ing students  with  robotics  experience." 
With  computer-aided  design  and  manu- 
facturing (CAD/CAM)  now  the  hot  item 
in  industry,  he  believes  recruiters  coming 
to  campus  will  be  more  and  more  inter- 
ested in  seniors  with  hands-on  experience 
in  these  exciting  new  fields.  Future  MEAC 
projects  will  focus  on  integrating  CAD 
and  CAM.  interfacing  with  WPI's  CAD 
lab.  and  thus  minimizing  the  need  for  hu- 
man intervention  in  the  design-manufac- 
turing process.  (See  "The  CAD/CAM 
Connection."  W^?lJounml.  August  1982.) 
Yet  the  kinds  of  projects  completed  and 
envisioned  for  MEAC  cut  across  several 
disciplines,  according  to  Morton  Fine,  '37 
CE.  MEAC's  Program  Administrator. 
They  encompass  electrical  engineering, 
computer  science,  social  sciences,  and 
other  fields. 

Currently,  robotics  installations  in  the 
U.S.  number  only  about  5.000.  while  Ja- 
pan boasts  three  times  this  figure.  But 
sales  are  taking  off.  say  industry  experts, 
reflecting  shifts  in  consumer  preferences 
c   and  growing  demand  for  customized 
I   products.  Because  of  this  need  for  greater 
Q  product  variety  and  quality,  financial  an- 
I  alysts  expect  sales  to  soar  from  $100  mil- 
S  lion  in  1980  to  $2  billion  by  decade's  end.

WPI   JOURNAL 


And  if  IBM  plunges  into  the  field,  say 
most,  the  market  could  balloon  even  more. 

"At  Emhart,"  says  Wally  Abel,  "we're 
gearing  up  now  for  the  competitive  battles 
we  see  looming  on  the  horizon  as  a  result 
of  available  and  rapidly  developing  ro- 
botics technologies."  MEAC,  he  be- 
lieves, has  provided  the  competitive  edge 
Emhart  naturally  seeks. 

"The  beauty  of  MEAC,"  says  Tech- 
nical Director  David  Asmus,  "is  that  it's 
not  just  a  think  tank.  Applications  are 
what  industry  needs,  and  adapting  state- 
of-the-art  technologies  to  unique  situa- 
tions— often  requiring  wholesale  conver- 
sion of  stock  robots — is  MEAC's  value  to 
industrv  and  education." 


Though  MEAC  presently  brings  to 
campus  only  Emhart  projects  and 
the  robots  owned  or  leased  by  the 
company,  WPI  is  committed  to  welcom- 
ing additional  corporations  into  this  un- 
common partnership.  "When  it  comes  to 
robotics,  companies  are  talking  to  each 
other,"  Director  Zwiep  says,  "naturally 
not  about  product,  but  certainly  about 
process."  Japan's  perceived  lead  in  man- 
ufacturing, he  says,  has  broken  through 
many  a  barrier  between  U.S.  firms  com- 
peting with  the  Japanese  and  other  Asian 
and  European  nations  in  tight  markets. 

Together  with  MEAC's  sister  program, 
the  Center  for  Management  of  Advanced 
Automation  Technology  (MA AT),  fledg- 


ling robotics  users  can  now  develop  more 
prudent  approaches  to  applying  and  man- 
aging existing  automated  technologies.  In 
the  future,  MEAC  will  also  invite  com- 
panies to  attend  short  courses,  briefing 
sessions,  and  tours,  enabHng  a  modest  in- 
vestment to  result  in  real  benefits  without 
total  commitment  of  the  firm. 

According  to  Dean  of  Faculty  Ray  E. 
Bolz,  accommodating  MEAC's  antici- 
pated growth  will  call  for  the  college  to 
acquire  several  of  its  own  robots  for  ed- 
ucational and  corporate  projects,  to  com- 
plement those  brought  in  by  MEAC  par- 
ticipating companies.  "By  next  summer," 
he  says,  "we  plan  to  have  several  more 
firms  and  robots  on  board." 


The  Eyes  Have  It 

An  increasing  variety  of  automated 
.tasks,  from  welding  and  grinding  ir- 
regular surfaces,  to  inspecting  parts  and 
monitoring  empirical  research,  to  con- 
trolhng  video-guided  missiles  and  fore- 
casting the  weather,  call  for  continual  ad- 
justment of  the  machinery  as  the  task 
proceeds.  Normally  easy  for  humans,  sen- 
sing and  responding  to  ever-changing 
stimuli  are  exceedingly  difficult  for  robots 
and  other  automated  equipment.  And  not 
surprisingly,  developing  even  rudimen- 
tary vision  to  assist  automation,  for  ex- 
ample, in  orienting  work  pieces  or  iden- 
tifying damaged  parts,  looms  as  one  of 
the  stickiest  challenges  facing  research- 
ers. 

"Image  processing,  or  computerized  vi- 
sion," says  Professor  Robert  J.  Schalkoff , 
EE,  "is  an  area  of  artificial  intelligence 
which,  when  fully  developed,  will  imbue 
automation  with  a  large  dose  of  'blue-col- 
lar' humanness.  The  difficulty  in  simulat- 
ing vision  through  known  means  helps  us 
appreciate  just  how  marvelous  and  often 
taken  for  granted  our  sense  of  sight  is." 

Schalkoff  leads  a  small  but  active  group 
of  faculty  and  student  researchers  looking 
at  alternative  ways  of  coupling  cameras 
and  computers.  The  hard  part,  he  be- 
lieves, is  not  in  enabling  robots  to  see,  but 
in  getting  them  to  reason  visually,  to 
"think,"  if  you  will. 

Today's  most  advanced  robots,  for  ex- 
ample, use  any  of  several  vision  systems, 
such  as  digital  comparison  of  computer- 
converted  camera  images  with  templates 
stored  in  the  computer's  memory.  When 
the  computer  "discovers"  a  similar  set  of 
information — such  as  the  area,  perimeter. 


and  diameter  of  an  object — it  "recog- 
nizes" instantly  what  it  is  looking  at,  but 
only  if  object  and  template  match  closely 
enough.  Other  methods  are  available,  but 
the  results  are  similar:  To  be  commer- 
cially feasible  and  as  effective  as  human 
sight — perceiving  depth,  seeing  in  stereo, 
or  merely  distinguishing  objects — current 
vision  systems  would  have  to  be  a  million 
times  faster  and  far  more  adaptable. 

The  research  of  Schalkoff  and  others  in 
EE,  including  Professors  John  A.  Orr  and 
David  Cyganski,  indicates  that  a  major 
roadblock  to  effective  image  processing  is 
insufficient — or  inappropriate — com- 
puter power.  What's  needed,  he  believes, 
is  a  completely  new  computer  architec- 
ture, divorced  from  today's  von  Neu- 
mannesque  approaches.  The  underlying 


structure  of  the  computer,  he  says,  should 
reflect  the  type  of  data  being  examined. 
"Since  vision  consists  of  simultaneously 
interpreting  many  non-numeric  symbols, 
which  are  not  easily  quantifiable,  it  seems 
only  logical  that  the  'thinking'  of  com- 
puters should  reflect  this  task  as  closely 
as  possible."  In  Atwater  Kent's  sparkling 
new  labs,  faculty  and  student  research  is 
focusing  on  this  challenging  endeavor. 

Some  experts  see  the  day  when  robots 
will  be  driving  cars  far  more  safely  than 
their  creators  possibly  could.  As  back-seat 
drivers,  we  will  watch  our  steely-eyed 
chauffeurs  negotiate  oncoming  curves, 
signals,  and  traffic  with  unerring  preci- 
sion. But  in  the  eyes  of  Bob  Schalkoff,  at 
least,  this  scenario  is  a  long,  long  way 
from  reality. 


Professor  Robert  J.  Schalkoff  teaches  machines  to  see. 


FEBRUARY  1983        7 


Foreign  Exchange: 

Global  Engineers  and  Scientists  in  the  Making 


This  year  marks  the  beginning  of  the  second  decade  of 
WPrs  Student  Exchange  Program  with  foreign  tech- 
nical universities.  Since  1972.  some  150  British  and 
American  students  have  participated  in  academic  exchanges 
with  our  chief  partner — The  City  University  in  London's  his- 
toric district  near  St.  Paul's  Cathedral  and  the  Tower.  In  ad- 
dition, other  WPI  students  have  spent  semesters  at  Trent 
Polytechnic  in  Nottingham.  England;  the  Federal  Technical 
University  (where  Albert  Einstein  studied)  in  Zurich,  Switz- 
erland: and.  most  recently,  with  the  Royal  Institute  of  Tech- 
nology in  Stockholm,  Sweden.  A  new  program  in  Munich, 
Germany,  is  in  late  stages  of  development.  In  fact,  WPI  now 
offers  more  opportunities  to  study  in  more  locations  abroad 
than  any  other  U.S.  college  of  engineering  and  science. 

Unlike  many  other  exchange  programs,  WPI's  program 
places  third-  and  fourth-year  students  directly  in  British, 
Swiss,  or  Swedish  classes  at  the  foreign  university.  Our  stu- 
dents usually  live  in  dormitories  with  a  cosmopolitan  mixture 
of  native-speaking  and  other  foreign  students.  To  ensure  that 
their  academic  programs  proceed  in  proper  fashion,  most 
WPI  students  concentrate  on  research  for  IQPs  which  con- 
trast American  and  foreign  practices  for  applying  technology 
to  social  needs  and  concerns.  Many  students  also  work  on 
humanities  Sufficiencies  by  studying  European  art,  history  or 
literature,  or  (for  Sweden  and  Switzerland)  foreign  languages 
and  cultures.  And  despite  costs  and  time  demands,  almost 
everyone  makes  good  use  of  travel  opportunities  in  Europe. 

Participating  in  a  foreign  exchange  may  become  increas- 
ingly vital  to  the  education  of  WPI  students,  for  it  can  con- 
tribute in  real  terms  to  successful  careers  in  engineering,  sci- 
ence and  management.  Day  by  day,  news  stories  remind  us 
of  how  international  in  scope  science,  technology  and  busi- 
ness have  become. 

One  recent  alumnus  of  the  London  program  stopped  by 
WPI  not  long  ago,  on  his  way  to  a  new  assignment  in  Saudi 
Arabia.  The  exchange,  he  explained,  had  whetted  his  appe- 
tite for  working  overseas. 

Recently,  about  25  participants  in  WPI's  exchange  pro- 
gram gathered  for  an  informal  discussion.  There  were  U.S. 
students  preparing  for  or  recently  back  from  Europe,  British 
students  studying  stateside,  faculty  from  WPI  and  abroad. 
Tlic  purpose  of  this  forum  was  to  share  experiences,  percep- 
tions and  expectations  about  the  Exchange  and  student  life 
abroad,  and  to  compare  notes  on  British  and  U.S.  education. 
What  follows  are  excerpts  from  that  lively  session  where,  for 
one  thmg.  we  learned  that  academics  is  only  part  of  the  Ex- 
change experience. 

Professor  Lance  Schachlcrlc  (HU) 
Student  Exchange  Program  Coordinator 


Question:  What  do  students  gain  from  being  part  of  the 
Exchange? 

Christopher  Heyl,  '84,  ME  (Planning  to  study  at  City  Uni- 
versity. Spring  1983):  It's  a  great  opportunity  to  get  to  Eu- 
rope and  study  in  a  different  environment — and  a  great 
chance  to  see  Europe  for  a  reasonable  price. 

Keith  Agar,  '83,  EE  (Spent  Fall  1981  at  City):  City's 
strong  emphasis  is  in  engineering.  But  the  manner  in  which 
the  courses  are  taught  is  totally  different  from  the  American 
system  of  education.  The  Exchange  is  valuable  in  enabling 
you  to  see  how  other  people  learn.  And  the  project  opportu- 
nities are  diverse  in  Europe. 

Roger  Weber,  '84,  CE  (To  City,  Spring  1982):  City  is 
really  different.  You  take  seven  courses,  and  each  course 
meets  once  a  week.  The  course  work  varies  from  course  to 
course,  of  course  [laughter].  During  the  first  half  of  the  year 
we  take  it  easy,  but  then  we  start  working  harder  and 
harder.  Finally,  in  the  last  three  weeks,  we  really  go  nuts! 

Mark  Palmer,  '84,  CS  (Trent  Polytechnic,  now  at  WPI): 
Back  home  we  cram  for  exams  at  the  end  of  the  year.  It 
seems  that  in  the  U.S.  you  cram  every  couple  of  weeks  for 
exams.  At  Trent  you  tend  to  build  up  a  large  body  of  knowl- 
edge over  the  course  of  a  year. 

David  Holden,  '82,  CS  (Trent,  now  at  WPI):  I'd  like  to 
add  something.  I  had  the  opportunity  of  working  here  in  the 
co-op  system,  and  that  was  a  very  valuable  experience  for 
me. 

Gwyn  Crouch,  '85,  MA  (To  Trent.  Winter  1983):  On  a 
more  personal  level,  is  European  teaching  more,  or  less,  per- 
sonalized than  in  the  States? 

Kirsteen  ("Kiki")  Hodgson,  '83,  MG  (City,  now  at  WPI): 
At  City  University  at  least,  you  find  that  you're  not  allowed 
to  ask  questions  in  lecture,  because  the  classes  tend  to  be 
much  larger,  and  the  professor  doesn't  want  his  lecture 
schedule  delayed  by  questions.  Questions  and  discussions 
must  wait  until  free  time. 

John  Mansour,  '83,  PH  (To  City.  Fall  1981):  I  found  that 
some  of  the  courses  we  took  didn't  have  many  students  and 
weren't  as  impersonal.  Classes  were  more  formal  than  I  was 
used  to,  but  not  to  the  point  where  you  couldn't  ask  ques- 
tions in  lecture. 

Keith  Pugh,  '84,  CS  (City,  now  at  WPI):  At  Trent  it's 
fairly  informal.  The  classes  are  small,  and  you  can  approach 
the  professors. 

Keith  Agar:  City  doesn't  seem  to  offer  such  an  integrated 
system  of  teaching  as  U.S.  schools.  If  you're  a  civil  engineer, 
British  schools  have  a  program  designed  for  you.  In  electrical 
engineering,  you  have  a  different  program  of  studies.  Within 
each  department,  they  teach  you  all  the  necessary  outside

you  a  whole  new 
perspective  on 
education  and 
people." 


David  Holden 


Chris  Heyl,  Gwyn  Crouch,  Paul  Wilhams 


courses,  like  mathematics  and  chemistry,  but  it's  all  within 
one  department.  It  is  such  a  difference  in  teaching  tech- 
niques that  you  can't  really  count  on  the  courses  .you  take 
over  there  to  back  up  the  courses  that  you  have  here.  You 
get  a  new  and  different  perspective  on  how  to  learn  once 
you're  over  there. 

John  Mansour:  At  City  there  are  no  formal  textbooks  and 
everything's  taught  verbatim  from  the  notes  the  professor  has 
set  up  for  his  lecture.  Here,  I'd  be  lost  without  a  textbook 
for  all  of  my  courses,  but  over  there  the  professor  suggests 
books  for  the  course. 

Professor  Keith  Jacques  (University  of  Stirling,  Scotland): 
You've  pretty  much  covered  the  whole  spectrum  of  possibili- 
ties in  all  British  universities.  Each  method  is  committed  to 
solving  problems  in  one  form  or  another.  The  modern  sub- 
jects— like  management,  for  example — tend  to  be  less  formal 
than  the  traditional  subjects.  But  it  does  depend  on  the  insti- 
tution. 

Robert  Catino,  '83,  CS  (To  City,  Spring  1982):  From  what 
I've  seen  in  my  IQP  on  comparative  engineering  education 
[see  box,  p.  11],  the  British  student's  work  is  based  pretty 
much  upon  formal  examination,  whereas  in  America  it's 
more  of  a  continuous  assessment. 

Kiki  Hodgson:  That  depends  on  the  department.  In  my 
department — management — course  work  throughout  the  year 
counts  40  percent  of  the  final  grade.  So  the  exams  count  only 
60  percent. 

Bob  Catino:  In  England  there's  a  much  narrower  scope  of 
educational  institutions,  because  there  exists  a  different  sys- 
tem of  accreditation.  Here  we  have  the  American  Board  for 
Engineering  Technology  [ABET],  which  accredits  each  de- 
partment at  each  institution.  The  U.K.  has  what's  called  an 
external-examiner  system,  where  professors  from  different 
institutions  assess  each  other's  programs.  In  that  way  they  try 
to  ensure  that  the  education  offered  at  one  institution  is 
nearly  equal  to  that  of  every  other  institution. 

Professor  Jacques:  I  would  agree  with  that. 

Question:  Let's  talk  about  your  project  experiences. 

Chris  Heyl:  I'm  going  to  City  University  next  spring  to 
complete  my  WPI  Sufficiency  requirement  on  the  origins  of 
the  Royal  Society. 

Gwyn  Crouch:  I'm  going  to  Trent  next  spring  to  do  a  Suf- 
ficiency in  drama.  I'll  be  researching  realism  and  the  contem- 
porary end  of  the  drama  in  England  and  comparing  it  to  that 
of  America. 

Paul  Williams,  '84,  EE  (To  Trent,  Spring  1983):  I'll  be 
doing  an  IQP  on  the  economics  of  the  branch  lines  of  British 
railways,  not  the  glamorous  ones,  but  the  not-so-glamorous 
ones,  which  some  people  want  to  get  rid  of  and  some  people 


want  to  keep.  It's  very  controversial.  It's  part  of  what  a  lot 
of  people  at  WPI  are  studying  for  their  IQPs. 

Frank  Dunn,  '83,  EE  (To  City,  Fall  1982):  I  did  an  IQP 
on  the  electrication  of  the  British  rail  system.  The  Railroads 
Group,  as  we're  called,  studies  different  countries'  rail  sys- 
tems. Some  do  the  U.S.  railroads,  some  the  British,  some 
the  Swiss.  All  the  reports  are  compiled,  so  you  can  compare 
nations.  With  this  as  background,  exchange  students  know 
where  to  start,  and  you  can  get  to  the  important  sources  of 
information  quickly. 

Roger  Weber:  I  compared  English  and  American  building 
design  with  respect  to  energy  efficiency. 

John  Matthews,  '84,  EE  (To  City,  Spring  1983):  My  IQP 
will  be  a  comparative  study  of  the  Reagan  and  Thatcher  eco- 
nomic policies  and  how  they  affect  technological  innovation 
of  affected  countries. 

Kevin  Cavanaugh,  '83,  EE  (To  City,  Spring  1982):  I  did 
my  IQP  on  the  British  telecommunications  industry.  I  met 
many  of  the  people  in  City's  EE  Department  as  well  as  peo- 
ple in  industry  and  the  Departments  of  Industry  and  of 
Trade.  I  spent  an  hour  or  two  with  these  people,  took  tea 
with  them,  and  had  quite  an  enjoyable  time. 

Keith  Agar:  Right.  Just  being  a  foreign  student  helps  im- 
mensely in  getting  in  touch  with  people  who  are  more  than 
willing  to  talk  to  you. 

Question:  Kiki,  why  did  you  come  to  WPI  as  a  Manage- 
ment major? 

Kiki  Hodgson:  The  management  courses  I  take  at  WPI 
are  very  different  from  those  I  take  back  home.  But  mostly 
I'm  attracted  by  the  cultural  experience  of  the  Exchange. 
Everybody's  talking  about  academics,  but  I  think  the  cultural 
aspect  is  probably  the  major  thing  that  attracts  many  students. 

John  Mansour:  Keith  [Agar]  and  I  arrived  in  England  a 
few  weeks  before  classes  started.  We  spent  a  week  in  Lon- 
don. There  were  lots  of  students  there  from  all  over  the 
world.  Then  we  went  to  Scotland  for  a  week,  staying  in  hos- 
tels and  "bed-and-breakfasts." 

Matthew  Turner,  '83,  EE  (To  Trent,  Spring  1982):  I  lived 
with  an  elderly  couple  a  few  miles  from  Nottingham.  Living 
off-campus  tends  to  increase  the  cost  of  the  Exchange,  be- 
cause you  have  to  use  public  transportation  every  day,  but  if 
I  had  to  do  it  over  again,  I'd  do  it  the  same  way.  You  get  to 
see  the  British  way  of  life  a  lot  more  closely  when  you're  ac- 
tually Uving  within  it.  After  all,  that's  what  you're  going  over 
for,  to  see  what  England's  like. 

Dave  Holden:  I'm  enjoying  the  opportunity  of  living  in  a 
fraternity  at  WPI.  It's  a  very  interesting  experience.  I  had  no 
idea  what  fraternities  were  like  before  I  came  to  the  States. 

Eileen  Kelleher,  '84,  CE  (To  Stockholm,  Summer  1982):

Even  though  most  people  speak  English  in  Stockholm,  if  I 
had  stayed  with  a  family  I  would  have  learned  a  lot  more 
Swedish  and  a  lot  more  about  the  Swedish  culture. 

Question:  What  is  student  life  like  in  Europe?  Social  and 
campus  activities,  clubs,  things  like  that. 

Mark  Graves,  CS  (Trent,  now  at  WPI):  The  social  life  in 
Britain  is  a  lot  quieter  than  in  the  U.S..  from  what  I've  seen. 

Keith  Agar:  I  thought  it  was  just  the  reverse  at  City  Uni- 
versity. There  are  quite  a  variety  of  activities  that  always 
seemed  to  be  going  on.  Some  of  the  fraternity  parties  were 
more  classy  than  typical  parties  here. 

Professor  Sam  Woolford  (Mathematics,  on  faculty  ex- 
change last  year  at  Trent):  In  England  the  social  life  centers 
about  the  pub.  which  is  a  completely  different  type  of  social 
atmosphere  than  you'll  find  here.  I  think  it  works  out  to  be 
about  the  same  as  our  social  system,  except  maybe  it's  less 
formal  than  our  fraternity  or  organized  parties.  The  pub  is 
one  of  the  prime  places  where  you'll  meet  English  people. 
where  you'll  see  the  English  social  system  in  practice.  I  think 
some  of  the  attitudes  and  things  you'll  learn  there  are  very 
pleasant,  very  nice. 

Professor  Jacques:  I  presume  people  have  just  about 
enough  pocket  money  to  buy  other  things  besides  beer,  espe- 
cially if  you're  in  a  place  like  London.  There's  an  enormous 
amount  to  do  in  London,  but  spend  a  few  weekends  in  other 
parts  of  the  country,  because  London  is  not  fully  representa- 
tive of  Great  Britain  at  all. 

Kiki  Hodgson:  As  any  great  city  does,  London  offers  a 
great  many  facilities — theaters,  cinemas,  nightclubs  and  mu- 
seums. And  I  think  sports  are  taken  much  more  seriously 
here  than  at  City. 

Bob  Catino:  I'd  like  to  add  a  word  about  travel.  Traveling 
in  England — and  elsewhere  in  Europe — is  ridiculously  easy — 
when  the  trains  are  running.  It's  great  to  simply  get  on  a 
train  and  say,  "That  looks  like  a  nice  place,"  and  go  there. 
If  you  have  a  British  Rail  pass,  you  can  just  get  on  and  get 
off  anywhere  and  walk  around  the  town  and  find  a  bcd-and- 
breakfast  or  a  hostel. 

Kiki  Hodgson:  True,  and  a  Eurail  Pass  or  Interail  Pass  is 
a  very  good  and  inexpensive  idea  for  European  travel. 

Mark  Graves:  Americans  seem  a  lot  more  open  and  a  lot 
more  friendly  than  the  British.  But  when  you  go  to  England, 
don't  feel  we're  unfriendly,  we're  just  rather  introverted. 
English  people  seem  really  quiet  to  start  with,  but  they're 
pretty  friendly  underneath. 

Professor  Jacques:  I  think  that  applies  even  more  when 
you  get  out  into  the  provinces. 

Professor  Woolford:  It's  probably  the  same  anywhere  in 
the  world  that  you  go.  You  will  be  received  in  England  in 


probably  the  same  way  that  British  students  feel  that  they 
have  been  received  here. 

John  Mansour:  That's  really  true.  The  British  are  very 
helpful  and  very  friendly. 

Professor  Willy  Eggimann  (EE.  Coordinator  of  the  Swiss 
Exchange):  One  of  the  advantages  of  the  Swiss  and  Swedish 
exchanges  and  of  the  German  Exchange,  which  we  plan  to 
organize  soon,  is  that  you  have  to  learn  a  foreign  language. 
We  handled  it  fairly  well  with  the  Swiss  Exchange,  and  the 
German  exchange  will  be  even  easier  because  we  have  a  very 
good  German  language  program  at  WPI.  But  there's  no  op- 
portunity to  learn  Swedish  m  Worcester.  So  I  offer  a  Swed- 
ish course  on  cassette  tapes.  This  should  be  sufficient  to  fa- 
miliarize you  with  the  language. 

Question:  What  words  of  advice  can  any  of  you  offer  stu- 
dents preparing  for  or  interested  in  the  Exchange? 

Keith  Agar:  Living  in  the  City  University  subsidized  hous- 
ing helps  tremendously,  because  London  is  so  expensive.  I'm 
not  sure  of  the  exact  figures,  but  it  didn't  break  me.  Summer 
jobs  help  a  lot.  As  far  as  suggestions  for  students  going  over, 
I  think  it's  critical  that  you  get  knowledge  about  the  Ex- 
change Program  at  least  by  the  end  of  your  freshman  year. 
You  should  plan  your  schedule  very  carefully  for  the  next 
three  years,  because  you  want  to  make  sure  that  you're  not 
missing  anything  essential  for  graduation. 

Bob  Catino:  Just  be  open-minded.  Go  abroad  to  get  the 
project  work  done,  but  to  have  a  good  time  as  well.  Don't 
forget  the  Continent  is  only  26  miles  away. 

Professor  Bruce  McQuarrie  (Mathematics.  Coordinator  of 
the  Trent  Exchange):  Be  flexible  when  you  go.  If  there's  one 
thing  you  can  count  on,  it's  that  things  will  be  different. 
They  won't  be  better,  they  won't  be  worse.  They  will  simply 
be  different. 

Keith  Agar:  That's  good  advice.  It  might  be  naive,  but 
before  I  went  to  England,  I  thought  everyone  did  things  the 
way  Americans  do.  But  when  I  got  to  London,  1  found  that 
not  to  be  true.  That  eye-opening  experience  is  valuable. 

Eileen  Kelleher:  I  have  a  question  concerning  being  a  fe- 
male and  traveling,  finding  bed-and-breakfasts,  and  so  on. 

Kiki  Hodgson:  A  young  American  woman,  who  was  my 
next-door  neighbor  at  City  University  for  a  semester,  trav- 
eled alone  for  a  month  in  the  U.K.  I  don't  think  I'd  do  that 
myself.  But  traveling  with  one  or  two  people  is  a  good  alter- 
native. 

John  Mansour:  One  thing  I  learned  was  that,  unlike  trav- 
eling, for  example,  to  Canada,  when  you  move  between  Eu- 
ropean countries,  within  a  few  miles  you're  in  another  world 
almost,  a  different  culture,  needing  a  different  language.  To 
people  here,  the  United  States  is  the  world.  I  think  once

you're  over  there  you're  going  to  see  that  there  is  a  whole 
other  part  of  the  world.  I  think  that's  probably  the  best  part 
of  the  program.  You  become  very  aware  that  there  are  other 
countries,  other  views,  other  peoples. 

Keith  Agar:  Just  being  in  different  countries  gives  you  a
