with  new  and  unfamiliar  situations.  When  you're  traveling  in 
France,  for  example,  you  may  not  know  much  French,  but 
you  manage  to  find  someplace  to  eat,  to  deal  with  unfamiliar" 
currency,  to  get  a  hotel  room  for  the  night. 

Matt  Turner:  Probably  the  most  rewarding  things  for  me 


were  non-academic  related.  I  definitely  encourage  people 
going  on  the  Exchange  to  get  involved  in  the  English  lifestyle 
and  go  to  sporting  events  and  the  theater  and  don't  just 
worry  about  academics  because  there's  a  lot  more  to  it  than 
that. 

John  Mansour:  There  were  good  and  bad  experiences,  but 
all  in  all,  it  was  well  worth  it.  One  of  the  first  things  you  do 
is  find  a  map  of  London  and  mark  off  where  the  McDonald's 
restaurants  are. 

Keith  Agar:  Just  one  last  piece  of  advice:  Don't  let  your 
classwork  interfere  with  your  education. 


At  Home  and  Abroad 


In  the  United  Kingdom,  an  engineer  can  be  anyone  from  a 
bus  driver  to  an  appliance  repairperson  to  a  college-edu- 
cated professional.  According  to  WPI  seniors  Malcolm  Berke 
(MGC)  of  Livingston,  NJ,  and  Robert  Catino  (CS)  of  Rocky 
Hill,  CT,  this  situation  is  a  fascinating  and  somewhat  trou- 
bling commentary  on  engineering  education  in  Britain  and 
the  status  of  her  professional  engineers.  But,  they  say,  Brit- 
ain's centuries-old  educational  methods  seem  to  enjoy  sev- 
eral advantages  over  the  U.S.  system. 

Roommates  at  WPL  Berke  and  Catino  spent  part  of  their 
junior  year  based  at  London's  City  University,  systematically 
comparing  undergraduate  engineering  education  in  the  U.K. 
and  the  U.S.  Now  in  its  final  stages,  their  IQP  gets  to  the 
heart  of  some  controversial  issues  and  spells  out  how  faculty, 
students  and  administrators  in  both  countries  rate  their  own 
institutions. 

In  January  1980,  a  British  report  called  Engineering  Our 
Future,  also  known  as  the  Finniston  Report,  concluded  that 
the  quality  of  the  British  engineering  system  is  a  key  factor 
in  that  country's  current  industrial  woes.  According  to  the 
report,  British  professional  engineers  are  used  ineffectively, 
their  status,  compared  to  that  of  their  U.S.  counterparts,  is 
depressed,  and,  because  British  engineers  are  unprotected  by 
licensing  or  registration  practices,  there  are  no  universal 
British  employment  policies  or  salary,  training,  or  academic 
requirements. 

The  picture  painted  by  the  Finniston  Report  of  British  en- 
gineering is  far  from  rosy.  But  is  it  a  valid  portrayal?  And 
how  does  the  U.S.  stack  up? 

These  are  the  questions  Berke  and  Catino  set  out  to  an- 
swer. Through  questionnaires  and  interviews  with  students, 
faculty  and  administrators  of  selected  British  and  U.S.  tech- 
nological colleges,  they  sought  to  separate  myth  and  fact. 


Bob  Cantino  and  Malcome  Berke  compared  U.S.  and  British 
engineering  education. 

"By  and  large,"  reports  Catino,  "our  study  reflects  the 
findings  of  the  Finniston  Report."  To  illustrate  the  dilemma 
facing  the  engineering  profession  across  the  U.K.,  he  recalls 
the  reply  of  a  British  laundromat  owner,  when  Catino  in- 
formed the  man  of  a  jammed  clothes  washer.  "The  engi- 
neer," said  the  man,  "will  be  up  presently  to  fix  it." 

Fortunately,  says  Berke,  the  British  government  has  com- 
missioned a  body  committed  to  reversing  this  image.  The 
mission  of  the  Engineering  Council  is  to  develop  guidelines 
for  ensuring  consistent  programs  in  engineering  education, 
clarifying  in  the  public's  eye  the  level  of  professional  engi- 
neers' expertise,  and  encouraging  the  testimony  of  engineers 
in  litigation  calling  for  the  opinions  of  experts. 

But,  he  adds,  while  the  British  system  may  have  its  share 
of  problems,  its  engineering  cooperative  program  appears  to 
be  much  stronger  in  practice  than  that  of  the  U.S.,  due  in 
large  part  to  nearly  full  tuition  subsidies  by  the  British  gov- 
ernment. Plus,  traditional  U.S.  higher  education  emphasizes 
periodic  examinations,  while  the  U.K.  employs  one-  or  two- 
year  comprehensive  exams.  "At  WPL"  says  Berke,  "the 
Plan's  two  qualifying  projects  and  the  competency  exam 
combine  the  best  of  both  worlds." 


FEBRUARY  1983 


11 


PROJECTS 


Foreign  Countries 
And  the  EMP  Threat 

Interactive  Qualitying  Project  (IQP) 
Frederick  Gummow,  '84,  EE 
Patricia  Martone,  '84,  EE 
Patricia  Porcaro,  '84,  CE 
Dean  William  Grogan,  '46,  and 
Prof.  Joseph  Soetens,  Management, 
Advisors 

If  a  thermonuclear  device  is  exploded  at 
a  high  altitude,  a  powerful  electromag- 
netic pulse  (EMP).  or  "burst,"'  is  propa- 
gated. People  would  not  die  from  the  ex- 
plosion, nor  buildings  be  destroyed.  But, 
according  to  an  IQP  compiled  by  WPI 
juniors  Frederick  Gummow  of  West 
Bridgewater,  MA,  Patricia  Martone  of 
Mechanicville,  NY,  and  Patricia  Porcaro 
of  Boylston,  MA,  the  electronic  destruc- 
tion caused  by  an  EMP  could  instantly 
produce  chaos  throughout  any  nation. 

"Virtually  all  exposed  communications 
and  computer  systems  could  be  wiped  out 
by  an  EMP,"  Gummow  reports.  "With- 
out protection  there  would  be  few  func- 
tioning telephones,  radios,  power  systems 
or  computers." 

Dean  William  Grogan,  project  advisor, 
adds,  "Unlike  the  old  vacuum  tube  cir- 
cuits, the  high-density  electronic  'chips' 
on  which  our  electronic  American  society 
now  depends  could  be  seriously  af- 
fected." 

Although  lagging  behind  the  U.S.  in 
the  application  of  the  vulnerable  micro- 
chip components,  foreign  countries  seem 
very  concerned  about  EMPs.  In  this  proj- 
ect, sponsored  by  the  National  Research 
Council  at  WPI's  Washington.  DC,  Proj- 
ect Center,  students  examined  unclassi- 
fied literature  on  EMPs  developed  by  nine 
foreign  countries.  Not  only  did  they  read 
and  abstract  75  documents,  they  also  per- 
sonally contacted  32  international  experts 
in  the  field.  They  studied  and  analyzed 
EMP  information  published  in  Canada, 
Europe,  and  the  U.S.S.R. 

The  group  concluded  that  public  aware- 


ness of  EMP  risks  is  highest  in  Switzer- 
land, Norway  and  Sweden  and  that  these 
countries  and  West  Germany  have  taken 
significant  steps  to  protect  their  electron- 
ics and  to  educate  personnel  for  emer- 
gency situations.  According  to  the  IQP 
final  report,  unclassified  literature  indi- 
cates that  several  countries  are  worried 
that  the  U.S.  is  not  yet  capable  of  with- 
standing an  EMP  situation.  From  Switz- 
erland comes  the  article.  "The  Bomb  That 
Could  Leave  America  Power  Less;"  from 
Britain,  "[U.S.]  Communications  Clob- 
bered;" and  from  Norway  the  warning, 
"If  the  Soviet  Union  exploded  a  nuclear 
bomb  above  the  atmosphere  in  North 
America  and  West  Europe,  all  the  NATO 
countries  would  be  helpless." 

The  importance  of  the  WPI  research  is 
confirmed  by  a  recent  statement  by  Ed- 
ward Teller  of  the  Hoover  Institution 
(IEEE  Spectrum,  October  1982,  pg.  65): 
"Without  widespread  dissemination  of 
[declassified]  information  to  industry,  .  .  . 
the  civilian  economy  faces  the  prospect  of 
grinding  to  a  halt  in  a  nuclear  encounter." 


Safer  Knots  for 
Surgical  Sutures 

Major  Qualifying  Prt)jcct  (MQP) 
Donald  R.  Soubie,  '82,  ME 
Michael  R.  Williams,  '83.  ME 
Prof.  Allen  H.  Hoffman,  ME,  Advi.sor 

A  postoperative  patient  may  occasionally 
complain,  "I  think  I've  slipped  a  stitch." 
But  because  of  the  extensive  testing  given 
surgical  knots  by  Donald  Soubie,  of  Fitch- 
burg,  MA.  and  Michael  Williams,  of  East 
Templeton,  MA,  in  their  recently  com- 
pleted MQP,  such  patient  complaints  may 
become  even  more  of  a  rarity  in  the  fu- 
ture. 

The  project,  which  was  carried  out  with 
the  cooperation  of  Dr.  John  Herrmann  of 
the  Department  of  Surgery  at  Worcester's 
University  of  Massachusetts  Medical 
Center,  investigated  the  mechanics  of  knot 


security  in  surgical  sutures.  Knot  slippage 
was  examined  using  large-diameter  rope 
as  a  model.  Visual  observations  and  force 
measurements  were  also  made,  according 
to  methods  formulated  by  the  American 
Society  for  Testing  and  Materials. 

Because  the  knot  used  by  the  surgeon 
plays  such  an  important  role  in  keeping 
the  surgical  suture  from  slipping,  the  three 
most  common  knots  (square,  surgeon's 
and  granny)  were  tested  thoroughly.  After 
being  tied  and  placed  in  a  tensile  test  ma- 
chine, the  knots  were  pulled  until  they 
slipped  or  tightened.  Through  the  use  of 
a  videotape  recorder,  Soubie  and  Wil- 
liams discovered  that,  as  the  knots  were 
being  pulled,  they  turned,  became  smaller 
and  changed  from  their  original  shape.  In 
the  final  analysis,  the  square  knot  was 
found  to  be  most  secure,  with  the  sur- 
geon's knot  second.  The  granny  knot  was 
least  secure. 

They  also  found  that  the  square  knot 
attempted  to  align  itself  to  become  a  two- 
force  member.  The  surgeon's  knot  com- 
pletely changed  its  shape  and  became 
smaller,  but  not  as  small  as  the  square 
knot.  On  the  other  hand,  the  granny  knot 
changed  shape  only  slightly,  by  twisting 
and  rotating,  and  did  not  become  smaller. 
More  than  the  square  knot  and  the  sur- 
geon's knot,  the  granny  knot  tended  to 
retain  its  original  shape  and  size  during 
the  pulling. 

In  a  rigorous  knot  slippage  test,  the 
granny  knot  failed  100  percent  of  the  time. 
The  surgeon's  knot  failed  80  percent  of 
the  time,  with  the  square  knot  having  a  0 
percent  failure  rate. 

It  was  also  discovered  that  a  knot  formed 
from  a  multifilament  suture  material  is 
more  secure  than  the  same  knot  formed 
in  a  monofilament  material — the  former 
tends  to  have  a  higher  coefficient  of  fric- 
tion and  a  lower  flexural  rigidity. 

The  findings  of  Soubie  and  Williams 
indicate  that  square  knots  in  multifila- 
ment materials  could  be  just  what  the  doc- 
tor orders  for  your  next  operation.  You 
might  mention  this  to  your  surgeon  just 
before  you  go  under. 


12        WPI   JOURNAL 


Somewhere    between    a    gleaming 
Tomorrowland  and  a  red-hot  cinder 
lies  the  likely  future  of  the  globe.  Join 
us  to  ponder  it — spot  a  few  problems, 
eye  a  few  technologies,  and  eavesdrop 
on  a  convention  full  of  futurists. 


^Culture  & 
Clairvoyance 

Is  it  possible  to  know  the  future? 
Or  is  the  ''future"  a  window  on  the  past  and  present? 

By  Mark  Mosko 


I  hat  will  happen  tomorrow?  Next  year?  Next  cen- 

Wtury?  Will  there  be  peace?  Is  there  an  afterlife?  To 
most  people,  such  are  the  interesting  questions  about 
the  future,  questions  concerned  with  prediction. 

Anthropologists,  however,  tend  to  shy  away  from  speculating 
about  the  future,  because  the  study  of  many  cultures  reveals 
subtle  complexities.  What  is  meant  by  "future"?  The  future  ac- 
cording to  whom?  You  or  me?  This  culture  or  another  culture? 

Anthropologically  speaking,  the  interesting  aspect  of  any  pre- 
diction is  not  what  it  says  about  the  future— which  may  or  may 
not  turn  out  to  be  accurate — but  rather  what  it  reveals  of  general 
assumptions  about  time  and  reality  current  in  the  culture  of  the 
present.  Culturally  shared  notions  about  time  and  reality  vary 
considerably  within  and  between  cultures,  and  the  kinds  of  fu- 
tures that  each  foretells  will  vary  accordingly. 

For  instance,  most  of  us  have  probably  observed  that  the  spe- 
cific visions  a  person  has  of  heaven  or  paradise  reflect  aspects 
of  his  life  which  he  regards  as  vital  in  the  here  and  now.  The 
tired  may  think  paradise  will  be  eternal  rest,  while  the  Angeleno 
will  think  it  is  Southern  California.  (So  may  the  Minnesotan.) 
Within  a  culture,  similarly,  specific  ideas  of  the  future  express 

Mark  Mosko  is  an  anthropologist  at  Hartwick  College. 


that  culture's  most  basic  beliefs  and  values.  From  this  perspec- 
tive, "the  future"  can  serve  as  a  code  to  the  present. 

In  our  Western  culture,  we  tend  to  think  of  the  future  as  an 
extension  of  the  directional,  linear,  non-repetitive  unfolding  of 
time.  There  was  the  past,  we  live  now  in  the  present,  and  when 
the  future  does  come  it  will  consist  largely  of  novel  occurrences — 
new  inventions,  new  societies,  new  institutions.  Time  in  general 
moves  on  for  us,  never  repeating  itself,  so  that  we  see  the  future 
as  marked  by  never-ending  change.  Hence  the  difficulties  we 
perceive  in  offering  reliable  predictions  about  the  future.  In  this 
view  of  time,  no  one  can  ever  be  certain  which  changes  will 
indeed  eventuate. 

But  in  other  areas  of  our  culture  and  in  some  non-Western 
cultures,  time  is  very  differently  conceived,  such  that  the  future 
is  much  less  problematic.  In  Hindu  culture,  to  take  a  well-known 
example,  the  entire  universe  undergoes  never-ending  cycles  of 
creation  and  destruction.  Each  cycle  of  Hindu  cosmic  time  lasts 
billions  of  years.  Time  is  here  cyclical  rather  than  linear,  repet- 
itive rather  than  non-repetitive.  At  a  smaller  scale,  in  Hindu 
culture,  living  creatures  (including  humans)  are  bound  to  cycles 
of  life/death/rebirth  reincarnation.  The  lifetime  is  a  circle,  and 
the  next  lifetime  will  partially  repeat  the  former  one.  So  while 
there  is  change  within  any  one  cycle — cosmic  or  human — each 


FEBRUARY  1983 


Ihc  Hcttmann  Archive.  Inc. 


II    ALUMNI  MAGAZlNb  CONSORTIUM 


cycle  repeats  elements  of  other  cycles.  Essentially,  the  Hindu 
future  holds  a  unending  repetition  of  the  past. 

This  cyclical  perspective  is  quite  common  among  cultures  of 
the  world.  Indeed,  even  in  Western  cultures  we  tend  to  break 
time  up  into  days,  weeks,  months,  seasons,  years,  generations, 
etcetera,  which  reveals  a  pervasive  use  of  repetitive  cyclical  time. 
Consider  especially  our  notion  of  "life  cycles."  Certainly  the 
beginning  of  a  life  is  distinct  from  its  end.  yet  we  along  with 
peoples  of  many  other  cultures  tend  to  equate  birth  and  death. 
Through  the  progression  of  seasons,  the  natural  world  emerges 
rejuvenated.  In  a  parallel  way,  the  human  spirit  also  does  not 
die.  It  too,  as  our  culture  sees  it,  is  reborn  upon  bodily  death. 
I  suggest  that  by  thinking  in  terms  of  these  cycles,  we  gain  a 
measure  of  confidence,  order,  and  intelligibility,  because  the 
cycles  let  us  foresee  a  fate  consistent  with  the  perceived  structure 
of  the  whole  universe. 

Still  other  patterns  for  describing  time  are  found  among  the 
world's  cultures.  Quite  com.monly  time  is  broken  up  into  con- 
trasting and  alternating  phases.  There  is  night,  for  example,  then 
there  is  day,  then  night,  then  day,  and  so  on.  Summer/winter, 
wet  season/dry  season,  peacetime/wartime,  and  sacred  time/pro- 
fane time  are  additional  anthropological  instances  of  making 
sense  of  time  through  alternations. 

As  with  cyclical  time  but  distinct  from  linear  time,  alternating 
time  is  repetitive,  so  that  future  events  can  be  reliably  predicted 
by  past  events.  If  it  was  night,  and  now  is  day,  then  night  will 
certainly  follow. 

Moreover,  note  that  in  certain  situations  we  Americans  use 
this  alternating  temporal  order  side  by  side  with  cyclical  and 
linear  views  of  time.  Usually,  as  we  switch  from  one  mode  to 
the  other,  we  do  not  notice  even  the  mildest  discomfort.  Con- 
sistent with  our  various  views  about  "work,"  for  instance,  we 
might  first  remark  upon  the  monotony  of  our  "day-in,  day-out" 
routine  (alternating  time);  then  we  might  anxiously  anticipate 
starting  all  over  on  some  new  job  (cyclical  time);  but  nonetheless 
grieve  for  opportunities  that  are  slipping  past,  never  to  come 
again  (linear  time) — all  with  the  greatest  of  ease. 

Ordinarily,  it  is  only  when  we  argue  about  the  course  of  future 
events  that  the  contradictory  implications  of  these  various  views 
of  time  become  evident.  Compare  the  familiar  image  of  the 
political  conservative  who  fights  for  return  to  conditions  of  the 
past,  on  the  one  hand,  with  the  liberal  who  pushes  relentlessly 
for  entirely  novel  formulations  of  public  policy.  Both  have  the 
future  in  view,  and  both  accept  the  inevitability  of  change,  but 
the  conservative  struggles  to  reclaim  a  prior  moment  in  time  and 
work  from  there,  while  the  liberal  abandons  the  past.  The  clash 
of  their  divergent  assumptions  about  the  nature  of  time  and 
consequently  of  the  future  makes  their  political  contest  una- 
voidable. 

In  spite  of  their  various  implications  for  perceiving  the  future, 
linear,  cyclical,  and  alternating  temporal  modes  share  one  ele- 
ment in  common.  They  all  assume  change — whether  it  be  re- 
petitive or  non-repetitive — to  be  an  essential  aspect  of  reality. 
But  what  if  there  were  a  culture  that  conceived  time  in  a  radically 
different  way,  one  that  denied  the  very  possibility  of  change? 
Specfically,  what  kind  of  future  would  be  conceivable  when  things 
do  not  and  cannot  change?  Well,  the  Dreamtime  beliefs  of  Aus- 
tralian Aborigines  provide  just  such  astonishing  possibilities. 

The  Dreamtime  in  traditional  Aboriginal  culture  unified  three 
major  kinds  of  experience:  myths  and  stories  of  creation,  annual 
religious  rituals  and  activities,  and  people's  dreams.  Basically, 
through  their  dreams  and  rituals  Aboriginal  peoples  play  out  the 
events  and  interactions  of  their  own  ancestors,  ancestors  of  whom 


they  are  the  living  reincarnations.  However,  the  Aborigines  take 
this  association  to  a  remarkable  extreme.  From  a  Westerner's 
point  of  view,  it  would  appear  that  they  constantly  rewrite  the 
past  to  match  the  present.  They  argue,  though,  that  they  have 
the  same  names,  same  personalities,  same  possessions,  and  same 
relationships  as  their  ancestors.  Indeed,  in  some  essential  way, 
living  Aborigines  are  their  own  ancestors.  Therefore,  whatever 
people  do  and  observe  "now"  in  their  Dreamtime  activities  is 
what  they  have  always  been  doing  and  always  will  be  doing.  In 
their  cultural  beliefs  about  the  Dreamtime,  past,  present,  and 
future  are  completely  collapsed  into  what  has  been  called 
"everywhen." 

It  might  come  as  a  surprise  for  some  to  discover  these  same 
four  modes — linear  time,  cyclical,  alternating,  and  non-changing 
time — expressed  in  the  theories  of  modern  Western  science.  With 
the  notion  of  entropy,  according  to  the  Second  Law  of  Ther- 
modynamics, time  is  linear  and  non-repetitive  for  all  matter  and 
energy  in  the  Universe.  Similarly,  biological  evolution  proceeds 
through  time  linearly,  without  repetition  or  reversal.  The  mo- 
tions of  the  planets  as  described  by  Kepler  and  Newton  exemplify 
repetitive,  cyclical  time.  And  in  astrophysics,  some  versions  of 
the  Big  Bang  theory  involve  alternating  phases  of  expansion  and 
contraction  of  the  universe.  Finally,  the  concept  of  equilibrium 
in  chemistry,  as  well  as  Steady  State  theories  that  vie  with  the 
Big  Bang  theory,  each  presume  (at  one  level)  a  notion  of  con- 
stant, non-changing  time  and  homeostasis — very  similar  to  Ab- 
original Dreamtime. 

Need  I  mention  that  each  of  these  kinds  of  scientific  theory 
projects  a  different  kind  of  future,  just  as  any  cultural  system 
does?  And  here  we  may  detect  another  explanation  for  why 
accurate  predictions  remain  so  difficult. 

In  addition,  quite  apart  from  concepts  of  time,  predictions  of 
the  future  reflect  the  facts  of  the  present  as  we  see  it.  To  be 
deemed  relevant,  or  even  to  be  understood,  any  description  of 
the  future  must  to  some  extent  be  grounded  in  the  present.  The 
more  the  predicted  future  derives  from  the  familiar  today,  the 
more  it  will  be  believed. 

We  can  see  this  clearly,  I  think,  in  the  genre  of  science  fiction, 
which  usually  explicitly  deals  with  the  future.  Why,  otherwise, 
should  so  many  societies  depicted  in  science  fiction  take  on  the 
form  of  past  or  present  societies,  or  combinations  of  both?  Why 
is  any  truly  novel  form  so  novel  in  science  fiction?  The  remaining 
traces  of  feudal  aristocracy  have  been  dwindling  throughout  this 
century,  but  intergalactic  "empires"  of  the  year  20,000  A.D.  or 
whenever  are  often  constituted  of  feuding  dukes,  barons,  prin- 
cesses and  kings.  Why?  What  is  the  hidden  sense  of  projecting 
neolithic  tribal  and  chiefly  forms  of  society  into  the  21st  cen- 
tury?— albeit  adorned  with  ray  guns,  thought  control,  and  travel 
at  the  speed  of  light?  Even  the  images  of  the  totalitarian  state 
commonly  transported  into  the  future  undeniably  reflect  the  facts 
of  our  present  experiences  as  much  as  anything  else.  As  many 
devotees  insist,  what  is  best  in  science  fiction  is  not  fictional. 

Indeed,  the  future  changes  as  a  direct  function  of  changes  here 
in  the  present.  It  is  re-conceived  constantly,  as  an  ongoing 
amendment  to  traditions  stretching  deep  into  the  past.  As  things 
change  now,  our  view  of  things  in  the  future  tends  to  change 
accordingly.  Specific  predictions  about  the  future,  therefore,  may 
or  may  not  "work";  they  may  or  may  not  have  "real"  foretelling 
value.  But  each  prediction  has  value  in  telling  us  much  about 
our  ideas  of  ourselves  and  our  place  in  the  universe,  much  about 
the  values  and  culture  of  our  present.  By  placing  the  future  in 
its  wider  cultural  context,  perhaps  we  can  better  decipher  the 
things  which  vitally  concern  us  now. 


FEBRUARY  1983     III 


When  the  World  Future  Society 

assembled  to  find  today's  solutions  to 

tomorrow's  problems,  we  climbed 

aboard  Spaceship  Earth  .  .  . 

Future  Bound 


By  Gina  Maranto 


I  hen  I  arrived  at  the  Sheraton  Washington  Hotel  for 

Wthe  opening  ceremonies  at  the  Fourth  General  As- 
sembly of  the  World  Future  Society,  the  central  air 
was  in  overdrive.  It  was  a  hot.  humid  Sunday  after- 
noon, typical  of  D.C.  weather  in  July.  In  a  cordoned-off  area  in 
front  of  the  registration  desk,  waited  several  dozen  luggage- 
strapped  people.  "Futurists,"  I  noted,  were  capable  of  being 
late. 

Several  thousand  on-time  futurists,  meanwhile,  were  weaving 
through  labyrinthine  chrome  and  mirror  corridors,  dressed  in  a 
bewildering  array  of  styles.  There  were  people  wearing  polyester 
three-piece  suits,  people  wearmg  bleached  Levis.  Summer  wool 
pinstripes,  Indian-print  cotton  dresses,  digital  watches,  long  hair 
and  beards,  Birkenstock  sandals,  high-heeled  open-toed  shoes, 
khaki  shorts,  close-cropped  hair  and  clean-shaven  faces.  Was 
this  Berkeley,  the  boardroom,  or  the  boardwalk? 

On  my  way  upstairs  to  the  press  room.  I  crossed  paths  with  a 
pigtailed  little  girl  wearing  espadrilles.  She  was  yelling  up  to  a 
compadre  leaning  over  the  mezzanine  rail  above.  "You  have  to 
get  your  briefing  packet  from  the  press  room,"  the  girl  shouted, 
very  harried.  'Then  you'll  know  everything  you  need  to  know." 
Her  compadre  contorted  himself  against  the  rail.  "But  where," 
he  plaintively  asked,  "is  the  press  room?" 


In  his  own  way,  that  perplexed  eight-year-old  had  asked  one 
of  the  main  questions  that  the  Assembly  was  supposed  to  answer: 
Given  that  there  is  information  in  the  world,  how  does  one  get 
access  to  it?  More  important,  with  the  proliferation  of  so-called 
communications  technologies — cable  television,  video,  com- 
puters, fiber  optics,  direct  transmission  satellites,  and  a  horde  of 
permutations  and  combinations  thereof — who  will  have  access 
to  what  information?  And  what  will  they  do  with  both  access 
and  information? 

I  too,  of  course,  had  to  find  the  press  room.  And  then  I  had 
to  wade  through  a  flood  of  information,  to  learn  that  the  theme 
would  be  communication,  with  6()()-plus  speakers  and  3,2()()-odd 
participants.  Over  five  days,  there  were  to  be  several  dozen 
symposia  and  seminars,  nightly  "Paper  Fairs"  (where  authors 
would  discuss  and  defend  their  notions),  and  290  90-minute  ses- 
sions in  which  artists,  computer  specialists,  corporate  long-range 
planners,  economists,  educators,  full-time  futurist  networkers, 
military  advisors,  professors — and  others — would  attempt  to  scope 
out  the  role  that  communications  would  play  in  times  to  come. 
In  the  process,  they  would  attempt  to  forecast  the  fate  not  only 
of  societies,  but  also  of  humanity  and  life  itself. 

I  made  my  way  to  the  opening  ceremonies  through  a  maze  ol 
branching  corridors,  atria,  and  areas  with  too  many  exits  to  the 


IV    ALUMNI  MAGAZINE  CONSORTIUM 


Sheraton  Ballroom.  It  was  gargantuan,  badly  lit,  and  two-thirds 
full.  About  halfway  up  the  aisle,  a  row  of  yellow-shirted  children, 
including  the  espadrilled  girl  and  compadre,  fidgeted  and  chat- 
tered. They  were  reporters  for  the  Children's  Express  newspaper, 
and  two  teenage  staff  members  were  having  a  hard  time  mar- 
shalling the  restless  ones.  A  petite  blonde  in  braids  flipped  the 
conference  volume  open  and  reiad  aloud,  "  'The  future  of  the 
arts  is  one  that  will  be  modified  and  undoubtedly  impaired  by 
the  technological  society  in  which  we  live."  "  Slapping  the  volume 
shut,  she  repeated  with  worldly  exhaustion,  "  'Undoubtedly  im- 
paired,' oh,  I  can  tell  Fm  not  going  to  like  this."  I  wasn't  at  all 
sure  whether  she  meant  the  next  50  years  or  the  next  few  days. 

WFS  president  Edward  Cornish  took  the  podium.  Cornish,  a 
powdery-looking  man,  has  written  that  the  Society's  purpose  is 
to  further  the  "creation  of  a  world  future  network  that  can  pro- 
vide a  basis  for  the  global  wisdom  we  desperately  need  to  manage 
our  planet  during  this  period  of  convulsive  change."  In  his  open- 
ing speech,  Cornish  reiterated  that  message,  and  said  it  was  the 
task  of  futurists  (the  society  boasts  close  to  40,000  members  in 
80  countries)  to  find  solutions  to  the  world's  problems,  and  to 
use  new  technologies  to  help  people  understand  their  options. 
He  admonished  the  audience,  "Your  task  is  urgent  and  important 
because  the  real  changes  that  will  lead  us  to  a  sustainable  plan- 
etary future  won't  happen  openly,  but  in  the  hearts  and  minds 
of  people.  You  should  take  your  task  seriously,  but  enter  into 
it  with  a  festive  spirit." 

Several  items  in  that  brief  speech  were  to  re-echo  throughout 
the  conference.  First,  Cornish  used  the  global  "we,"  always 
speaking  in  terms  of  "our  planet,"  "our  options,"  and  "what  we 
must  do  to  solve  our  problems."  This  usage  reflected  the  view 
(not  a  new  one)  that  we're  all  in  this  thing  together  and  that  the 
life  of,  say,  a  lesser  bureaucrat  in  India  has  a  bearing  on  the  life 
of  an  auto  mechanic  in  Montana,  and  vice  versa. 

Then,  the  term  "sustainable  future."  "Future,"  as  the  word 
was  used  at  this  conference,  did  not  always  mean  tomorrow  and 
the  day  after,  but  referred  to  a  pervasive  system  of  organization. 
(Capitalism,  or  nation  states,  or  Roman  Catholicism,  to  name  a 
few,  are  such  systems  of  organization.)  A  "sustainable  future," 
then,  meant  a  system  able  to  last  for  a  long  time,  without  de- 
stroying itself  or  life  in  general.  (Of  course,  precisely  what  such 
a  system  would  entail  depended  upon  the  perspective  of  the 
speaker  imagining  it,  and  perspectives  varied.) 

Finally,  in  Cornish's  speech,  there  was  the  notion  that  people 
working  together,  "thinking  globally,  acting  locally,"  to  use  a 
phrase  coined  by  the  late  Rene  Dubos,  can  change  the  world  for 
the  better.  There  are  several  assumptions  here.  One,  that  those 
with  power,  whether  governments  or  individuals,  must  eventu- 
ally give  in  to  the  will  of  the  people.  And,  two,  that  access  to 
information  gives  people  power.  And,  that  an  individual's  free 
determination  of  his  acts  is  the  most  desirable  condition  of  human 
life. 

At  this  point,  however,  many  futurists  diverged.  Some  wanted 
individual  freedom  worldwide,  and  believed  that  easy  access  to 
communications  technologies — computers,  satellites,  tele- 
phones— would  bring  about  worldwide  democratization.  In  this 
way,  current  economic  and  political  systems  would  become  ob- 
solete, giving  way  to  holistic,  organic  arrangements.  Others,  though 
desiring  much  the  same  end — democratization — did  not  either 
anticipate  or  want  current  systems  to  wither.  Governments,  they 
said,  are  necessary  and  have  simply  to  adapt. 

"Man,"  the  soft-spoken  George  Gerbner  began  his  keynote 
speech,  "is  an  animal  who  lives  in,  of,  and  by  his  fantasies.  He 
is  a  storytelling  animal."  Gerbner,  dean  of  the  Annenberg  School 


Is  1984 
Coming  True? 


ST'TSext  year  would  be  just  an- 
l^kj  other  year  if  it  weren't  for 
JL  ^  George  Orwell's  novel  1984, 
that  nightmarish  vision  published 
in  1949.  Are  Orwell's  predictions 
coming  true?  Here  is  a  checklist. 

Big   Brother.    The   ruler   of 

Oceania,  his  enormous  face  gazes 
out  from  posters  hung  everywhere. 
"BIG  BROTHER  IS  WATCH- 
ING YOU,"  the  posters  proclaim, 
and  Big  Brother  sees  everything. 
He  is  also  infallible,  and  is  kept 
that  way  by  the  Ministry  of  Truth 
(Minitrue),  at  which  Winston  Smith 
w.orks.  Each  day  Smith  is  given 
copies  of  obsolete  news  articles, 
books,  and  speeches  that  need  to 
be  rectified.  For  instance,  when  Big 
Brother  makes  a  speech  predicting 
a  Eurasian  offensive,  but  the  Eur- 
asian front  remains  quiet.  Smith  has 
to  "rewrite  a  paragraph  of  Big 
Brother's  speech  in  such  a  way  as 
to  make  him  predict  the  thing  that 
had  actually  happened."  All  cop- 
ies of  the  original  speech  are  then 
destroyed.  In  this  way  Big  Brother 
is  never  wrong. 

Doublethink,  which  enables 

most  citizens  to  accept  such  blatant 
contradictions.  A  form  of  "reality 
control,"  doublethink  allows  one 
"to  be  conscious  of  complete  truth- 
fulness while  telling  carefully  con- 
structed lies."  This  process  makes 
possible  the  three  Party  slogans: 
"War  is  peace.  Freedom  is  slavery. 
Ignorance  is  strength."  Unfortu- 
nately, Winston  Smith  finds  him- 
self unable  to  practice  double- 
think; he  is  thus  guilty  of 
thoughtcrime ,  an  offense  punisha- 
ble by  the  Thought  Police  in  what- 
ever fashion  they  choose. 

Newspeak,  a  language  in 

which  unnecessary  words  are  de- 
stroyed, thus  diminishing  the  range 
of  unnecessary  thought.  For  in- 
stance. Smith  gets  this  assignment: 
"times  3.12.83  reporting  bb  day- 
order  doubleplusungood  refs  un- 
persons rewrite  fullwise  upsub  an- 
tefiling."  Translated  into  less 
efficient  standard  English:  "The 
reporting  of  Big  Brother's  Order 
for  the  Day  in  the  Times  of  De- 
cember 3rd  1983  is  extremely  un- 
satisfactory and  makes  references 
to  nonexistent  persons.  Rewrite  it 
in  full  and  submit  your  draft  to 
higher  authority  before  filing." 

A  new.  efficient  way  of  deal- 
ing with  crime:  vaporization.  The 
Party  needn't  waste  time  putting 
criminals  on  trial;  the  Thought  Po- 
lice simply  arrest  offenders,   kill 


them,  and  have  their  names  struck 
from  any  records.  The  offender  be- 
comes an  unperson  and  the  offense 
ceases  to  exist. 

—  Development  of  new  crimes: 
thoughtcrime,  to  ensure  good- 
thinkful  citizens,  and  sexcrime,  to 
ensure  that  all  of  a  citizen's  vital 
energies  are  focused  towards  the 
good  of  the  Party,  instead  of  being 
squandered  in  selfish  pursuit  of 
pleasure.  And  to  make  sure  that 
neither  thoughtcrime  nor  sexcrime 
goes  unpunished,  there  is  face- 
crime.  Maybe  the  Thought  Police 
can't  always  catch  you  in  the  act  of 
criminal  thoughts  or  pleasures,  but 
they  can  always  fall  back  on  the 
old  standby:  they  don't  like  the  look 
on  your  face. 

Two-way  television,  the  bet- 
ter to  monitor  Party  members.  Each 
morning  Smith  is  awakened  by  his 
television,  which  turns  on  auto- 
matically and  orders  him  and  every 
other  citizen  to  take  part  in  "the 
Physical  Jerks,"  the  morning  cal- 
isthenics. When  Smith  does  not 
quite  touch  his  toes  in  one  exer- 
cise, the  television  screams  at  him: 
"  '6079  Smith  W!  Yes,  you\  Bend 
lower,  please!  You  can  do  better 
than  that.'  "  No  one  can  change 
the  channel  or  turn  the  TV  off. 

A  new  balance  of  world  power 

that  ensures  perpetual  peace 
through  continual  war.  The  three 
world  powers — Oceania,  Eurasia, 
and  Eastasia — keep  their  econo- 
mies humming  by  waging  war 
against  one  another.  It  hardly  mat- 
ters who  is  fighting  whom — Oceania 
has  allied  itself  in  the  past  with 
Eastasia  against  the  brazen  enemy 
Eurasia,  and  has  switched  its  alli- 
ance to  Eurasia  to  fight  Eastasia. 
The  point  is,  an  enemy  is  vital. 

New  sociological   advances, 

such  as  the  60-hour  work  week  and 
the  public  hangings  of  prisoners  of 
war;  as  well  as  breakthroughs  in 
the  arts,  such  as  the  novel  writing 
machine  and  the  "versificator"  (or 
songwriting  machine);  and  new 
forms  of  community  activity,  such 
as  Hate  Week.  (It  is  supplemented 
by  the  daily  Two  Minute  Hate.) 

If  you  don't  see  these  develop- 
ments by  next  year,  though,  you 
can't  relax  yet — this  might  be  a 
checklist  for  1985.  For  at  the  start 
of  the  novel,  when  Smith  writes  the 
date  in  his  diary,  "he  did  not  know 
with  any  certainty  that  this  was 
1984.  ...  It  was  never  possible 
nowadays  to  pin  down  any  date 
within  a  year  or  two."  — PR 


FEBRUARY  1983     V 


Population 
Trends 


"^"decade  ago.  the  Club  of 

/\  Rome  report  '"Limits  to 
X  ILGrowth"  had  questioned 
whether  the  planet  could  supply  the 
resources — food,  water,  energy, 
clean  air.  sheer  breathing  room — 
needed  to  support  continued  growth 
of  population  and  industry.  De- 
mographer John  Kantner  doesn't 
question  that  such  inherent  limits 
to  growth  exist.  He  just  doesnt 
think  world  population  will  ever 
reach  the  point  where  resources  are 
stretched  to  the  breaking  point. 

Kantner.  who  heads  the  popu- 
lation dynamics  department  of  the 
Johns  Hopkins  School  of  Hygiene 
and  Public  Health,  sees  birth  rates 
plummeting  around  the  world — in 
east  Asia  and  southeast  Asia,  in 
Costa  Rica,  Chile,  parts  of  the 
Caribbean.  The  situation  in  less 
developed  countries,  he  declares. 
"is  more  optimistic  than  we  could 
have  thought  ten  years  ago." 

Countries  along  Chinas  perim- 
eter were  first  to  record  a  drop.  In 
places  like  Korea  or  Taiwan,  says 
Kantner.  "theyre  not  squeamish 
about  telling  their  people  what  to 
do."  In  other  countries,  industrial 
and  urban  development  sent  hus- 
bands off  to  the  cities  for  work, 
leaving  their  wives  alone  back  in 
the  villages.  Family  planning  pro- 
grams have  also  helped. 

Not  that  population  won't,  for  a 
time,  continue  to  climb.  According 
to  World  Bank  and  L.N.  esti- 
mates, a  century  from  now  there 
will  be  about  10  billion  people  on 
the  globe.  "There  is  a  momentum 
to  population  growth,  "  Kantner 
says — ""sort  of  like  a  flywheel  that 
stores  energy.  ■  Youthful  popula- 
tions, a  legacy  of  once-high  birth 
rates,  produce  new  babies.  That, 
combined  with  a  precipitous  de- 
cline in  death  rates  since  World  War 
II,  means  it  may  still  be  several 
generations  before  world  popula- 
tion levels  off. 

When  it  docs,  there  will  be  far- 
reaching  side  effects.  Until  re- 
cently, demographers  spoke  of  a 
"population  pyramid  "  in  which  the 
young  are  markedly  overreprc- 
scnted:  for  instance.  Indonesia's  l.l.S 
million  people  in  1975  mcluded 
about  2.1  million  younger  than  .5. 
but  less  than  half  that  many  be- 
tween .10  and  ?5.  In  more  "ma- 
ture "  Japan,  on  the  other  hand,  a 
population  roughly  ihc  same  as  In- 
donesia's had  only  10  million  peo- 
ple in  its  under-5  category — about 


the  same  as  the  nine  million  in  its 
30-35  age  group.  As  development 
proceeds,  says  Kantner.  Japan's 
type  of  "slab-sided"  pattern  is  what 
the  world  is  coming  to. 

The  consequences  of  this  new- 
pattern?  One  can  be  seen  as  the 
U.S.  debates  what  to  do  with  the 
Social  Security  system,  now  that 
proportionately  fewer  young  peo- 
ple support  a  growing  elderly  pop- 
ulation. What  Kantner  calls  ""a  dif- 
ferent opportunity  structure"  in  the 
working  world  may  be  another, 
marked  by  more  second  careers.  He 
even  sees  high  U.S.  divorce  rates  as 
an  almost  inevitable  by-product  of 
these  demographic  trends:  a  low- 
birth  rate  yields  fewer  children  to 
""hold  the  family  together."  Living 
apart  becomes  more  affordable. 

And  as  populations  shift,  says 
Kantner.  the  world's  geopolitical 
landscape  is  changing.  Migration  is 
making  Germany  more  Turkish. 
Britain  more  Pakistani,  the  United 
States  more  Latin.  And  "hetero- 
geneity." he  comments,  ""means 
problems."  Meanwhile,  the  slow- 
ing of  growth  in  the  West,  coupled 
with  continued  growth  for  the  next 
few  generations  in  the  developing 
countries,  could  mean  "an  entirely 
new  world" — in  which  nations  like 
India.  Indonesia,  or  Brazil  will  be- 
come powers  to  be  reckoned  with. 
— RK 


of  Communications  at  the  University  of  Pennsylvania,  thinks  that 
art,  medicine,  statecraft,  law.  and  the  rest  are  fabricated  through 
three  sorts  of  stories  which  intertwine  to  shape  the  world:  stories 
about  how  things  work  (fiction),  about  how  things  are  and  were 
(news  and  legends),  and  about  values  and  choices  (sermons, 
instruction,  commercials). 

In  pre-Industrial  society,  Gerbner  said,  ritualistic  storytelling 
was  a  "centralized,  stylized,  institutionalized,  socializing  ele- 
ment." During  the  Industrial  era,  now  passing,  the  printing  press 
and  its  issue,  the  book,  weakened  the  ritual.  "A  book,  an  en- 
capsulation of  knowledge,"  explained  Gerbner,  "could  be  smug- 
gled across  hitherto  impenetrable  boundaries."  Now  able  to  com- 
municate over  long  distances  of  time  and  space,  storytellers  received 
information  about  other  societies.  Conscious  of  different  inter- 
ests, they  began  to  break  up  narratives  and  tell  tales  from  mul- 
tiple points  of  view. 

Today,  Gerbner  said,  in  the  post-Industrial  age,  the  cycle  has 
come  full-circle  and  stories  are  told  mainly  by  television.  Akin 
to  the  storytelling  of  the  pre-Industrial  age,  TV  is  "an  instrument 
of  mass  ritual."  Americans  watch,  on  the  average,  6'/;  hours  of 
TV  a  day.  They  have  few  programs  to  choose  from.  Conse- 
quently, this  "tribal  religion"  is  able  to  convey  an  extremely 
homogeneous  and  limited  set  of  stories  to  a  great  number  of 
people — stories  about  a  world  that  is  shown  as  male,  white, 
middle-aged,  and  crime-ridden. 

Cable  TV,  video,  direct  transmission  satellites,  and  computers 
offer  a  way  out  of  such  a  world,  Gerbner  feels,  because  they 
give  viewers  more  choice  and  more  chance  for  control.  Instead 
of  being  run  by  distant  corporations,  such  channels  might  be 
collectively  owned  and  locally  operated.  "The  question  to  ask 
about  these  new  communications  media,"  he  concluded,  "is  not 
'Will  they  make  our  offices  more  efficient?"  but  'Will  they  make 
our  humanity  more  efficient,  humane,  equitable,  and  peaceful?' 
We  should  ask  about  the  stories  we'll  be  able  to  tell  and  weave. 
Will  they  enrich  and  diversify,  or  limit  and  confirm  inequities? 
As  we  construct  our  future  communications  networks — which 
determine  how  we  are  able  to  tell  stories — we  define  ourselves." 

The  lights  came  down,  and  for  the  next  12  minutes — it  seemed 
longer — we  were  bombarded  by  a  multi-image  presentation  on

communications  technology,  prepared  not  by  WFS  but  by  the 
National  Audiovisual  Society.  A  Star  Wars-type  soundtrack,  dec-

smiling  workers,  absorbed  students,  vivid  charts.  A  voice-over 
narrative  offered  a  paean  to  technology.  In  the  future  techno- 
logical society,  the  narrator  boomed,  everyone  will  have  ac- 
cess to  information  via  computer,  satellite,  teleconferencing, 
picturephone®.  No  one  will  commute  to  work;  they  will  "tele- 
commute,"  hooked  up  to  offices  by  phone  and  computer.  Shop- 
ping will  be  done  right  at  home,  too.  Through  cable  television, 
video,  interactive  television,  entertainment  will  take  on  whole 
new  dimensions.  As  mankind  gains  "ever  greater"  ability  to 
acquire,  manipulate,  transport,  deliver,  and  duplicate  informa- 
tion, all  human  life  will  be  enriched.  People  will  begin  to  realize 
their  true  potentials!  A  triumphant  crescendo.  Magenta  and  char- 
treuse images  machine-gun  across  the  screen.  The  thing  was  over, 
as  were  the  opening  ceremonies. 

n  Day  Two,  near  the  entrance,  someone  was  handing 
out  flyers.  They  proved  to  be  a  warning:  "BEWARE 
THE  REVENGE  OF  PROMETHEUS."  Its  diatribe 
was  compellingly  senseless: 

If  you  believe  this  conference  is  anything  but  a  huge  psyche- 
delic brainwashing  session,  read  the  program  again.  .  .  .  The 
futures  movement  is  the  20th  century  update  of  the  Roman 
Empire's  bread  and  circuses  policy  for  social  control  in  a  period 
of  collapsing  living  standards  and  population.  ...  As  the  popu- 
lation was  decimated  by  waves  of  plagues,  famine,  and  the  in- 
fertility resulting  from  the  popularity  of  homosexuality,  the  in- 
famous Roman  sporting  events  were  expanded  until  they 
occupied  almost  as  much  time  as  do  rock'n  roll,  television, 
video  games,  and  parapsychology  fads  in  the  life  of  a  Futurist 
today. 

I  decided  to  take  my  chances  and  headed  for  Session  1101,  a 
seminar  on  "Images  of  the  Communications  and  Information 
Era:  An  Overview." 

Willis  Harman,  already  speaking  as  I  squeezed  into  the  au- 
ditorium, is  a  sanguine-complexioned  professor  of  engineering 
and  economic  systems  at  Stanford,  and  a  senior  social  scientist 
at  the  Strategic  Environment  Center  of  SRI  International  in 
Menlo  Park,  California.  As  I  found  a  space  on  the  floor,  Harman 
was  saying,  "The  future  of  the  globe  depends  on  our  reevaluating 
our  situation.  We're  moving  into  a  time  when  a  major  fraction 
of  our  workforce  is  going  to  be  engaged  in  the  processing  of 
information  and  the  creation  of  technology  to  handle  that  in- 
formation. But  I  think  we  need  to  ask  ourselves  what  all  that 
technology  and  information  is  for.  We've  been  very  good  up 
until  now  at  answering  the  'how-to'  questions — how  to  put  a  man 
on  the  moon,  how  to  split  the  atom — but  we  haven't  been  so 
good  at  the  'what  for'  questions.  It's  important  to  realize  that 
economics  and  technology  aren't  goals,  but  tools." 

Harman  was  taking  up  where  Gerbner  left  off,  with  talk  of 
assessing  values.  He  went  further  and  pinpointed  two  currently 
accepted  assumptions  which,  he  insists,  must  be  discarded  if  there 
is  ever  to  be  a  "global  society."  One,  the  assumption  that  people 
can  be  defined  by  their  role  in  the  mainstream  economy.  In  the 
past  30  years,  Harman  said,  people  have  stopped  thinking  of 
themselves  as  people.  Instead,  they  consider  themselves  "con- 
sumers" or  specialists.  Partly  as  a  result,  he  argued,  economic 
logic  has  been  the  prime  shaper  of  society — but  economic  im- 
perative isn't  a  suitable  basis  for  social  decisions.  Though  Har- 
man believes  people  should  rise  up  and  insist  that  economic  logic 
is  no  longer  legitimate,  he  also  hinted  that  the  current  system, 
"kept  going  in  large  part  by  armaments  production,"  will  even- 


An  Artificial 
Pancreas? 


5?magine  this:  You  are  one  of  the 
I  10  million  Americans  who  have 
JL  diabetes  mellitus,  the  disease  in 
which  the  pancreas  fails  to  produce 
enough  insulin  to  control  blood 
sugar  levels.  Yet  you  need  no  in- 
sulin shots,  nor  is  your  diet  re- 
stricted. Instead,  a  three-inch, 
pancake-shaped  device  has  been 
implanted  in  your  body.  It  contains 
a  microcomputer,  a  ten-year  bat- 
tery, and  a  vial  of  insulin;  your 
doctor  has  programmed  the  com- 
puter to  meter  out  your  insulin  over 
a  24-hour  cycle.  In  addition,  you 
can  tell  the  computer  to  prepare 
for,  say,  a  "light  snack."  (Ac- 
tually, you  punch  a  code  number 
on  a  control  box.)  And  the  implant 
gives  you  exactly  enough  insulin  to 
take  care  of  a  light  snack,  just  as 
your  body  should  do.  The  com- 
puter records  blood  sugar  fluctua- 
tions and  can  deliver  the  records 
to  your  doctor  over  the  telephone; 
if  necessary,  the  doctor  can  repro- 
gram  your  implant.  Your  insulin 
comes  in  a  highly  concentrated  form 
developed  especially  for  this  de- 
vice. You  go  in  to  the  doctor's  of- 
fice every  few  months  for  a  refill. 

This  device  is  called  a  Pro- 
grammable Implantable  Medica- 
tion System,  or  PIMS,  and  it  is  not 
a  daydream.  Used  to  deliver  in- 
sulin, it  is  already  working  well  in 
dogs.  A  variety  of  other  applica- 
tions are  being  developed  by  a  team 
of  engineers  and  scientists  at  the 
Johns  Hopkins  Applied  Physics 
Laboratory  and  physicians  at  the 
Johns  Hopkins  Medical  Institu- 
tions. (This  is  the  same  team  that 
developed  the  first  rechargeable 
cardiac  pacemaker  in  1973.) 

The  first  human  PIMS  implant 
will  likely  call  for  release  of  mor- 
phine into  the  spinal  fluid,  to  re- 
lieve intractable  pain  in  cancer  pa- 


tients. In  this  application,  as  with 
the  "artificial  pancreas,"  a  key  fea- 
ture is  that  PIMS  can  deliver  such 
a  finely  calibrated  dosage.  With  di- 
abetic patients,  for  example,  blood 
sugar  level  would  never  swing  far 
from  normal — thus  perhaps  elim- 
inating some  of  the  long-range  ef- 
fects of  diabetes  such  as  blindness 
and  cardiac  problems.  In  pain  pa- 
tients, the  pain  would  never  be  al- 
lowed to  grow  unbearable. 

Another  advantage  of  PIMS  is 
that  it  can  deliver  a  drug  straight 
to  the  affected  organ,  as  well  as  to 
the  blood  system  as  a  whole.  Thus 
it  may  eventually  prove  a  good  way 
to  deliver  cancer  chemotherapy  to 
a  particular  organ,  such  as  a  kid- 
ney. Cancer  drugs  are  engineered 
to  kill  cancer  cells,  but  they  injure 
all  cells,  causing  side  effects  like 
nausea  and  hair  loss.  If  they  could 
be  released  slowly,  steadily,  and  in 
minute  amounts  directly  into  the 
affected  organ,  the  effect  on  the 
cancer  should  be  maximized,  the 
side  effects  minimized. 

The  obvious  question:  Could  a 
microcomputer  be  programmed  and 
equipped  to  detect,  say,  a  rise  in 
blood  pressure  (or  drop  in  blood 
sugar)  and  respond  appropriately? 
Yes,  it  could,  and  such  a  device, 
the  SAMS — Sensor  Actuated 
Medication  System — is  now  in  the 
planning  stage.  Here,  you  don't  or- 
der the  device  to  respond  to  "light 
lunch";  it  knows  you  need  the  in- 
sulin and  delivers  it  accordingly. 
"The  likelihood  that  continuous 
automatic  control  of  blood  pres- 
sure can  be  achieved  is  high,"  says 
APL's  Robert  E.  Fischell,  inven- 
tor of  PIMS.  SAMS  might  also  be 
effective  for  hemophilia,  recurring 
blood  clots,  and  various  diseases  of 
the  nervous  system. 

— William  Buchanan 


FEBRUARY  1983     VII 


tually  collapse.  "In  order  to  have  a  world  economy  that  continues 
to  grow  as  it  has  over  the  last  20  years  or  so,  we  would  have  to 
create  one  billion  jobs  by  the  end  of  the  century.  Where  are 
those  jobs  going  to  come  from?" 

The  second  outworn  assumption:  War  is  an  acceptable  means 
for  solving  international  problems.  "'War  is  impractical  and  crazy. 
It's  obsolete."  said  Harman.  Like  The  Fate  of  the  Earth  author 
Jonathan  Schell.  he  sees  nuclear  war  as  the  end  to  civilization 
as  we  know  it.  "There's  no  way  we  can  feel  secure  as  long  as 
world  peace  is  based  on  the  balance  of  terror.  And  I  think,  as 
does  Schell.  that  there  can  be  no  solution  of  the  nuclear  dilemma 
with  the  present  economic  and  political  configuration.  There's 
no  solution  short  of  reinventing  the  world.  Non-proliferation 
measures  merely  buy  time.  They  may  not  buy  very  much  time." 

The  aside,  "if  we  don't  blow  ourselves  up  first."  was  made 
again  and  again  by  various  speakers.  Buckminster  Fuller  said  it. 
Russell  Peterson,  president  of  the  Audubon  Society,  said  it. 
Nearly  everyone  threw  it  in  as  a  subclause  somewhere — with  the 
notable  exception  of  Herman  Kahn.  head  of  the  Hudson  Institute 
and  proponent  of  the  winnable  war  theory. 


As  Harman  finished.  I  hurried  upstairs  to  Session  1 109.  "Com- 
munication for  a  Sustainable  Society."  There.  Russell  Peterson, 
who  is  also  chairman  of  the  Global  Tomorrow  Coalition,  echoed 
Harman's  theme,  the  inefficiency  of  economic  logic.  "The  GNP 
as  a  measure  of  productivity  is  blind.  Whether  a  country  is  pro- 
ducing carcinogens  or  rice  doesn't  make  any  difference  in  the 
GNP.  Where  it  does  make  a  difference,  though,  is  in  the  quaHty 
of  life. 

"What  determines  quality  of  life?  Well.  I  think  we  can  certainly 
say  that  the  opportunities  for  life  and  quality  are  reduced  by 
putting  toxic  chemicals  into  the  environment,  or  by  blowing  up 
the  world  some  afternoon."  The  bulge  of  laughter  subsided  and 
Peterson  continued.  "I  think  work  is  one  of  the  things  that  de- 
termines the  quality  of  life.  It's  hard  to  find  a  human  being  who 
doesn't  want  to  have  a  job.  And  yet  we  have  huge  numbers  of 
people  unemployed  in  this  country.  My  own  feeling  is  that  we 
ought  to  give  available  jobs  to  those  least  able  to  compete  for 
them — the  rest  of  us  could  take  care  of  ourselves."  At  that, 
Peterson  sat  down  to  enthusiastic  applause. 

Then  Peterson,  Lester  Brown  (president  of  Worldwatch  In- 


The  Future  According  to  Disney 


^^^^alt  Disney's  greatest 
\^/  dream,"  the  Experimental 

T  ▼  Prototype  Community  of 
Tomorrow  (EPCOT).  has  opened 
to  an  expectant  public.  EPCOT  was 
an  idea  that  absorbed  Disney  dur- 
ing the  last  years  of  his  life.  Almost 
20  years  in  the  making.  EPCOT 
was  initially  intended  to  be  what 
its  name  imphes:  an  experimental 
city,  master-planned  by  the  engi- 
neers of  the  imagination — the  im- 
agineers. 

According  to  Walts  vision,  in 
EPCOT  private  enterprise  could 
demonstrate  its  ability  to  solve  to- 
morrows  problems,  using  an  ac- 
tual, inhabited  city  as  laboratory, 
inspiration,  and  showcase.  EP- 
COT was  to  be  a  city  without  slums, 
unemployed,  or  political  ma- 
chines, a  city  to  which  science  and 
technology  could  be  applied  in  an 
innovative  and  experimental  way. 
Perhaps  even  more  importantly,  the 
city  was  to  inspire  new  science  and 
technology  to  emerge  from  the 
wellsprings  of  private  initiative. 

For  this  purpose  Disney  ac- 
quired a  huge  parcel  of  land  near 
Orlando.  Florida  and  sought  (and 
received)  the  cooperation  of  the 
Florida  state  legislature  and  local 
officials  F3ut  the  concept  has 
evolved  away  from  Disncys  orig- 
inal idea  Even  a  complaisant  Flor- 
ida legislature  and  Disney  show- 
manship could  not  turn  a  town  of 
living  people  into  an  experimental 
prototype — one  without  politics  and 
some  measure  of  self-determina- 
tion A  community  cannot  be  en- 
gineered as  if  It  were  a  transpor- 
tation system.  What  the  dream  has 


become  is  Epcot  Center,  a  spec- 
tacular, billion-dollar  "educational 
entertainment  park."  Part  of  Walt's 
plan,  however.  EPCOT  as  a  show- 
case for  the  latest  technology,  has 
been  retained  by  Disney's  heirs. 

The  heart  of  Epcot  Center  is  Fu- 
ture World,  a  striking  collection  of 
architecturally  intriguing  pavil- 
ions. Each  pavilion  has  a  corporate 
sponsor,  and  each  pavilion  sends 
out  basically  the  same  message,  self- 
described  as  "celebrating  the  lim- 
itless potential  of  science,  industry 
and  technology  in  creating  a  better 
tomorrow."  Better  living  through 
chemistry,  and  physics,  and  bioen- 
gineering. 

The  visitor  first  encounters  Fu- 
ture World  through  Spaceship 
Earth,  a  huge  shining  geodesic 
sphere,  weighing  15  million  pounds 
and  towering  180  feet  above  the 
entrance.  Inside,  the  Bell  System 
has  helped  mount  an  18-story,  spi- 
ralling trip  through  time,  during 


which  the  history  of  communica- 
tions is  colorfully  presented  using 
audio-animatronics  (robotic  ani- 
mation) and  electronic  special  ef- 
fects. Other  pavilions,  on  motion 
(General  Motors),  energy  (Exxon), 
and  imagination  (Kodak),  use  sim- 
ilar approaches. 

With  all  the  showmanship  in  these 
very  popular  pavilions,  the  "ex- 
perimental" concepts  have  been 
shunted  aside.  The  show-exhibits 


offer  thumbnail  histories,  rapidly 
bringing  the  audience  from  pre- 
history to  the  present,  with  only 
the  most  perfunctory  nod  to  the 
future — a  car  shaped  like  a  tear- 
drop, a  recreational  vehicle  that  in- 
flates for  use. 

There  is  one  exception.  Perhaps 
the  pavilion  closest  to  Disney's 
original  concept  is  The  Land. 


stitute),  and  Robert  Rodale  (chairman  of  Rodale  Press,  Inc.) 
answered  questions  from  the  audience. 

Q:  You  didn't  talk  much  about  the  population  problem,  and  I 
think  it's  a  very  important  one.  When  you  look  at  what  the 
population  is  going  to  be  in  2000,  you  see  that  we  can't  afford 
to  do  without  nuclear  power.  But  a  lot  of  environmentalists  as- 
sociate peaceful  nuclear  with  bombs. 

Lester  Brown:  And  so  should  you.  Next  question. 

Q:  How  do  you  get  the  message  about  the  things  we  need  to  do 
across  to  a  non-reading  public?  Most  Americans  don't  read. 

Russell  Peterson:  Oh,  there  are  plenty  of  ways  to  reach  people. 
You  can  do  it  through  classrooms,  at  luncheons  of  civic  organi- 
zations, through  women's  organizations,  through  4-H  and 
Scouts,  just  wherever  you  are.  You  have  to  inform  people  be- 
fore you  can  motivate  them. 

Robert  Rodale:  People  cannot  act  against  problems  without  in- 
formation, because  many  solutions  to  problems  are  quite  com- 
plicated. I  believe  we  need  to  create  a  technical  secretariat,  as 
John  Freedman  suggests  in  Retracking  America,  to  help  foster 
an  interplay  between  planners  and  the  public.  That's  what 
we're  trying  to  do  with  our  Cornucopia  Project,  in  which  we're 


sponsored  by  Kraft.  The  Land  is 
billed  as  one  of  the  most  techno- 
logically advanced  display  centers 
in  the  world — and  it  is.  Under  a 
six-acre  structure  visitors  are  fer- 
ried along  canals  through  desert, 
prairie,  and  rain  forest  eco-sys-, 
tems.  Along  the  trip,  actual  ex- 
perimental cultivation  schemes  are 
being  carried  out:  "outer  space" 


A  cast  of  Audio-Animatronic 
foodstuffs  stars  in  the  Kitchen 
Kabaret,  a  musical  review  on 
basic  nutrition  at  The  Land, 
an  EPCOT pavilion  spon- 
sored by  Kraft. 


lettuce  and  soil-less  tomatoes. 

The  vision  of  the  future  as  laid 
out  in  Epcot  Center  is  true  to  the 
form  established  in  all  of  Disney's 
previous  ventures.  Like  Fantasy- 
land,  Adventureland  and  Main 
Street,  Future  World  is  a  world 
without  stress,  violence,  social 
problems,  wars  or  cares.  Disney 
prided  himself  on  his  optimism,  but 
his  optimism  was  of  a  restricted  sort: 
a  view  that  was  upbeat  because  it 
admitted  no  disturbing  realities.  In 
striking  contrast  to  most  other  vi- 
sions of  the  future  common  to  the 
20th  century,  Epcot  Center's  fu- 
ture is  defanged.  Science,  tech- 
nology, imagination  and  private 
enterprise  will  work  together  to  lead 
us  to  a  clean,  safe,  pleasant  world — 
a  world  with  no  divisiveness  or 
squalor.  People  come  to  Epcot  to 
be  entertained,  and  the  education 
imparted  must  be  consistent  with 
entertainment.  The  future  accord- 
ing to  Disney  is  comfortable  and, 
above  all,  familiar.  It  is  the  future 
according  to  Norman  Rockwell. 

Familiarity  is  the  essential  ingre- 
dient in  all  of  Walt  Disney  World. 
From  Main  Street  to  Future  World, 
stereotypes,  nostalgia  and  glossy- 
magazine  images  are  recalled  from 
the  audience's  mental  and  emo- 
tional storehouse  and  made  con- 
crete. The  message  is  bland,  but 
the  mode  of  presentation  aims  to 
be  spectacular.  The  secret  of  Dis- 
neyworld  is  the  triumph  of  tech- 
nique over  content.  Truly,  here, 
the  medium  is  the  message. 

— Robert  Kargon, 

Professor  of  the  History  of  Science 

Johns  Hopkins  University 


trying  to  create  a  forum  for  ideas  about  improving  U.S.  food 
production  and  to  let  people  know  about  the  way  the  food  sys- 
tem is  controlled. 

Russell  Peterson:  I'd  Hke  to  make  a  comment  in  reply  to  the 
earlier  question  about  population.  Population  is  a  tremendous 
problem,  but  in  every  area  but  Africa,  we've  seen  a  significant 
slowing  of  growth.  A  farmer  in  Thailand  may  not  understand 
the  intricacies  of  world  trading  that  create  inflation  in  his  coun- 
try, but  he  is  able  to  see  the  relation  between  the  number  of 
children  he  and  his  wife  have  and  the  quality  of  his  family's 
life. 

Toward  the  end  of  this  session,  Brown  asked  someone  seated  in 
front  to  make  a  brief  statement.  The  man  launched  into  a  rapid, 
compressed  analysis  of  what  he  called  the  "global  diseases":  the 
war  disease,  the  population  disease,  the  environmental  disease, 
the  consumption  disease.  I  later  learned  that  this  was  Rashmi 
Mayur,  environmental  scientist,  vice  president  of  the  Global  Fu- 
tures network,  and  resident  of  Bombay,  India.  The  ubiquitous 
Mayur  worked  his  way  into  many  discussions. 

By  mid-morning  Tuesday,  I  realized  that  hardly  anyone  was 
talking  about  the  future  the  way  the  canned  commentator  in  the 
multi-image  show  did.  Rarely,  during  the  whole  five  days,  did  I 
hear  anyone  describe  a  gleaming  Tomorrowland.  The  future  most 
speakers  talked  about  was  amorphous,  exceedingly  general.  On 
the  whole,  they  focused  on  inequities  and  dangerous  imbalances 
in  current  world  systems.  They  extrapolated  loosely  from  the 
present,  concluding  that  something  must  be  done  if  humankind, 
and  life  itself,  are  to  survive.  The  future,  if  things  continue  as 
is,  will  be  horrific,  many  seemed  to  say,  but  things  don't  have 
to  continue  as  is  because  we  have  the  power  to  change  them. 
Their  attitudes  swung  parabolically — down  into  gloom  when  they 
talked  about  the  future-as-extension-of-the-present,  and  up  into 
hope  when  they  talked  about,  well,  a  "sustainable  future."  A 
"better"  world,  a  global  village,  a  more  just  and  less  hierarchical 
society,  an  ecological  whole  Earth  system:  these  were  the  vague 
positive  shapes  of  their  hoped-for  future. 

Like  most  Utopian  visions,  it  sounded  lovely,  but  I  doubted 
the  likelihood  of  its  ever  arriving.  It  was  unlike  most  Utopian 
visions,  which  are  generally  presented  as  ends  sans  means.  This 
future — and  finally,  it  did  seem  to  be  a  single  entity  made  up  of 
many  parts,  like  a  jellyfish — seemed  to  be  an  extension  of  grass- 
roots organization  and  high  technology.  In  other  words,  the  means 
become  the  end. 

■■■■  uckminster  Fuller  and  Herman  Kahn,  whom  many  con- 

Bsider  archetypal  futurists,  challenged  pessimistic  atti- 
tudes about  the  world's  fate.  Fuller  did  so  indirectly,  by 
way  of  his  unbridled  ingenuity,  Kahn  not  so  indirectly. 

At  first  glance.  Fuller  and  Kahn  might  seem  to  be  at  antipodes. 
In  many  important  ways  they  are,  but  they  do  share  a  common 
belief:  that  people  are  capable  of  solving  the  world's  current 
problems  to  bring  about  a  bright  future. 

Fuller  spoke  at  Monday's  luncheon.  The  day  before,  he  had 
unveiled  a  Dymaxion  World  Map  the  size  of  a  basketball  court, 
on  which  red  tiddlywinks  represented  the  destruction  nuclear 
warheads  could  wreak.  The  tiddlywinks — one  for  each  nuclear 
warhead  on  earth — had  obliterated  all  the  landmasses  but  Ant- 
arctica. 

Nine  years  ago.  I  heard  Fuller  speak  at  California  Institute  of 
Technology,  and  it  was  sad  to  see  that  he  now  has  slowed  to  a 
cane.  His  speech,  too,  has  slowed.  At  87,  he  no  longer  thinks 
out  loud  at  a  near  incomprehensible  clip.  As  always,  though, 
Fuller  ranged  far  afield,  elaborating  and  illuminating  his  fun- 
damental tenet  that  humanity  can  survive  on  Spaceship  Earth 


FEBRUARY  1983     IX 


Rotating 
Buildings  ? 


SS^he  human  race  has  moved, 

I  from  caves  to  castles  to  high- 
^  rise  apartment  buildings,  and 
all  three  residences  have  one  thing 
in  common — they're  difficult  to 
heat.  So  whats  next,  now  that  weve 
become  so  energy  conscious?  Well, 
how  about  a  mobile  highrise,  one 
that  follows  the  sun?  Or  a  house 
whose  walls  open  up  during  the  day 
and  close  at  night,  like  the  petals 
of  a  flower?  These  are  some  of  the 
future  architectural  developments 
envisioned  by  Professor  Walter  M. 
Kroner,  director  of  Rensselaer  Po- 
lytechnic Institutes  Center  for  Ar- 
chitectural Research. 

Kroner  says  that  today's  build- 
ings are  static  and  too  limited  in 
function.  Seasons  change,  weather 
changes,  people's  needs  change — 
but  buildings  remain  the  same.  He 
offers  several  examples  of  "dy- 
namic architecture"  based  on  the 
premise  that  technology  now  exists 
to  make  buildings  more  responsive 
to  changes  in  environment. 

"If  you're  walking  into  a  cold 
wind,"  Kroner  notes,  "you  turn 
your  back  to  it."  He  says  we  should 
design  buildings  that  can  rotate  to 
turn  an  insulated  face  to  the  north 
or  to  windward.  Rotating  restau- 
rants have  been  built  atop  hotels, 
and  railroad  switching  yards  use 
turntables  to  rotate  heavy  loco- 
motives. "If  we  can  build  a  Saturn 
rocket — which  is  analogous  to  a 
skyscraper — and  move  it  to  its 
launching  pad.  we  can  build  a  mov- 
able skyscraper,  or  one  that  rotates 
on  a  fixed  base  to  track  the  sun" 
Buildings  floating  like  oceanliners 
could  do  the  same  thing.  Kroner 
also  envisions  buildings  that  sub- 
merge like  submarines  to  protect 
themselves  from  bad  weather. 

These  visions  belong  to  a  distant 
future,  he  admits.  "Closer  to  real- 
ity— or  acceptability.  I  should  say — 
we  can  have  buildmgs  with  walls 
that  change  from  morning  to  cve- 
nmg.  collecting  or  dispersing  heat. 
Buildmgs  will  be  more  analogous 
t( 


to  wear  each  day  depends  at  least 
partially  on  the  weather,  he  ex- 
plains. "On  a  cold  day,  you  wear 
a  sweater.  If  the  day  warms  up, 
vou  take  it  off."  Houses,  too,  could 
respond  to  the  weather.  Kroner  says 
that  walls  can  be  designed  that  react 
to  heat  and  light,  so  that  on  a  warm 
dav.  the  walls  open  up  or  become 
more  permeable.  On  a  cold  day, 
the  walls  close  up  and  gather  heat 
from  the  sun.  "Water  can  be 
pumped  through  the  walls  to  ra- 
diate heat  where  it  is  needed." 

Kroner  envisions  another  change 
that  could  conserve  both  energy  and 
space.  As  it  is  now,  he  says,  we 
have  many  single-use  buildings, 
such  as  churches,  theaters,  stadi- 
ums, or  the  lobbies  of  office  build- 
ings. A  building  that  sits  vacant  for 
five  out  of  seven  days  is  wasteful. 
Buildings  can  be  designed  to  serve 
multipurposes.  or  to  serve  both 
public  and  private  needs.  For  ex- 
ample, a  theater,  which  has  a  slop- 
ing floor,  could  be  built  with  a  hy- 
draulic lift  to  make  the  floor  flat, 
so  the  building  could  be  used  as  a 
gymnasium  when  it's  not  in  use  as 
a  theater. 

Finally,  Kroner  suggests  a  new 
approach  to  city  planning.  As  it  is 
now,  most  cities  are  autonomous. 
Each  has  its  own  cultural  and  en- 
tertainment centers,  business  and 
shopping  districts.  Kroner  envi- 
sions a  time  when  each  city  will 
specialize  in  a  single  service;  cities 
will  be  connected  by  high-speed 
transit.  "In  this  way.  you  reduce 
the  requirement  of  every  city  hav- 
ing all  things  for  all  people."  Does 
this  mean  that  you  might  work  in 
Washington,  shop  in  Philadelphia, 
and  catch  a  show  that  night  in  New 
York?  Kroner  does  not  find  that 
far-fetched,  pointing  out  that  this 
country  already  has  some  highly 
specialized  cities.  "What  is  Las  Ve- 
gas but  a  big  fun  city?  What  is 
Washington,  D.C.  but  one  big  ad- 
ministrative citv?"  What,  indeed? 
—PR 


by  using  technology  to  do  "more  and  more  with  less  and  less." 
"When  I  was  young,"  he  began,  "humanity  was  remotely  de- 
ployed on  the  globe.  If  I  went  up  into  the  second  floor  of  our 
house,  I  could  see  14  miles  every  way  to  the  horizon.  People 
normally  stayed  close  in,  inside  those  horizons.  Our  conditioned 
reflexes  were  based  on  our  seeing  a  lot  of  each  other. 

"We  all  walked  a  lot  within  those  boundaries,  and  we  wanted 
to  know  how  far  we  went,  so  we  wore  pedometers.  According 
to  those  pedometers,  the  average  person  walked  about  1,100 
miles  a  year,  and  rode  about  the  same.  After  World  War  II,  a 
salesman  drove  30,000  miles  a  year,  an  average  person  about 
10.000.  and  they  walked  the  same  1,100  miles. 

"Shortly  after  World  War  II,  people  were  moving  about  every 
five  years.  They  no  longer  stayed  inside  those  horizons.  Now, 
it's  closer  to  every  one-and-a-half  years.  One-and-a-half  years," 
he  mused.  "It  seems  incompatible  with  30-year  mortgages,  doesn't 
it?"  Pained  laughter. 

"Now  we've  got  the  highway  system,"  Fuller  went  on,  "and 
humanity  is  going  in  different  directions.  The  old  customs,  laws, 
and  the  shapes  of  the  cities  have  nothing  to  do  really  with  the 
wav  we  now  live.  We  have  to  look  at  the  whole  world  now  to 
know  what's  going  on. 

"In  the  past  50  years  or  so,  we've  begun  more  and  more  to 
move  from  what  I  call  the  visible  reality  to  the  invisible  reality. 
X-rays,  radio  waves,  electricity — we  use  resources.  99.99  percent 
of  which  no  one  knew  were  there  when  I  was  born  in  1895,  and 
99.99  percent  of  which  are  non-contactable  by  the  human  senses. 
I  think  there's  enough  of  everything  to  go  around."  concluded 
Fuller.  "It  depends  on  how  we  utilize  what  we  have.  A  geodesic 
dome,  for  instance,  uses  a  small  fraction  of  the  materials  that  a 
conventional  structure  takes." 

On  Wednesday,  I  heard  Kahn  speak.  He  looks  like  Henry 
VIII  with  wire-rimmed  glasses.  "A  lot  of  people  don't  like  the 
undercurrent  of  optimism  and  hope  that  runs  through  my  work," 
he  began  his  extemporaneous  talk.  "A  lot  of  people  call  me  an 
eternal  optimist.  Actually,  with  the  manic  pessimism  in  this  coun- 
try, a  realist — and  that's  what  I  really  am — looks  like  a  manic 
optimist.  Pessimism  pervades  this  country. 

"People  seem  unable  to  see  what's  in  front  of  their  faces.  In 
fact,  that's  been  true  since  I  was  a  kid.  One  time  in  elementary 
school,  the  teacher  said,  and  everyone  in  the  class  agreed  with 
her.  that  wars  never  settle  anything.  What  did  she  mean,  wars 
never  settle  anything?  Of  course  they  settle  things.  I  got  into  a 
big  argument  and  lost.  While  everyone  else  was  out  for  recess, 
I  wrote  on  the  board  '32  Things  Settled  by  War.'  Number  one, 
American  freedom  from  England.  Number  two,  the  possession 
of  Mexico.  Number  three,  and  on.  Everyone  came  in  from  recess 
and  we  got  in  another  big  argument  and  I  lost  again.  But  I  was 
right!  This  country,  particularly  the  press,  suffers  from  what 
Webern  calls  'educated  incapacity.'  People  are  overeducated, 
and  have  an  inability  to  deal  with  problems. 

"Every  major  issue  that  you  can  name  in  the  last  decade — law 
and  order,  busing,  gun  laws,  pornography,  sex  education — has 
been  mishandled  by  the  press.  Did  anyone  ever  think  that  busing 
kids  long  distances  to  bad  schools  was  the  way  to  solve  the  black 
problem?  I  could  have  told  them  it  couldn't  bo  done  in  a  de- 
mocracy. It  can't! 

"The  whole  business  about  the  energy  crisis  is  one  of  the 
biggest  hoaxes  of  all  time.  It  simply  isn't  the  case  that  we'll  run 
out  of  fossil  fuels — its  not  possible.  What  are  some  of  the  con- 
ceptual frauds  the  press  has  perpetrated  about  oil?  'Gas  prices 
have  been  going  up  for  the  last  decade.'  that's  one.  In  fact,  in 
terms  of  the  fixed  1972  dollar,  oil  has  gone  down  20  percent.
