"OK.  The  press  claims  that  demand  is  going  to  continue  to 
grow  and  that  we're  going  to  run  out  of  oil.  As  a  matter  of  fact, 
according  to  Exxon  projections,  there's  going  to  be  a  drop  in 
demand  over  the  next  decade.  That's  already  started  to  happen. 
What  were  the  mistakes  that  the  press  made  there?"  A  slide 
goes  up:  "They  ignored  the  possibility  and  probability  of  the 
following:  energy  conservation;  accelerating  U.S.  and  worldwide 
production;  breakthroughs  in  petroleum  technology;  changes  in 
the  structure  of  OPEC;  unconventional  sources  of  oil  and  gas; 
in  situ  conversion;  and  the  early  arrival  of  long-term  technology. 

"If  there's  an  energy  problem  it's  because  there's  too  much 
governmental  restriction  of  strip  mining,  nuclear  power,  and  oil 
and  gas  production.  That's  what  is  causing  stagflation,  not  the 
budget  deficit.  If  you  look  at  the  debt  in  constant  dollars,  you'll 
see  that  the  debt  has  been  paid  off  many  times  over  the  past 
decade  by  inflation. 

"The  information  you  receive  through  the  media  is  highly 
slanted  toward  pessimism.  But  I  can  tell  you,  there  are  good 
things  to  come."  Kahn  didn't  tell  what  those  things  were;  he  said 
we  could  read  his  book. 


y  this  time  it  was  obvious  that  most  futurists  see  basically 
two  avenues  to  a  sustainable  future:  technological  ad- 
vances and  people  networks.  I  moved  on  to  two  sessions 
which  promised  me  an  overview  of  the  former. 


Session  2201:  There  Robert  Lucky,  exuberant  executive  di- 
rector of  Communications  Services  at  Bell  Labs,  outlined  some 
of  the  esoteric  refinements  now  being  tested  at  Bell.  Take  mi- 
croelectronics. Fingernail-size  chips  which  carry  250,000  bits  (units 
of  information)  now  exist.  But,  said  Lucky,  "you  ain't  seen  nothin' 
yet.  It's  scary.  We're  getting  to  the  point  where  we  think  a 
600,000-bit  chip  is  perfectly  possible." 

And  then  there's  photonics,  the  use  of  optical  fibers  for  sending 
messages.  Bell  has  already  installed  15,000  miles  of  optical  fibers 
this  year,  25  times  more  than  in  1981.  A  single  optical  fiber  can 
replace  a  bunch  of  copper  wire  thick  as  a  thumb  and  carry  up 
to  90,000  conversations.  "Now  we're  experimenting  with  sending 
pulses  that  are  only  femptoseconds  [fempto  =  one-quadrillionth] 
long — a  femptosecond  is  so  short  that  light  only  travels  one-fifth 
the  length  of  a  human  hair  in  that  time — so  that  we  could  send 
a  billion  messages  before  Bell  would  even  earn  a  penny — not 
that  Bell  is  thrilled  with  that  prospect.  Using  femptosecond  mes- 
sage units,  every  conversation  going  on  in  the  U.S.  at  one  time 
could  be  carried  by  a  single  fiber." 

Some  more  "mundane  services"  that  Lucky  said  Bell  may  soon 
offer  are: 

1.  A  telephone  number  attached  to  person  rather  than  place. 
"You  could  dial  a  friend,  and  wherever  he  was,  the  phone 
would  ring." 

2.  A  phone  which  tells  who's  calling  before  you  pick  it  up. 


FEBRUARY  1983    XI 


3.  Data  communication  networks  for  people  or  companies  who 
want  to  share  information. 

4.  Picturephone^**  meeting  serNice.  hke  a  conference  call  with 
visibility. 

5.  ■"Intelligent"  telephones  which  can  send  electronic  mail, 
check  your  calendar  for  you.  and  look  up  numbers  in  your  per- 
sonal directory. 

6.  Information  kiosks  with  touch-sensitive  TV  screens  which 
will  give  you  information  about  restaurants,  hotels,  rides  (Dis- 
neyworld  has  a  test  kiosk),  then  give  you  access  to  a  data  base 
with  additional  information. 

7.  A  Good  Samaritan  computer  in  your  car  which  gives  infor- 
mation such  as  the  best  route  from  Tucson  to  Tucumcari.  tells 
which  stores  are  open  along  the  way.  and  offers  advice.  (If  peo- 
ple do  this,  it's  called  back-seat  driving.) 

8.  Home  information  systems  which  let  you  plug  into  local  li- 
brary listings,  train  times,  newspapers,  etcetera.  People  in 
Coral  Gables.  Florida  are  testing  a  prototype  for  Bell. 

9.  Remote  control  of  appliances  by  phone.  In  addition  to  turn- 
ing on  and  off  the  oven,  the  600  families  in  North  Carolina 
testing  this  system  are  able  to  arrange  phone  hook-up  with  util- 
ity companies  to  draw  power  out  of  plants  at  non-peak  hours 
and  store  it.  Power  stations  are  spared  brownouts,  and  people 
get  energy  at  low  rates. 

10.  Computer  games.  Besides  being  able  to  dial  bridge  partners 
and  play  a  game  via  terminals.  Lucky  thinks  you  will  eventually 
be  able  to  dial  a  number  and  drive  a  Grand  Prix  race  on  a  real- 
istic track  shown  on  the  terminal  screen. 


Of  course,  said  Lucky,  these  developments  depend  on  someone 
finding  enough  money  to  pay  for  them. 

Paul  Strassman,  a  PhD  in  strategic  planning  from  MIT,  now 
a  vice  president  with  Xerox  Corporation,  took  the  microphone 
and  put  a  big  damper  on  Lucky's  tinkly  forecast.  "My  colleague's 
talk  exemplified  the  quintessence  of  the  supply-side  mentality — 
there's  a  plethora  of  gadgets,  if  people  could  only  afford  them. 
I'm  here  to  tell  you  about  the  harsh  reality  of  the  demand  side." 
Basically,  Strassman  doubts  that  capital  will  be  available,  either 
in  the  U.S.  or  globally,  to  get  everyone  onto  the  high-tech  band- 
wagon. Right  now.  he  argued,  there  is  a  "misallocation  of  capital 
to  the  workforce."  Worldwide  expenditures  for  information  tech- 
nology or  hardware,  he  said,  "are  quite  low.  Only  a  small  number 
o(  countries  are  buying  equipment  today." 

Even  in  the  U.S.,  distribution  of  hardware  is  less  extensive 
than  one  might  think.  When  you  look  at  the  cost  of  dealing  out 
word  processors  and  other  information  paraphernalia  to  the 
workforce,  Strassman  said,  you  sec  that  there  are  great  barriers 
to  getting,  say,  50  percent  of  the  workforce  supplied  by  1990. 
Business  would  have  to  increase  its  capital  investment  to  ap- 
proximately $25,0(K)  per  worker  to  do  that — roughly  double  the 
present  per  worker  investment.  "It's  hard  to  take  resources  that 
have  been  going  somewhere  else  and  apply  them  to  this.  Unless 
business  learns  how  to  increase  capital  investment,  particularly 
in  equipment  for  managerial  and  technical  workers,  I'm  not  op- 
timistic— despite  the  tremendous  power  of  the  new  technology, 
and  the  fact  that  its  highly  profitable — that  we'll  see  worldwide 
adoption  of  these  technologies." 

^^^^  lit  what  happens  when  the  technologies  are  adopted? 

B>()u  get  something  like  California's  Silicon  Valley.  "Sil- 
icon Valley,"  said  Ivverett  Rogers,  organizer  and  mod- 
erator of  Session  1410,  "is  one  scenario — warts  and  all — 
of  the  future.  Others  being  Route  12S  in  Massachusetts.  Silicon 
Desert  in  Dallas,  and  Silicon  Mountain.  Colorado  Springs." 


The  session  speakers  had  seen  the  future.  Did  it  work? 

"California,"  quipped  Jorge  Schement,  associate  professor  at 
Annenberg's  branch  at  the  University  of  Southern  California, 
"is  different  from  the  rest  of  the  U.S.  It's  weird,  but  it  has  to 
be  taken  seriously.  It  has  3  percent  of  the  labor  force  in  the 
country,  and  produces  25  percent  of  the  agricultural  products. 
If  California  were  a  separate  nation,  it  would  have  the  thirteenth 
largest  GNP  in  the  world." 

Fifty-one  percent  of  the  labor  force  in  California  works  to 
produce,  recycle,  or  maintain  information.  That  number  is  grow- 
ing, and  Schement's  studies  show  that  workers  are  shifting  mainly 
out  of  jobs  in  the  agricultural  sector  to  take  up  positions  in  the 
information  industry. 

But  there  is  a  problem.  For  the  information  sector  to  keep 
growing.  California  needs  more  workers  with  high-  and  middle- 
level  skills  (engineers,  programmers,  writers).  The  state  would 
prefer  not  to  import  those  workers,  so  an  intensive  education 
effort  is  needed,  which  in  turn  requires  talented  teachers  and 
professors.  But  because  industry  can  outbuy  school  systems,  col- 
leges, and  universities,  Schement  said,  California  schools  are  in 
for  trouble.  Where  are  they  going  to  get  the  teachers?  "California 
simply  has  to  educate  its  masses,  and  that's  why,  in  many  ways, 
it  can  be  called  a  developing  country." 

In  addition,  the  balance  of  power  may  shift  as  the  workforce 
becomes  increasingly  divided  into  high-skill  and  low-skill  groups. 
"Having  an  elite  labor  force  can  be  tricky.  If  the  poor  have  many 
votes,  that  translates  into  a  kind  of  power.  But  if  you  see  an 
increasing  concentration  of  voting  power  in  the  hands  of  the 
income  rich — which  is  what  we're  seeing — then  what  will  hap- 
pen?" 

Some  of  the  social  costs  are  obvious  already,  said  Judith  Lar- 
sen.  Larsen,  a  senior  research  assistant  at  Cognos  Associates, 
showed  slides  of  Silicon  Valley,  the  smokeless  factories  cheek- 
by-jowl  down  a  20-mile  section  of  the  San  Francisco  peninsula. 
"Not  everything  is  ideal,"  she  commented.  Some  6500  acres  of 
prime  farmland  vanished  in  the  feverish  period  between  1950 
and  1975,  when  37  landscaped  industrial  parks  were  constructed. 
Longtime  residents  were  driven  out  and  housing  costs  rocketed. 
In  1950,  there  were  78,000  housing  units  in  the  area.  In  1975, 
there  were  325,000  multiple-dwelling  units  (apartments,  condos), 
and  500,000  single  dwellings  (many  in  communities  zoned  to 
require  at  least  five  acres  per  dwelling).  The  influx  of  people 
still  overwhelmed  available  housing.  High  rents  and  housing  costs 
drove  low-level  workers  20  and  30  miles  away,  to  San  Jose  or 
San  Mateo,  to  find  affordable  housing.  Public  transportation  is 
"dismal,"  said  Larsen,  so  bottom-level  workers  are  tied  into  a 
long  commute  on  the  overcrowded  Bayshore  Freeway  (known 
as  the  "Bloody  Bayshore"). 

These  results,  claimed  Everett  Rogers,  are  the  logical  exten- 
sion of  forms  of  production  in  the  information  industry.  In  the 
hands  of  both  U.S.  and  Japanese  supercapitalists.  Rogers  said, 
information  technology — both  its  production  and  implementa- 
tion— only  magnifies  the  gap  between  rich  and  poor. 

^^^■B  iscussions  of  new  technology  were  fairly  straightfor- 

Dward:  Here  is  the  hardware,  now  what  are  we  going  to 
do  with  it?  When  it  came  to  the  other  means  to  change, 
people,  discussions  could  follow  weird  and  circuitous 
routes.  Take  session  2306,  for  instance. 

Session  2.306  met  in  the  enormous  Washington  Ballroom,  which 
was  jammed.  As  soon  as  I  saw  who  was  at  the  table  on  stage,  I 
knew  why:  there  had  been  a  shrewd  PR  campaign.  Earlier  in 
the  day,  I  waited  through  an  endless  line  to  the  phone  bank. 


Xli    ALUMNI  MAGAZINE  CONSORTIUM 


WvTER,  ^X^TER  Everywhere 


S^ach  person  on  Earth  needs  two 
1^  liters  of  water  each  day  to 
X^  stave  off  dehydration.  While 
the  average  person  in  the  United 
States  uses  700  liters  daily  (wash- 
ing, cooking,  watering  lawns),  an 
estimated  two  billion  people 
worldwide  lack  an  easily  obtaina- 
ble supply  of  safe  water.  In  rural 
sections  of  Third  World  nations, 
women  and  children  may  spend 
eight  hours  each  day  traveling  to 
and  from  the  nearest  source  of 
water. 

The  United  Nations  has  de- 
clared 1981-1990  the  International 
Drinking  Water  Supply  and  Sani- 
tation Decade,  but  will  the  ambi- 
tious program,  undertaken  in  an 
era  of  global  economic  woes,  suc- 
ceed? "I'm  one  of  the  skeptics," 
says  environmental-health  engi- 
neer Kazuyoshi  Kawata.  The  pro- 
gram, says  the  Johns  Hopkins 
School  of  Hygiene  professor,  "has 
already  failed  in  many  areas,  al- 
though Latin  America  is  doing  bet- 
ter than  Africa."  Abel  Wolman,  a 
Hopkins  professor  emeritus  and  an 
international  water  expert,  agrees: 
"Real  progress  has  been  made  only 
in  Central  and  South  America,  the 
regions  with  greater  resources  in 
skilled  manpower  and  monies."  The 
African  deserts  continue  to  grow. 

In  contrast,  predicted  water 
shortages  in  the  United  States  take 
on  what  planners  call  a  "local"  as- 
pect. "For  the  U.S.  as  a  whole," 
says  M.  Gordon  Wolman,  a  Johns 
Hopkins  environmental  engineer, 
"there  is  not  a  shortage  of  water. 
There  are  areas  of  intensive  use — 
parts  of  the  Southwest  and  the 
Plains  states — which  may  not  be 
able  to  continue  using  water  as  they 
have."  Solutions?  Changing  from 
agriculture  to  less-water  uses  of  the 
land,  better  water  management, 
or — in  the  distant  future — import- 
ing water.  Everyone  will  still  have 
enough  water  to  drink.  Barring 
natural  disasters,  of  course.  "If  an 
earthquake  destroyed  the  aque- 
ducts that  Los  Angeles  depends 
on,"  warns  Theodore  Crusberg,  of 
Worcester  Polytechnic  Institute's 
biology  and  biotechnology  depart- 
ment, "L.A.  would  be  in  big  trou- 
ble." 

Cities  around  the  world,  includ- 
ing those  in  so-called  developing 
nations,  already  face  a  common 
problem.  Most  urban  water  sys- 
tems were  built  early  in  the  20th 
century,  with  pipes  made  to  last  50 
or  60  years.  In  many  cases,  those 
pipes  have  been  in  place  70  years 
or  more.  Kawata  tells  stories  ot  pa- 
per-thin pipes  in  Cairo,  of  pipes  in 
Manila  so  corroded  that  the  area 


wmitm^am0st 


through  which  water  can  flow  is 
less  than  half  the  original  cross-sec- 
tion. 

Closer  to  home,  the  New  York 
City  public-works  department  is 
said  to  avoid  touching  some  of  its 


water  valves,  fearing  that  the  cor- 
roded valves  would  burst  if  turned. 
Each  year,  Baltimore  City  loses  an 
estimated  18  percent  of  its  water 
through  leaks  and  "unaccounted- 
for  water."  Utilities  workers  still 


find  wooden  pipes  in  use  in  many 
cities.  "We're  about  20  years  be- 
hind in  maintenance  of  all  sys- 
tems," estimates  Abel  Wolman, 
"both  water  and  wastewater."  The 
solution  is  "rehabilitation.  It  means 
orderly  planning  of  major  repairs, 
work  that  will  cost  billions  of  dol- 
lars, but  which  will  have  to  be  done 
on  a  piecemeal  basis,  year  by  year." 

Corroded  pipes  mean  lost  water; 
they  can  also  mean  impure  water. 
In  fact,  says  Gordon  Wolman, 
"water  quantity  may  not  be  the 
problem — it  may  be  water  qual- 
ity." Daniel  Pope,  director  of  the 
Freshwater  Institute  at  Rensselaer 
Polytechnic  Institute,  agrees.  An 
expert  on  Legionnaires'  disease,  he 
studies  what  he  calls  "a  whole  new 
set  of  diseases  related  to  modern 
technology — and  related  to  water." 
Cholera  and  salmonella  are  no 
longer  threats  for  industrialized 
nations;  PCBs  and  other  carcino- 
gens are.  The  danger  of  ground- 
waters contaminated  by  hazardous 
wastes  is  real,  says  Charles  O'Me- 
lia,  a  wastewater  expert  at  Johns 
Hopkins. 

One  possible  solution  is  a  return 
to  ocean  dumping.  The  sites,  of 
course,  would  have  to  be  carefully 
picked.  "It  depends  on  what  the 
substance  is,"  says  O'Melia.  "Some 
should  be  placed  where  they  can 
be  dissolved,  carried  away."  He 
adds,  "I  don't  think  it  will  be  good 
for  the  ocean,  but  it  may  be  nec- 
essary." 

Another  threat  to  water  quality 
is  acid  rain,  much  talked  about  in 
the  press.  "Evidence  is  gradually 
accumulating,"  says  Gordon  Wol- 
man, "that  acid  rain  is  more  of  a 
problem  than  many  would  have 
predicted  a  decade  ago — with 
damage  to  lakes  and  rivers,  as  well 
as  deleterious  effects  on  forests, 
soils,  and  materials."  Liming  lakes 
in  New  York's  Adirondacks,  for 
example,  to  restore  proper  pH  bal- 
ance is  what  Daniel  Pope  calls  "a 
great  solution — so  long  as  you  don't 
have  to  do  it  every  year."  But 
Charles  O'Melia  warns,  "It's  not 
going  to  be  a  single-shot  process. 
Lakes  are  still  going  to  receive  acid 
rain,  and  the  sediments  at  the  lake 
bottom  are  also  acidic." 

And,  finally,  what  about  "the 
greenhouse  effect"?  It's  too  early 
to  tell,  says  Gordon  Wolman,  but 
a  real  warming-up  of  the  Earth, 
over  the  next  100  years  or  so,  could 
mean  the  melting  of  polar  ice  caps. 
The  sea  level  would  begin  to  rise, 
flooding  coastal  areas.  "It's  hap- 
pened before,"  he  notes  with  a 
geographer's  long-range  vision. 

— MRY 


FEBRUARY  1983     XIII 


When  I  reached  the  head  of  the  Hne,  a  woman  with  voluminous 
skirts  and  Medusa-like  hair  put  her  hand  over  the  mouthpiece 
of  the  phone  she  was  monopolizing  and  said.  "What  medium 
are  you  from?"  Medium?  She  had  noticed  my  press  badge.  "Have 
you  heard  about  the  mind-to-mind  session  this  afternoon?"  she 
asked.  I  said  that  I  had,  but  planned  to  hear  Rashmi  Mayur  talk 
about  communications  and  global  economic  development.  "Don't 
go  hear  him."  she  said,  "he  always  says  the  same  thing.  I  hear 
this  mind-to-mind  thing  is  going  to  be  great.  They're  supposed 
to  reveal  the  results  of  some  research  that's  never  been  discussed 
yet."  I  shrugged,  she  went  back  to  her  phone  and — back  at  the 
Washington  Ballroom — w  ho  should  be  sitting  on  stage?  Who  else 
but  my  informant.  Carol  Rosin.  This  medium.  I  said  to  myself. 
has  been  manipulated. 

James  Beal.  an  engineer  for  Martin  Marietta,  began  the  session 
with  a  talk  about  the  need  to  unlearn  the  definition  of  reality 
"that  has  been  imposed  on  us.  How?  Via  various  Asian  mind/ 
body  disciplines  (such  as  tai  ch'i),  externally  imposed  low  fre- 
quency sound,  harmony  generators  (?),  vertical  swings,  sensory 
deprivation  tanks.  Biofeedback.  The  electromagnetic  induce- 
ment of  sense  data.  The  Neurophone — a  thing  that  emits  a 
"scratchy,  thin  frequency"  which  shifts  you  into  your  creative, 
non-verbal  right  brain  and  allows  you  to  "empathize  and  pick 
up  on  people's  attitudes,  to  know  what's  wrong  with  them." 

Soon,  Beal  thinks,  even  newer  technology  will  lead  us  into  a 


new  world  of  enlightenment.  Soon  it  will  be  possible  to  feed 
sound  and  light  straight  into  people's  heads.  There  will  be  ways 
to  actually  record  experience,  a  vacation,  say,  and  replay  it  so 
you  feel  again  all  the  things  you  felt  then.  (All  of  them?  I  hoped 
Beal  or  someone  else  would  devise  an  editing  mechanism.) 

Then  Thomas  Beardon,  a  "senior  advisory  engineer"  (no  af- 
filiation) from  Huntsville.  Alabama,  took  the  mike,  saying, 
"Nothing  that  you  take  for  certain  now  is  certain."  Beardon's 
baritone  and  unshakable  delivery  made  him  sound  like  a  TV 
evangelist.  "Scientific  method  does  not  yield  truth,"  he  intoned. 
"Time  is  not  objective.  Scientific  instruments  destroy  time  in  the 
very  act  of  measuring  it.  Scientific  instruments  only  measure  a 
partial  reality."  (So  far,  it  sounds  familiar — but  wait.) 

"What  do  we  lose  when  we  measure  only  part  of  reality?" 
Beardon  demanded.  "What  is  the  only  thing  that  occupies  time 
but  no  space?"  He  paused  portentously.  "Being! — We  under- 
stand from  spatial  relativity  that  time  can  be  converted  into  length. 
That  means  instruments  can  be  built  to  measure  mind!  I  won't 
go  into  it  here,  but  take  my  word  for  it.  Such  instruments  exist! 

"Nikola  Tesla  discovered  the  22  flaws  in  electromagnetic  the- 
ory and  he  was  suppressed.  Why?  Because  he  would  have  pro- 
vided free  energy  to  the  entire  world.  [Tesla,  an  electrical  en- 
gineer, worked  with  high-tension  electricity  and  alternating  current 
transmission,  among  other  things,  and  designed  the  power  gen- 
erating system  at  Niagara  Falls.]  I  won't  elaborate  here,  but  it 


Corn  Stalks  into  Chemicals? 


SSShe  age-old  practice  of  burn- 
I  ing  wood  for  energy  is  inef- 
JL  ficient  and  lacks  finesse.  In 
the  near  future,  wood — and  bio- 
mass  in  genera! — will  likely  be  used 
in  cleverer  ways.  With  modern-day 
alchemy,  biomass  can  be  trans- 
formed into  cattle  feed  and  paper 
pulp — as  well  as  a  number  of  prod- 
ucts normally  produced  from  pe- 
troleum feedstocks.  In  fact,  ac- 
cording to  Rensselaer  Polytechnic 
Institute  engineer  Henry  R.  Bun- 
gay, wood  chips,  corn  stover,  and 
sugar  cane  will  soon  compete  with 
hydrocarbons  for  a  share  of  the 
chemical  manufacturing  market. 

Supported  in  its  early  stages  pri- 
marily by  Department  of  Energy 
grants,  most  research  on  biomass 
conversion  has  sought  cheap  alter- 
natives to  fossil  fuels,  such  as 
methane  and  methanol.  Bungay, 
however,  a  professor  in  RPIs 
chemical  and  environmental  engi- 
neering department,  argues  that 
while  such  fuels  arc  technically  fea- 
sible, they're  economically  im- 
practical. Better,  he  wrote  in  a  re- 
cent survey  in  Science  magazine,  to 
convert  wood  or  high-ccllulosc  ag- 
ricultural leftovers  into  valuable 
cellulose  pulp.  Iignin.  and  glucose- 
rich  syrups  at  a  competitive  cost. 
The  bulk  of  biomass  used  in  such 
operations  would  come  from  two 
main  stjurccs:  trees  and  corn  stover. 


Corn  stover — what's  left  of  plants 
once  ears  have  been  harvested — is 
normally  plowed  back  into  fields 
for  erosion  control  and  to  provide 
organic  nutrients  necessary  to  soil 
health.  The  topic  is  controversial, 
but  Bungay  says  that  as  a  rule  of 
thumb  one  half  of  every  crop  should 
be  plowed  under  to  aid  nitrogen 
fixation;  the  rest  could  go  to  bio- 
mass conversion.  The  best  source 
of  wood  would  not  be  fast-growing 
pine,  already  farmed  for  lumber, 
but  fast-growing  hardwoods  such 
as  poplar,  which  can  be  coppiced 
to  increase  yields.  Nurseries  ex- 
perimenting with  this  method  of 
pruning  have  harvested  as  much  as 
15  dry  tons  per  acre  per  year  (nor- 
mal stands  in  the  same  area  only 
yield  one  to  two  tons).  It  is  also 
possible  to  use  other  feedstocks: 
pilot  plants  in  oil-poor  Puerto  Rico 
use  a  special  strain  of  "energy"  cane 
which  provides  enough  growth  to 
satisfy  both  sugar  refining  and  bio- 
mass conversion  needs. 

Given  a  bunch  of  high-cellulose 
biomass,  there  are  several  ways  of 
refining  it,  many  recently  devel- 
oped. All  result  in  the  decompo- 
sition of  the  biomass  into  its  three 
main  components  (or  variations 
thereof):  cellulose  and  hemicellu- 
lose.  carbohydrate  polymers  which 
can  be  broken  down  into  sugars; 
and  liunin.  the  loueh  polviiicr  lh;il 


XIV   ALUMNI  MAGAZINE  CONSORTIUM 


is  now  possible  to  take  matter  apart  like  a  pretzel  and  put  it 
together.  Using  Tesla's  scalar  longitudinal  waves,  I  can  take  that 
table  apart  and  reassemble  it  over  there,  without  doing  any  work!" 

A  lot  of  people  turned  towards  the  back  wall,  waiting  for  the 
table  to  de/  and  rematerialize.  It  did  neither. 

Beardon  resumed  his  oration,  unfazed.  "What  are  the  impli- 
cations of  this?  It  means  that  we  now  have  the  ability  to  shake 
up  the  stream  of  time.  If  the  stream  of  time  is  shaken  up,  it's 
like  being  on  the  end  of  a  log  bouncing  in  water." 

Beardon  said  he  sees  three  possible  futures.  One,  that  incon- 
ceivably horrible  weapons  which  shake  up  the  time  stream  will 
be  set  off,  bringing  about  the  "Armageddon  described  in  the 
Revelations  of  John."  Two,  that  cities  will  be  tied  by  electro- 
magnetic energy,  creating  a  "primitive  nervous  system"  and  rais- 
ing the  level  of  the  collective  unconscious.  Three,  that  all  people 
will  take  their  fates  into  their  own  hands  and  utterly  change  the 
world,  becoming  one  great  body  with  eight  billion  hands.  Bear- 
don ended  by  saying  that  if  he  had  10  million  dollars  and  the 
right  eight  people,  he  could  bring  about  scenario  number  three 
in  two  years. 

As  Beardon  concluded,  Barbara  Marx  Hubbard,  the  Futures 
Network  president,  stood  up,  leaned  to  the  microphone,  and 
said,  "This  is  too  exciting  to  say  sitting  down."  Hubbard,  an 
energetic  woman  with  short,  ash-grey  hair,  proceeded  to  describe 
a  "fundamental  experience"  that  she  had  had  one  day  in  1966 


binds  the  celluloses  together. 

Products  differ  depending  on  the 
process.  For  instance,  a  new  tech- 
nique developed  by  Biological  En- 
ergy Corporation,  a  Philadelphia- 
based  consortium,  turns  hemicel- 
lulose  into  fermentable  sugars  and 
breaks  lignin's  three-dimensional 
structure,  leaving  freed  cellulose 
fibers  almost  intact.  Such  cellulose 
can  be  sold  as  high-quality  pulp  for 
paper  manufacture.  Other  meth- 
ods leave  the  cellulose  in  bad  shape, 
but  allow  lignin  to  be  extracted  in- 
tact: a  method  akin  to  that  used  in 
the  production  of  Masonite  board 
explodes  wood  chips  by  high-pres- 
sure steam.  The  chips  are  then  en- 
zymaticaliy  broken  down  into  a 
sugar-rich  syrup  which  can  be  fed 
to  cattle;  and  into  a  lignin  residue. 
Lignin,  it  has  been  discovered,  is 
a  cheap  substitute  for  the  chemical 
phenol,  used  in  wood  glues.  Phenol 
recently  sold  for  35  or  36  cents  per 
pound;  lignin,  selling  for  23  or  24 
cents,  would  cost  7  or  8  cents  to 
produce,  says  Bungay. 

There  are  problems,  of  course. 
Enzymes  for  breaking  down  cel- 
lulose are  expensive,  making  en- 
zymatic refining  only  marginally 
economical  at  present.  And  when 
solvents  are  used  there  is  an  ab- 
sorption problem:  heating  or 
washing  pulpy  chemical  soups  to 
recover  expensive  solvents  adds  to 


production  costs.  But,  says  Bun- 
gay, solutions  to  these  problems  are 
near.  New  biological  techniques  are 
bringing  down  the  price  of  en- 
zymes, and  when  oil  prices  rise 
again,  biomass  conversion  will 
probably  be  profitable. 

On  the  pollution  front,  biomass 
conversion  looks  good.  Most  sys- 
tems are  closed,  meaning  very  lit- 
tle waste  is  emitted,  and  there  are 
few  by-products,  since  a  large  per- 
centage of  the  initial  biomass  feed- 
stock is  converted  into  the  end 
products.  Some  of  the  end  prod- 
ucts could  be  burned  to  fuel  the 
factory  itself. 

Bungay  believes  the  technology 
will  be  beneficial  to  other  branches 
of  the  chemical  industry  as  well. 
Coupled  with  the  pine  chemicals 
industry,  high-pressure  steam  ex- 
plosion might  produce  cheaper  and 
more  homogeneous  turpentines  and 
resins,  as  well  as  saleable  cellulose. 
But  Bungay  is  pessimistic  about  bi- 
omass ever  supplanting  fossil  fuels. 
"It  just  doesn't  make  sense,"  he 
says,  "to  build  a  fancy  modern  plant 
that  starts  with  a  feedstock  and 
makes  a  feedstock.  The  profit  on 
that  kind  of  venture  is  bound  to  be 
slim.  Biomass  has  a  much  better 
chance  of  competing  with  the  pe- 
troleum industry,  which  refines 
about  7  percent  of  its  stocks  into 
chemicals."  — GM 


while  walking  on  a  road  in  Lakeville,  Connecticut.  Her  voice 
wobbled  with  excitement  as  she  talked. 

She  had  been  reading  Reinhold  Niebuhr  on  community.  Nie- 
buhr  had  quoted  the  Christian  apostle  Paul,  who  said,  "All  men 
are  members  of  one  body."  Hubbard  had  gone  for  a  walk  and 
as  she  reflected  on  Paul's  idea,  she  suddenly  rose  out  of  herself 
and  saw  the  Earth  as  Gaia,  who  was  gasping  for  breath.  Hubbard 
felt  as  if  she  were  a  cell  in  the  body  of  earth.  Then  she  felt  the 
body  reaching  into  outer  space,  and  "with  that  common  focus, 
the  entire  world  began  to  link  together  in  total  joy  and  empathy. 
It  was  an  ecstatic  cosmic  event.  I  heard  the  statement,  'Our  story 
is  a  birth,'  and  then  there  was  what  I  call  a  planetary  smile.  I 
was  bathed  with  divine  light  and  surrounded  by  love."  Hubbard 
then  went  out  into  space  and  experienced  the  Big  Bang,  the 
formation  of  the  universe,  and  the  development  of  organisms 
from  single  cells  to  multiple-celled  beings.  Finally,  she  became 
"a  little  stick  figure  again,  walking  down  a  road  in  Lakeville, 
Connecticut."  Hubbard  said  she  realized  then  that  the  people  of 
the  earth  had  the  option  of  Armageddon  or  the  planetary  smile. 

A  British  fellow  stood  and  asked  Hubbard  if  music  had  been 
connected  with  her  vision,  because  he  thought  music  was  integral 
to  universal  harmony. 

"Yes!"  said  Hubbard.  There  had  been  tones  which  were  dis- 
harmonious, then  gradually  merged  into  harmony.  "Why  don't 
we  try  this?  Why  doesn't  everyone  here  make  their  tone,  and 
we'll  see  if  they  become  harmonious?  I  don't  have  a  strong  voice, 
but  I'll  begin."  Hubbard  made  her  tone  into  the  microphone, 
something  akin  to  A-flat  above  middle  C,  and  a  sizable  portion 
of  the  audience  made  their  tones,  and  lo  and  behold,  the  tones 
began  to  shift  before  our  very  ears.  A  bearded  man  rolled  his 
eyes  at  me,  and  we  both  pushed  our  way  toward  the  exit. 

Later,  a  75-year-old  man  from  Port  Jefferson,  Washington 
asked  me  if  I'd  been  to  the  mind-to-mind  session.  I  said  I  had. 
"Some  of  that  stuff  was  mind-boggling,  wasn't  it?"  he  said.  "It's 
a  good  thing  we  have  you  people  [the  press]  to  wade  through 
this  stuff  for  us,  because  it's  just  mind-boggling." 

I  wouldn't  have  said  it  was  that.  What  I  would  have  said  is 
that  it  was  religious.  Eagerness  to  believe  had  been  almost  palp- 
able in  that  ballroom.  What  was  going  on  there  had  little  to  do 
with  analysis,  much  to  do  with  faith. 

^^^^•s  futurists  talked  about  reassessment,  reevaluation, 
^L  reinvention,  I  still  wasn't  sure  what  values  were  to  be 
/  ^  embraced  when  the  old  were  abandoned.  Several  com- 
JL  JL  mon  threads  did  run  through  discussions:  that  it  was 
necessary  to  have  a  global  perspective ;  that  the  values  of  Western 
capitalism  and  Eastern  statism  which  permeate  the  world  are  the 
source  of  major  problems;  that  it  is  necessary  to  have  an  eco- 
logical point  of  view;  that  the  new  communications  technology 
can  help  bridge  the  gap  between  the  haves  and  have-nots. 

Fritjof  Capra,  author  of  The  Tao  of  Physics  and  (more  re- 
cently) The  Turning  Point:  Science,  Society,  and  the  Rising  Cul- 
ture, spoke  about  the  "crisis  of  the  Northern  hemisphere."  A 
physicist  at  the  Lawrence  Berkeley  Laboratory  of  the  University 
of  California,  Capra  argued  that  the  mechanistic  Newtonian- 
Cartesian  model  of  the  universe,  which  constitutes  the  basis  of 
most  people's  current  world  view,  is  outdated.  Its  "reductionist 
tendencies"  have  led  to  industrial  expansion  and  massive  ex- 
ploitation, he  said.  "We  have  overshot  the  mark  with  technology. 
We  need,  now,  to  shift  to  a  new  paradigm,  a  holistic  perspective 
that  will  allow  us  to  deal  with  complex,  inter-connected  problems 
in  a  non-linear  fashion."

We  exist,  in  short,  in  the  future 
of  literacy,  a  future  that  has  been 
developing  since  about  the  8th  cen- 
tury B.C.  Literacy,  being  a  tech- 
nology, welcomes  other  technolo- 
gies: in  the  15th  century  Gu- 
tenberg's, in  the  19th  Mergenthal- 
er's,  today  IBM's.  If  I  type  these 
lines  on  a  computer,  that  is  not 
strange.  The  book  has  no  rebuke 
for  ubiquitous,  facile,  machine-en- 
abled production;  the  book  may 
have  been  the  first  mass-produced 
article  after  the  brick.  (A  similarity 
ol  shape  has  been  remarked  on.) 
Keeping  James  Joyce  somewhat 
extravagantly  alive  was  part  of  the 
long  effort  to  preserve  Homer.  That 
task  was  shouldered  by  an  English 
Quaker  lady  named  Harriet 
Weaver.  She  felt  guilty  about  her 
inherited  means,  and  guilt  has  its 
ecological  niche,  as  has  literacy. 
External  to  its  provender  as  the  il- 
literate  imagination  can  never  be. 
the  literate  imagination  surrounds, 
cannibalizes,  rethinks.  Metempsy- 
chosis, that  is  its  normal  feat;  illit- 
eracy only  cherishes  and  repeats. 
No  bookless  bard  would  have  dared 
tio  to  Homer's  tale  what  Joyce  did. 
lest  attempting  it  he'd  wipe  out  the 
tale  he  started  from.  But  Joyce  was 
the  supremely  literate  man  of  our 
age;  for  his  like.  T.S.  Eliot  thought, 
vou'd  seek  back  as  far  as  Milton. 
So  it  came  about  that  the  Odyssey 
relives  in  Ulysses,  itself  unre- 
placed,  with  among  other  odd  con- 
sequences this,  that  students  have 
been  spurred  by  Ulysses  to  want  to 
learn  some  Greek.  (And  Joyce 
could  not  read  Homer  in  Cireek.) 
He  was  totally  unpredictable, 
which  helps  show  why  the  future 
is.  We  exist  in  the  spaces  between 
such  events:  events  in  a  continuum 
that  extends  from  our  first  abced- 
minded*  forefathers  and  will  not 
terminate  on  account  of  new  fash- 
ions in  hardware  because  its  habit 
of  externalizing  what  we  are  think- 
ing is  the  software  on  which  they 
thrive.

American  University,  added  that  development  is  different  from 
modernization.  Modernization  provides  technology;  develop- 
ment, on  the  other  hand,  is  an  open-ended  process  of  individual 
and  social  discovery.  "The  Saudis,"  said  Said,  "are  an  obscene

said,  that  events  do  not  happen,  but  are  discovered. 

Capra  and  Said  echoed  many  speakers.  On  the  subject  of 
ecological  perspective,  Russell  Peterson  had  said,  "We  have  to 
begin  to  respect  our  life-support  system.  The  thought  that  man 
doesn't  have  to  worry  about  the  air  he  breathes,  the  water  he 
drinks,  and  the  earth  he  gets  food  from  is  utter  folly."  And 
Rashmi  Mayur  insisted  that  the  global  diseases  can  only  be  erad- 
icated in  a  moral,  ethical  atmosphere  that  takes  into  account 
environment.  "We  are  living  on  a  planet  totally  interdependent 
and  fragile.  Its  maintenance  now  depends  on  us.  Since  there  are 
one  thousand  times  more  of  us  than  there  were  a  hundred  years 
ago,  our  responsibility  is  one  thousand  times  as  great." 

^^^^^■ayur.  wherever  I  heard  him  speak,  was  a  voice  of 

M  cogency  and  clarity.  And  he  is  a  difficult  person  to 
catch  up  with.  He  moved  about  the  halls  like  an  ex- 
cited atom. 

I  was  finally  able  to  arrange  to  see  him,  though,  after  he  had 
done  a  radio  interview.  Mayur  shook  my  hand  warmly,  even 
though  we  had  shaken  hands  four  times  in  the  previous  half  hour, 
and  we  gravitated  toward  a  sofa  in  the  hall.  I  asked  him  what 
he  thought  of  the  conference,  and  whether  he  had  noticed  how 
few  Third  Worlders  were  present.  "Oh  yes,"  he  replied.  "What 
do  I  think  of  the  conference?  I  think  Americans  are  very  myopic 
and  carried  away  by  technology  which  they  think  will  bring  them 
to  Shangri-la.  They  tend  to  miss  two  important  things. 

"First,  the  real  job  of  information  technology  is  providing 
proper  information.  What  is  going  to  be  put  into  the  system? 
That  depends  on  the  brains  using  it.  I  would  say  right  now  there 
is  information  pollution,  because  people  are  using  these  systems 
unwisely.  Second,  I  think  Americans  don't  realize  that  technol- 
ogy is  in  the  hands  of  the  few,  not  the  many.  The  globe  is  starved 
of  information  and  technology,  and  we  are  seeing  the  widening 
of  the  gap  between  information  poor  and  information  rich.  Three 
billion  people  in  the  world  have  no  access  at  all  to  this  type  of 
technology.  They  do  not  even  have  access  to  TV  sets." 

Does  Mayur  mean  that  the  Third  World  doesn't  need  the 
technology?  "No,"  he  said,  "I  am  simply  challenging  that  the 
medium  is  the  message.  The  Third  World  needs  the  message, 
and  it  is  media-starved,  but  it  is  only  the  message  that  validates 
the  technological  medium,  not  the  other  way  around." 

In  the  Third  World  right  now.  said  Mayur.  technology  is  used 
only  by  the  bureaucracy,  the  elite.  They  use  communications 
technology  to  perpetuate  their  power.  "They  do  not  let  people 
know  the  truth,  and  they  keep  their  image  before  the  public 
constantly."  What  Mayur  and  others  are  working  for  is  the  dis- 
semination of  information  and  technology  to  the  common  man, 
and  thus,  they  hope,  the  dissemination  of  power.  "We  want  the 
whole  world  to  have  access  to  technology,  and  we  want  the  planet 
to  be  united." 

What  if  those  with  power  try  to  resist  democratization?  "There 
arc  too  many  disenfranchised  people,"  answered  Mayur  simply. 
"You  cannot  put  the  lid  on  past  a  certain  point.  It  will  explode." 
He  warned,  "If  people  in  the  U.S.  do  not  recognize  the  rest  of 
the  world,  this  great  air-conditioned  boat  will  sink  in  the  sea  of 
the  world's  poverty.  We  must  join  with  people  to  bring  about  a 
better  world.  I  assure  you,  we  all  have  the  same  destiny."

teammates  had  not  seen  each  other  since  their 
50th  reunion  in  1977.

ming 500  miles  between  1971  and  1982.

Ed  Delano  placed  eighth  in  the  75-year-plus

his  category]  and  I  was  the  only  American  par- 
ticipating. Many  were  retired  pros,  who  are  still 
fast.  I  was  happy  to  come  in  eighth."  Earlier, 
Ed  had  competed  in  various  races  in  Hartberg, 
Austria.

Ray  Moeller: 

GE's  Former  Magic  Man 

He  had  absolutely  no  intention  of  taking 
a  job  with  General  Electric,  but  at  the 
insistance  of  the  Dean.  Ray  Moeller.  "35. 
reluctantly  took  part  in  a  GE  inter\ie\v 
during  his  senior  year  at  WPI.  "The  end 
result."  says  .Moeller.  who  is  now  retired, 
"was  31  years  of  the  most  thoroughly  en- 
joyable employment  possible. "" 

Ray.  GE  discovered,  excelled  in  the  area 
of  "special  assignments. ""  That  meant  he 
was  given  jobs  which  nobody  had  held 
and  which  nobody  held  after  him.  "A 
wonderful  arrangement.  .My  performance 
could  never  be  compared  with  that  of  any- 
one else!" 

On  one  special  assignment.  Ray  and 
five  other  GE  employees  were  asked  to 
predict  the  future  growth  of  everything 
electronic  in  nature — both  inside  and  out- 
side the  company — and  to  report  their 
findings.  They  set  up  office  in  New  York 
City  and  commuted  weekly  to  and  from 
their  homes  by  air.  living  in  a  different 
hotel  each  week.  At  year's  end.  they  took 
eight  hours  to  present  a  summary  of  their 
findings  to  GEs  top  management. 

When  GE  decided  to  use  Broadway  ac- 
tors and  actresses  in  a  nationwide  trav- 
eling road  show  to  teach  nontechnical  dis- 
tributors how  to  sell  technical  products  in 
an  easy-to-learn  fashion.  Ray  was  as- 
signed to  the  troupe.  He  wrote  his  own 
magician  and  \cntriloquist  acts  and  "felt

BORN:  to  Elisheva  and  Leo  Price  a  daughter, 
Shiry  Lee,  in  October  1981 .  In  June,  Price  joined 


the  HQ  VU  Corps  in  Stuttgart,  Germany,  where

Donald  Bean  has  been  named  director  of  sales 
and  marketing  at  Smith  Valve  Corp.  in  West- 
boro,  MA.  Previously,  he  was  vice  president 
and  general  manager  of  Kieley  and  Mueller, 
Inc.,  Middleton  (NY)  manufacturers  of  control 
valves.  He  also  had  product  and  sales  man- 
agement and  design  engineering  assignments 
with  Jamesbury  Corp.,  Worcester. 

David  Edfors,  a  project  engineer  for  Lane 
Construction  Corp.  of  Meriden,  CT,  has  re- 
cently been  concerned  with  the  building  of 
reinforced  earth-retaining  walls  for  the  final 
stretch  of  1-66  running  from  the  Washington, 
DC,  Capital  Beltway  east.  Experts  quoted  in 
the  magazine  ENR  estimated  that  using  the 
retaining  walls,  originally  suggested  by  Lane, 
saved  the  state  of  Virginia  about  $8  million  in 
construction  costs.  Dave  is  currently  project 
engineer  on  a  $64-million  project  with  Lane, 
building  the  western  approach  to  the  new  Fort 
McHenry  Tunnel  under  Baltimore  harbor.

1961 


Al  Sherman  was  a  coauthor  of  a  three-part  ar- 
ticle, "Comparison  of  direct  and  indirect  meth- 
ods of  measuring  arterial  blood  pressure."  Ap- 
pearing in  Medical  Instrumentation,  the  article 
was  named  the  best  paper  published  in  that 
journal  in  1981 .  Sherman  continues  at  Hewlett- 
Packard,  Waltham,  MA,  where  he  develops 
computer  systems  which  monitor  patients  in 
coronary  intensive  care  units.  He  received  his 
MS  from  WPI  in  October  of  1981,  "after  years 
of  procrastination." 

Paul  Sledzik  is  now  a  general  manager  for

at  WPI.

Dr.  Robert  Desmond  has  been  named  a  vice 
president  of  the  RIT  Research  Corp.,  a  wholly

for  two  years,  he  joined  RIT  in  1970,  was  named 
head  of  the  ME  department  in  1972  and  served 


The  Pied  Piper  of  Potomac 

The  Potomac  Almanac  of  Rockville,  MD, 
believes  that  any  man  who  gets  up  at  4:30 
a.m.  every  Thursday  morning  to  make 
breakfast  for  a  church  full  of  teenagers 
and  who  opens  his  own  house  to  100  young 
people  every  weekend  for  two  decades, 
deserves  special  recognition.  Last  June, 
the  newspaper  presented  Paul  Kokulis, 
'45,  with  its  first  Community  Service  Award. 

\yhy  does  the  successful  senior  partner 
of  the  Washington  law  firm  of  Cushman, 
Darby  &  Cushman.  a  devoted  husband 
and  father  of  four,  spend  so  much  time 
with  other  people's  children?  "It's  sim- 
ple," he  says.  "I  enjoy  them."  He  adds, 
"But,  I  must  admit  that  in  the  beginning, 
our  home  became  a  teen-age  open  house 
more  or  less  by  accident." 

In  the  early  196()s,  when  Kokulis  was 
new  to  the  Rockville  area  and  began 
teaching  Sunday  school  at  the  local  Pres- 
byterian Church,  his  pupils  started  drop- 
ping by  on  weekends.  They  usually  had 
no  money  to  spend  on  dates  and  liked 
being  in  a  home,  as  long  as  it  wasn't  theirs. 
"My  wife  and  I  called  our  place  the  house 
of  last  resort." 

The  Sunday  school  class  spread  the  good 
word  about  the  Kokulis  hospitality,  the 
impromptu  games  and  the  "brass  tacks" 
conversation.  The  weekend  gatherings 
grew. 

Says  a  neighbor,  who  characterizes  the 
Kokulis  get-togethers  as  very  quiet,  "Paul 
is  an  oasis.  He  provides  a  role  model  and 


Host  of  the  "House  of  Last  Resort" 

a  listening  ear.  We  all  try  to  live  up  to  his 
example." 

Over  the  years,  there  have  been  few 
problems  aside  from  a  broken  window  and 
an  accidental  hot-chocolate  spill  on  a 
brand-new  carpet.  (Not  a  bad  record, 
considering  that  six  active  Kokulis  "alum- 
ni" ended  up  playing  football  in  the  NFL!) 
In  spite  of  spills  and  general  wear  and 
tear,  Mrs.  Kokulis  enthusiastically  backs 
up  her  husband's  efforts. 

Paul  and  Carolyn  Kokulis  care  more 
about  the  quality  of  life  of  the  local  teen- 
agers than  about  the  lifespan  of  their  fur- 
niture. 


as  director  of  RITs  Institute  for  applied  En- 
ergy Studies  until  1980.  Formerlv.  he  taught  at 
WPI  and  Clarkson. 

Allen  Hoffman,  associate  professor  of  me- 
chanical engineering  at  WPI,  took  first  place 
in  the  "Over  40"  category  of  the  Hubbardston 
Field  Day  road  race  in  September.  Placing  ninth 
overall,  his  winning  time  was  28:54  for  the  5.2- 
mile  run. 

Francis  Kennedy,  Jr.,  was  coauthor  of  "Sin- 
gle-Pass Rub  Testing  of  Abradable  Seal  Ma- 
terials" in  the  September  issue  of  Lubrication 
Engineering.  Dr.  Kennedy  has  an  MS  from 
Stanford  University  and  a  PhD  from  RPI.  He 
carried  out  postdoctoral  research  in  tribology 
at  Polytechnic  Institute  of  Bucharest,  Ro- 
mania.'in  1973,  at  RPI  from  1973  to  1974  and 
at  NASA-Lewis  Research  Center  during  the 
summers  of  1977  and  1978.  Since  1974,  he  has 
been  at  Dartmouth  College,  teaching  and  su- 
pervising students  in  mechanical  engineering 
and  doing  research  in  solid  mechanics  and  tri- 
bology.

water  off  Majorca,  Spain.

Ronald  Jones,  his  brother-in-law  and  brother 
have  helped  his  father.  Joseph,  build  an  electric 
car  from  a  fiberglass  replica  of  a  1952  MG  that 
comes  in  a  kit  form.  The  car.  built  on  a  1968 
VW  chassis,  weighs  2,660  pounds  and  can  go 
from  0  to  30  miles  an  hour  in  eight  seconds. 
At  an  average  driving  speed  of  40  miles  an 
hour,  it  can  go  65  miles  before  the  batteries 
have  to  be  recharged.  Reportedly,  the  Jones 
car  has  a  light  hum  as  it  travels  along  the  high- 
ways in  West  Hartford.  CT. 

Mahendra  Patel  has  been  elected  vice  pres- 
ident of  the  Engineering  Societies  of  New  Eng- 
land (ESNE).  A  former  ESNE  treasurer,  he  is 
also  a  professional  engineer  and  a  senior  me- 
chanical engineer  in  the  engineering,  planning 
and  research  department  of  the  Boston  Edison 
Company.  He  has  been  associated  with  fossil- 
fueled  power  plants  since  1969  and  has  been 
involved  with  air  pollution  studies  and  envi- 
ronmental regulations.  He  is  currently  the 
project  manager  for  a  $20-million  Boston  Serv- 
ice Center  facility  under  construction  in  Dor- 
chester. 

V.  J.  Shah  continues  as  principal  engineer 
at  Gilbert  Commonwealth  in  Jackson,  MI. 

Thomas  Starr  has  been  elected  chairman  of 
the  800-member  Boston  section  of  the  Amer- 
ican Institute  of  Chemical  Engineers.  He  has 
an  MBA  from  Rutgers  and  is  currently  super- 
visor of  process  engineering  for  Koch  Process 
Systems,  Inc.,  Westboro,  MA.  Starr,  his  wife.

Have  You  Seen  This  Man? 

Fun  with  Dick  and  Jane 
and  Pete  and  Tillie 

The  next  time  you  settle  down  for  an  eve- 
ning of  TV  and  suddenly  think  you've 
spotted  a  familiar  face  on  the  screen,  you 
probably  have.  The  face  could  well  belong 
to  Robert  Lussier,  '56,  who  during  the 
past  25  years  has  appeared  in  dozens  of 
TV  shows  and  more  than  200  commer- 
cials. 

If  you  thought  you'd  caught  him  in  the 
movies,  that  would  be  true,  too.  There 
have  been  16  movies,  as  well  as  numerous 
stock  productions  and  off-Broadway  plays. 


Some  of  Bob's  latest  television  credits 
are  The  Gambler,  Herbie,  Hart  to  Hart 
and  The  Dukes  of  Hazzard.  He  also  ap- 
peared in  Salem's  Lot,  The  Sam  Sheppard 
Murder  Case  and  Sandcastles.  His  TV 
specials  include  Cricket  on  the  Hearth, 
Hellzapoppin.  Pinocchio  and  Jack  and 
the  Beanstalk.  He  worked  in  the  pilots  of 
Housecalls  and  The  Practice. 

Among  his  feature  films  are  Love  at 
First  Bite,  High  Anxiety,  Fun  With  Dick 
and  Jane,  Mother.  Jugs  and  Speed,  Air- 
port '75,  Pete  and  Tillie  and  The  Killing 
of  Sister  George. 

Bob  did  a  hitch  in  the  Navy  after  at- 
tending WPI  and  Brown.  A  pianist,  singer 
and  nightclub  performer  since  age  12,  he 
later  studied  classical  music,  and,  as  a 
member  of  the  Peloquin  Chorale,  taped 
several  network  TV  specials. 

He  says,  "I  began  my  acting  career  in 
community  theatre.  One  of  my  early  tours 
was  with  the  national  company  of  The  Odd 
Couple  with  George  Gobel."  His  first  work 
in  Los  Angeles  was  in  That  Girl.  Later  he 
appeared  in  TV  oldies:  Get  Smart,  Bon- 
anza, Owen  Marshall  and  Room  222. 

Bob  serves  as  musical  director  for  his 
parish  in  Hollywood  and  has  conducted 
an  acting  workshop  at  a  penitentiary.  He 
was  affiliated  with  the  Los  Angeles  Rep- 
ertory Company  and  for  ten  years  he  has 
been  with  Theatre  East,  serving  on  the 
board  of  directors  for  two  years.  "I  need 
constant  involvement  in  theatre."  he  says.

36        WPI   JOURNAL 


Paul  Cleary 

Wins  Writing  Awards 

Paul  Cleary,  71,  an  editorial  writer  for 
the  Tulsa  World,  has  received  two  awards 
in  the  Associated  Press  newswriting  com- 
petition for  Oklahoma.  He  won  first  place 
for  newspaper  column  writing  and  second 
place  for  public  service  reporting. 

The  columns  included  analyses  of  U.S. 
House  Budget  Committee  Chairman  James 
Jones's  battle  over  the  Reagan  administra- 
tion's budget  proposal,  the  campaign  by 
the  National  Conservative  Political  Action 
Committee  to  unseat  Jones,  and  the 
constitutionality  of  executing  juvenile 
criminals. 

With  the  World  for  five  years,  Cleary 
placed  second  in  public-service  reporting 
for  an  article  on  loopholes  in  the  state's 
drunken-driving  laws.  The  story  led  to  an 
overhaul  of  the  Oklahoma  statutes. 

After  graduating  from  WPI  with  a 
degree  in  Humanities  and  Technology  (En- 
glish),  Cleary  served  as  a  regional  reporter 
for  the  Worcester  Telegram  &  Gazette  in 
Clinton,  Southbridge  and  Webster,  MA, 
and  as  a  member  of  the  city  news  staff. 
Later,  he  was  a  visiting  professor  in 
English  and  an  associate  project  adminis- 
trator at  WPI.  He  also  served  on  the  WPI 


His  articles  mold  public  opinion. 

Student  Alumni  Relations  Committee. 
He  holds  a  law  degree  from  the  University 
of  Tulsa  and  is  a  member  of  the  Oklahoma 
bar. 

Cleary  joined  the  Tulsa  World  as  a  re- 
porter while  attending  the  University  of 
Tulsa.  After  receiving  his  degree,  he  be- 
came political  editor  until  named  editorial 
writer  in  July  1981.  His  wife,  Julia  Ann, 
also  a  writer,  graduated  from  the  Uni- 
versity of  Missouri  Journalism  School.

COMPLETED  CAREERS 


Kenneth  F.  Burns,  former  crew  coach  at  WPI, 
died  in  Worcester  on  August  4,  1982,  at  the 
age  of  77. 

One  of  the  Worcester  area's  most  accom- 
plished rowing  enthusiasts.  Burns  was  instru- 
mental in  bringing  the  Olympic  crew  trials  to 
Lake  Quinsigamond  in  1932  and  1952  and  in 
making  the  intercollegiate  Eastern  Sprints  re- 
gatta a  regular  event  at  the  lake  for  many  years. 
In  1952,  he  was  a  member  of  the  official  staff 
at  the  Olympic  Games  held  in  Helsinki. 

Burns  captured  the  New  England  novice 
championship  on  Boston's  Charles  River  in  1922. 
He  was  a  longtime  crew  coach  at  Shrewsbury 
(MA)  High  School,  and  his  teams  won  more 
than  eight  national  crowns  and  two  New  Eng- 
land titles.  He  was  a  former  coach  at  St.  John's 
High  School  and  WPI.  In  1962,  he  was  named 
to  the  Helms  Rowing  Hall  of  Fame 

For  23  years  he  was  the  chief  law  enforce- 
ment officer  in  Shrewsbury.  A  past  president 
of  the  Massachusetts  Police  Chiefs'  Associa- 
tion, he  had  also  served  as  a  member  of  the 
state  Division  of  Fisheries  and  Game. 

Burns  received  citations  and  resolutions  from 
the  Massachusetts  State  House  of  Represen- 
tatives and  Senate  in  recognition  of  his  accom- 
plishments in  the  fields  of  boating,  hunting  and 
conservation.  A  native  of  Chelsea,  MA,  he 
graduated  from  North  High  School  in  Worces- 
ter. 

Henry  J.  Potter,  '03,  a  former  safety  engineer 
and  one  of  WPI's  oldest  living  alumni,  passed 
away  on  September  25,  1982,  in  Hyannis,  MA, 
following  a  long  illness.  He  was  101  years  old. 

He  was  born  in  Webster,  MA,  on  February 
22,  1881.  He  graduated  as  a  mechanical  engi- 
neer from  WPI.  He  had  been  affiliated  with 
the  Draper  Co.  and  the  Travelers  Insurance 
Co.  From  1914  to  1949  he  was  with  the  Hart- 
ford Accident  and  Indemnity  Co.  (now  the 
Hartford  Insurance  Group),  from  which  he  re- 
tired as  supervising  engineer. 

A  former  member  of  the  ASME  and  the 
American  Society  of  Safety  Engineers,  he  also 
belonged  to  the  Masons  and  the  Dukes  County 
Historical  Society,  Edgartown.  He  had  been  a 
deacon  in  the  Congregational  Church  and  a 
member  of  the  Methodist  Church. 

Mr.  Potter  served  with  the  Connecticut  State 
National  Guard  in  World  War  I. 


Millard  F.  Clement,  '10,  died  in  the  Holyoke 
(MA)  Soldiers'  Home  on  September  15,  1982. 
He  was  95. 

Born  on  August  28,  1887,  in  Bethlehem,  NH, 
in  1910  he  received  his  BSEE  from  WPI  and 
in  1912  his  professional  EE  degree.  During  his 
career  he  was  with  the  New  York,  New  Haven 
&  Hartford  Railroad,  the  Pennsylvania  Rail- 
road, McGraw  Hill  Publishers  and  Chas.  H. 
Tenney,  Engineers.  From  1924  until  1957,  when 
he  retired  as  vice  president,  he  was  employed 
by  Orange  and  Rockland  Utilities,  Middle- 
town,  NY. 

A  veteran  of  World  War  I,  he  served  aboard 
the  USS  New  York  in  the  North  Sea  and  later 
retired  as  a  lieutenant  commander  in  the  USNR. 
He  obtained  the  32nd  degree  Scottish  rites  as 
a  Mason  while  in  Scotland  in  World  War  I.  He 
received  the  Silver  Beaver  award  for  his  many 
services  to  Scouting.  He  belonged  to  Theta  Chi, 
the  Rotary  Club  and  the  Baptist  Church.  In 
1978,  he  received  the  Gold  Cane  Award  as  the 
oldest  male  resident  of  Granby,  MA. 

Mr.  Clement  had  been  a  member  of  the  New 
York  State  and  National  Society  of  Profes- 
sional Engineers,  the  American  Gas  Associa- 
tion, the  American  Legion  and  the  Middletown 
University  Club. 

Cedric  E.  Thurston,  '15,  died  on  July  29,  1982, 
in  Springfield,  MA.  He  was  89. 

After  studying  electrical  engineering  at  WPI 
and  MIT,  Mr.  Thurston  was  associated  with 
the  coal  industry  in  western  Pennsylvania  and 
West  Virginia  from  1922  to  1959,  when  he  re- 
tired. 

He  was  an  Army  veteran  of  World  War  I,  a 
member  of  ATO  and  a  former  church  deacon. 
A  native  of  Rumford,  ME,  he  was  born  on 
August  22,  1892. 

Oakley  C.  Walker,  '18,  formerly  with  Norton 
Co.,  died  in  St.  Vincent  Hospital,  Worcester, 
on  August  22,  1982,  at  the  age  of  88. 

A  Worcester  native,  he  was  born  on  June 
19,  1894,  and  later  was  a  student  at  WPI.  He 
graduated  from  the  University  of  Michigan 
School  of  Engineering. 

Mr.  Walker  was  with  Norton  Co.,  Worces- 
ter, for  47  years,  retiring  in  1959.  He  belonged 
to  SAE  and  the  Episcopal  Church  and  served 
in  the  Army  Air  Service  in  World  War  I. 


bridge-Palmer  district.  Previously,  he  had  been 
manager  of  the  Attleboro  office  and  district 
manager  of  the  Marlboro  office.  Steen  started 
at  Mass.  Electric  in  1961.  In  1975,  he  was  named 
assistant  director  of  consumer  services  for  New 
England  Electric,  the  parent  company  of  Mass.

Rhode  Island.

Susan  Fontaine,  '82,  a  science  teacher  at  Mar- 
ianapolis  Prep  School,  Thompson,  CT,  is  also

Dr.  Frederic  R.  Butler,  '20,  professor  emeritus 
of  chemistry  at  WPI,  died  on  July  15,  1982,  in 
St.  Vincent  Hospital,  Worcester. 

Born  in  Worcester  on  May  26,  1899,  he  re- 
ceived his  MS  from  WPI  in  1922  and  his  PhD 
from  Harvard  in  1925.  He  taught  at  the  Uni- 
versity of  Massachusetts  for  two  years  and  served 
on  the  WPI  faculty  from  1927  until  1965.  He 
was  a  former  moderator  of  the  Newman  Club 
at  WPI,  as  well  as  golf  club  coach. 

Dr.  Butler  belonged  to  Sigma  Xi,  Tau  Beta 
Pi,  Phi  Kappa  Theta,  the  Tech  Old-Timers  and 
the  American  Chemical  Society.  He  was  a  for- 
mer member  of  the  Worcester  Planning  Board 
and  a  past  president  of  the  Worcester  Engi- 
neering Society.  He  had  served  on  the  Central 
Massachusetts  Regional  Planning  Commission 
and  the  Comprehensive  Health  Planning  Com- 
mission. 

A  member  of  the  board  of  directors  at  the 
Research  Foundation  of  St.  Vincent  Hospital, 
he  was  also  a  charter  member  and  past  presi- 
dent of  the  Worcester  Chemistry  Club.  An  avid 
tennis  player,  he  belonged  to  the  Worcester 
Tennis  Club. 

Warren  A.  Ellsworth,  '22,  retired  associate 
treasurer  and  assistant  secretary  of  the  Paul 
Revere  Life  Insurance  Co.,  died  at  his  home 
in  Palm  Beach,  FL,  on  October  7,  1982. 

He  was  born  in  Worcester  on  April  30,  1901 , 
and  received  his  BSME  from  WPI.  From  1924 
to  1967,  when  he  retired,  he  was  with  the  Paul 
Revere  Life  Insurance  Company  (Mass.  Pro- 
tective Association,  Inc.),  Worcester.  At  the 
time  of  his  retirement,  he  was  associate  trea- 
surer and  assistant  secretary  of  the  company. 

Besides  serving  as  director  of  his  company 
and  of  the  Paul  Revere  Variable  Annuity  In- 
surance Company,  Mr.  Ellsworth  had  been  a 
past  president  of  the  University  Club  and  past 
commander  of  the  Worcester  County  Power 
Squadron.  He  was  a  trustee  of  the  Worcester 
Natural  History  Society,  the  Hillside  School  of 
Marlboro  and  the  Massachusetts  4-H  Foun- 
dation and  a  past  trustee  of  the  Worcester 
Foundation  for  Experimental  Biology.  In  1972, 
he  received  an  honorary  doctorate  from  As- 
sumption College. 

Mr.  Ellsworth  was  a  member  of  the  Masons, 
the  Shrine,  the  Worcester  Club  and  the  Tat- 
nuck  and  Worcester  country  clubs.  He  was  a

former  member  of  the  President's  Advisory 
Council  at  WPI.  His  son.  Warren  A.  Ellsworth, 
Jr.,  graduated  from  WPI  in  1952. 

Enfried  T.  (Larry)  Larson,  '22,  of  Worcester 
was  killed  in  an  automobile  accident  in  Brook- 
field.  VT.  on  July  24.  while  returning  from  a 
family  reunion.  He  was  81. 

Larry  was  born  on  October  8.  1900.  in 
Worcester.  After  recei\ing  his  BSME  from  WPL 
he  was  a  sales  engineer  at  Norton  Co. .  Worces- 
ter, where  he  worked  for  43  years,  retiring  in 
1965.  From  1952  to  1965.  he  was  an  editor  of 
Grits  and  Grinds,  a  Norton  technical  publica- 
tion. 

Long  active  in  WPI  alumni  affairs.  Larry  was 
chairman  of  the  60th  class  reunion  last  June 
and  had  served  on  the  publications  committee 
and  as  a  former  president  and  program  chair- 
man for  the  Tech  Old-Timers.  He  belonged  to 
Theta  Chi  fraternity,  the  Masons,  Knights 
Templar,  the  American  Society  of  Tool  and 
Manufacturing  Engineers,  the  Massachusetts 
Industrial  Editors  Association  and  the  Society 
of  Professional  Engineers.  He  was  a  member 
of  the  Worcester  County  Mechanics  Associa- 
tion, the  Swedish  Charitable  Association.  Trin- 
ity Lutheran  Church  and  the  Norton  Co.  25- 
Year  Club. 

Stanley  F.  Johnson,  '26,  of  Oakland.  CA,  died 
on  July  15.  1982.  following  several  strokes  and 
a  heart  attack.  He  was  79  years  old. 

Mr.  Johnson  graduated  as  a  civil  engineer 
from  WPI.  He  had  been  a  consultant  for  In- 
dustrial Risk  Insurers.  During  World  War  II. 
his  specialty  was  fire  safety. 

After  retiring  14  years  ago.  he  went  to  Mer- 
ritl  College  and  the  University  of  California. 
where  he  studied  computer  systems.  Later  he 
designed  fire-extinguishing  systems  for  nuclear 
plants  and  nuclear-powered  vessels. 

Mr.  Johnson  belonged  to  Phi  Sigma  Kappa, 
Skull,  the  Poly  Club,  the  Masons,  the  May- 
flower Society,  the  Sons  of  the  American  Rev- 
olution, the  Massachusetts  Historic  and  Ge- 
nealogical Society  and  several  professional 
engineering  groups.  Born  in  Worcester,  he  was 
related  to  three  of  the  first  colonial  governors 
and  to  Francis  Cook,  who  came  to  Massachu- 
setts on  the  Mayflower. 

Frank  C.  Taylor,  '28,  passed  away  suddenly 
on  September  6.  1982.  in  Camden,  NJ. 

A  native  of  West  Sprmgficld.  MA.  he  was 
born  on  November  27.  1904.  He  graduated  as 
an  electrical  engmccr  and  was  with  Mutual 
Benefit  Life  Insurance  Co.,  Newark,  for  26 
years  before  retiring  in  1976  as  assistant  sec- 
retary-treasurer. 

Mr.  Taylor  was  a  past  president  and  trustee 
of  the  West  Essex  First  Aid  Squad  and  a  mem- 
ber of  the  Mortgage  Bankers  Association  of 
Newark  and  Phi  Sigma  Kappa 

Eilman  R.  .Smith,  '30,  a  longtime  manager  of 
Union  .Mills  Paper  .Manufacturing  Co.,  died  in 
Hyannis,  MA,  on  August  8.  1982. 

A  native  of  Norwood.  MA,  he  was  born  on 
October  5,  1907.  Graduating  as  a  mechanical 
engineer,  he  belonged  to  Ihcta  Chi  From  \9^^ 
to  1941,  he  was  with  Hollingsworth  &  Vose 
Co.  He  joined  Union  Mills  of  New  Hope,  PA. 
in  1941,  staving  with  the  firm  until  he  retired 
m  1970. 

He  had  served  as  a  member  of  his  local  board 


of  education,  as  a  past  master  of  a  Masonic 
lodge  and  as  an  elder  of  the  Presbyterian  Church. 
Later,  he  was  a  member  of  the  administrative 
board,  finance  committee  and  choir  of  the 
Eastham  United  .Methodist  Church. 

He  belonged  to  the  Technical  Association  of 
the  Pulp  and  Paper  Industry  and  to  the  Paper 
Industry  Management  Association. 

Allan  F.  Hardy,  Jr.,  '35,  class  vice  president 
and  a  retired  vice  president  and  director  of 
Norton  Co..  Worcester,  died  on  July  19.  1982. 
at  his  home  in  Chatham.  MA.  at  the  age  of  69. 

In  1935,  Mr.  Hardy  began  his  career  at  Nor- 
ton in  the  manufacturing  control  department. 
Later,  after  serving  as  plants  engineer,  he  was 
named  general  manager  of  the  Norton  Refrac- 
tories Division  in  1961.  He  was  elected  vice 
president  in  1962  and  director  of  Norton  Co. 
in  1963.  In  1965,  he  became  executive  vice 
president  and  director  of  National  Research 
Corp..  Newton,  a  Norton  subsidiary.  Subse- 
quently, he  was  named  president. 

Associated  with  the  Guardian  National  In- 
spection and  Warantee  Corp.  of  Orleans.  Mr. 
Hardy,  a  professional  engineer,  was  also  an 
executive  vice  president  of  the  San-Vel  Con- 
crete Co.  of  Littleton  from  1972  to  1975.  He 
had  been  president  of  Hardy  Contractors  of 
Princeton  and  of  Allan  Hardy  Assoc.  Weston. 

He  received  his  BS  in  chemistry  and  then  did 
postgraduate  work  at  the  Harvard  Graduate 
School  of  Business  and  the  University  of  Iowa. 
He  belonged  to  Theta  Chi  and  the  Congrega- 
tional Church. 

Mr.  Hardy  was  active  in  WPI  affairs,  serving 
as  a  former  vice  president  of  the  Alumni  As- 
sociation, as  an  Alumni  Council  representative 
from  Worcester,  as  a  Fund  Board  member  and 
as  vice  president  of  the  WPI  Executive  Com- 
mittee. 

A  Worcester  native,  he  had  held  leadership 
roles  with  Norton  Machines,  Ltd..  the  Mas- 
sachusetts Taxpayers  Association.  Holden  Dis- 
trict Hospital.  Worcester  Community  Chest, 
the  Kiwanis  Club,  the  Red  Cross  and  the  Greater 
Boston  Chamber  of  Commerce.  He  and  his 
wife  co-founded  the  New-Comers  of  Chatham. 

John  J.  Molloy,  '35,  died  of  cancer  on  August 
13,  1982,  at  his  home  in  Tucson,  AZ.  He  was 
born  in  Worcester  on  January  27,  1915. 

A  graduate  civil  engineer,  he  retired  in  De- 
cember 1973  after  37  years  with  the  U.S.  Geo- 
logical Survey.  His  career  with  the  agency  took 
him  to  several  states,  culminating  with  a  post 
as  district  chief  of  the  Columbus,  OH.  office. 

During  World  War  II,  he  was  a  U.S.  Army 
officer  stationed  in  the  South  Pacific;  he  retired 
as  a  colonel  in  the  U.S.  Army  Reserve  in  1968. 

A  longtime  member  of  the  ASCE,  he  was  a 
former  president  for  two  different  sections.  John 
also  belonged  to  the  New  York  Stale  Society 
of  Professional  Engineers,  the  American  Le- 
gion and  Phi  Kappa  Theta. 

George  H.  Perry,  '35,  a  former  manager  of 
finance  for  Warner  &  Swasey  Co.,  Grinding 
Machine  Division,  Worcester,  died  in  Cape  Cod 
Regency  Nursing  Home,  Ccnterville,  MA.  on 
September  12,  1982.  He  was  69. 

He  graduated  from  WPIs  School  of  Indus- 
trial Management  in  1956  and  also  graduated 
from  Harvard  University  School  ol  Industrial 
Management  and  New  England  School  of  Ac- 
counting. From  1935  to  1941  he  worked  for  the 


City  of  Worcester.  In  1941  he  joined  Norton 
Co.,  where  he  served  as  a  supervisor.  He  was 
named  manager  of  finance  for  Warner  &  Swa- 
sey Co.,  Grinding  Machine  Division  in  1972. 
Mr.  Perry  was  a  master  sergeant  in  the  Army 
Corps  of  Engineers  in  World  War  II.  He  was 
a  past  director  of  the  New  England  Chapter  of 
the  National  Association  of  Accountants  and 
a  past  president,  vice  president  and  treasurer 
of  the  association's  Worcester  chapter.  He  had 
been  district  director  of  the  New  England  Dis- 
trict Collie  Club  of  America.  Inc. ,  president  of 
the  Associated  Collie  Club  of  Northeastern 
United  States,  president  of  Wachusett  Kennel 
Club,  Inc.,  a  licensed  judge  for  the  American 
Kennel  Club  and  a  member  of  Cape  Cod  Ken- 
nel Club.  He  belonged  to  the  Congregational 
Church  and  SAE. 

Roy  O.  Swenson,  '35,  a  retired  senior  engineer 
from  Norton  Co.,  Worcester,  died  on  July  31, 
1982.  at  his  home  in  Holden,  MA. 

He  was  born  on  August  31,  1912,  in  Worces- 
ter. Graduated  as  a  mechanical  engineer,  dur- 
ing his  career  he  was  with  Alden  Hydraulic 
Laboratory  and  Cragin  &  Wilkins.  Formerly 
he  owned  and  operated  Swenson  Engineering. 
He  was  with  Norton  Co.  for  40  years. 

Mr.  Swenson  belonged  to  ATO,  the  Poly 
Club  and  the  Tech  Old-Timers.  as  well  as  the 
Worcester  Chemists  Club,  the  Masons,  the 
Eastern  Star,  the  Commandery  and  the  Mas- 
sachusetts Society  of  Professional  Engineers. 
He  was  an  avid  sportsman  and  had  traveled 
throughout  South  Africa  on  safari,  as  well  as 
on  Norton  Co.  business.  He  had  also  toured 
Scandinavia. 

Frederick  A.  Anderson,  '42,  a  professor  of  me- 
chanical engineering  at  WPI  for  many  years, 
died  in  Worcester  on  October  11,  1982. 

He  graduated  as  a  mechanical  engineer  and 
was  appointed  an  instructor  at  WPI,  beginning 
a  40-year  teaching  career.  In  1953,  he  received 
his  MSME  and  in  1963  he  was  appointed  a  full 
professor.  In  1974,  he  taught  for  a  term  at  the 
City  University  of  London  under  WPI's  ex- 
change program  with  that  university. 

In  1956,  Professor  Anderson  became  the  first 
coordinator  of  the  Evening  Graduate  Study 
Program,  a  post  he  held  until  he  served  on  the 
faculty  of  the  U.S.  Naval  Academy  during  a 
leave  of  absence  in  1959  and  1960.  He  did  sum- 
mer work  at  Cornell.  Purdue,  the  University 
of  Colorado,  Stanford,  MIT  and  Northeastern. 

A  native  of  Worcester,  he  was  born  on  March 
3,  1920.  He  belonged  to  the  Wesley  Methodist 
Church,  where  he  was  a  teacher  in  the  Home 
Planners  Class.  A  registered  professional  en- 
gineer in  Massachu.setts,  he  was  also  a  member 
of  Sigma  Xi,  the  American  Society  for  Engi- 
neering Education,  the  American  Society  for 
Testing  Materials  and  the  ASME. 

In  World  War  11,  he  served  with  the  U.S. 
Navy  and  was  captain  of  a  minesweeper  in  the 
Pacific.  He  joined  the  Naval  Reserve  after  the 
war,  later  retiring  as  a  lieutenant  commander. 

David  P.  Gracie,  '74,  who  graduated  with  a 
degree  in  business,  died  in  San  Francisco,  CA, 
on  September  10.  1982,  following  a  brief  ill- 
ness. He  was  M)  years  old. 

At  the  time  of  his  death,  he  was  chief  systems 
programmer  and  analyst  for  the  Levi  Strauss 
Co.  A  native  of  New  Bedford.  MA,  he  be- 
longed to  AEP  and  the  Baptist  Church.

Poly  Club  Installs 

WPI  Athletic  Hall  of  Fame 

In  late  January.  Poly  Club  and  WPI  of- 
ficials put  the  finishing  touches  on  an  idea 
which  has  been  nearly  ten  years  in  the 
making.  The  goal?  To  formally  recognize 
outstanding  individuals  who  have  be- 
stowed honor  and  recognition  upon  them- 
selves and  the  college  as  a  result  of  their 
involvement  in  WPI  athletics.  The  idea? 
WPI's  brand-new  Athletic  Hall  of  Fame, 
which  is  now  prepared  to  accept  nomi- 
nations for  its  first  members. 

Selection  of  members  will  be  based  on 
their  athletic  ability,  integrity,  sportsman- 
ship and  character,  with  special  emphasis 
on  the  athlete's  contribution  to  WPI  and 
to  the  teams  on  which  he  or  she  played. 
In  addition,  membership  may  also  be  of- 
fered to  non-athletes  who  have  provided 
distinguished  service  to  WPI  athletics — 
for  example,  coaches,  staff,  faculty,  and 
alumni. 

Know  of  a  deserving  member  of  the 
WPI  community — now  or  from  the  past? 
Why  not  take  a  few  minutes  to  complete 
the  nomination  form.  Please  provide  as 
much  information  as  you  can.  The  nom- 
inee need  not  be  living  to  be  eligible,  nor 
must  he  or  she  be  a  WPI  graduate.  Just 
one  condition:  Athletes  are  eligible  for 
nomination  no  sooner  than  five  years  after 
their  class  graduates,  while  coaches,  staff, 
faculty,  etc..  are  eligible  at  any  time.

regular  season  16-2-1 .  rankinfi  I6th  in 
the  nation.  The  team  placed  third  in  the 
New  England  College  Conference  Wres- 
tling Association  championships. 

Above  right:  Kim  Fay.  'H6.  of  Na- 
shua, NH.  Women  s  basketball  compiled 
a  20-5  .season  and  won  the  Eastern  Col- 
legiate Athletic  Conference  North  Wom- 
en's Division  III  champufnship.  Quite  a 
showing  all  around!
