APRIL  1983        1 


The  Many  Sides  of 
Financing  College  Costs 
with  Wisdom 


For  parents  of  college-bound  children,  meeting  skyrocketing  costs 
can  be  tough.  A  leading  tax  expert  offers  no-nonsense, 
ambitious  strategies  for  financing  college,  reducing  your  taxes — and 
keeping  the  IRS  off  your  back. 


by  Alexander  A.  Bove,  Jr. 


As  we  go  to  press.  President  Reagan  has 
just  proposed  to  Congress  legislation  to 
establish  Educational  Savings  Accounts 
(ESAs).  Similar  to  Individual  Retirement 
Accounts  (IRAs)  in  terms  of  their  tax 
benefits,  ESAs  are  designed  to  encourage 
saving  and  thus  to  help  savers  finance 
their  children's  college  education. 

But  while  Mr.  Reagan's  idea  is  a  step 
in  the  right  direction,  many  educational 
groups  believe  that  the  proposed  legisla- 
tion would  stop  far  short  of  offering  real 
relief  to  weary  taxpayers.  For  example, 
the  National  Association  of  Independent 
Colleges  and  Universities  (NAICU)  and 
others  support  the  concept  behind  the 
Reagan  bill,  but  have  publicly  called  for 
greater  flexibility  and  additional  tax  ben- 
efits. 

Our  space  does  not  allow  a  detailed 
discussion  of  this  issue.  But  one  thing  is 
certain:  the  ESA  proposal  will  be  de- 
bated in  this  session  of  Congress.  More- 
over, in  some  form,  it  stands  a  good 
chance  of  passage.  And,  to  he  sure,  the 
popular  press  will  give  full  coverage  to 
the  ongoing,  urgent  debate  over  tax-fa- 
vored fmancmg  of  college  costs.  We  urge 
you  to  remain  abreast  of  developments 
as  they  occur. 

Tax -favored  financing  of  college  costs, 
like  any  financial  strategy,  can  be  techni- 
cally complex  and  may  present  pitfalls 
for  the  unwary.  We  advise  the  reader  to 
consult  his  or  her  professional  tax  ad- 
visor before  engaging  m  any  aggressive 
financial  maneuver. 


Quotations  are  wonderful  things. 
They  can  convey  the  wit  and  wis- 
dom of  the  ages  in  a  sentence  or 
two,  and  some  Hve  on  virtually  forever. 
With  any  piece  of  wit  or  wisdom,  how- 
ever, there  can  be  many  sides.  For  ex 
ample,  someone  once  said:  "Education  is 
what  you  have  left  over  after  you've 
learned."  Here's  my  definition: 

"Education  is  a  financial  transfusion 
from  you,  the  parent,  to  the  college,  where 
practically  nothing  is  left  over  except  the 
hope  that  your  child  will  appreciate  the 
sacrifices  you  made  and  will  use  the  ed- 
ucation you  provided." 

But  quotations  don't  pay  the  bills,  and 
in  this  case,  the  bills  can  have  harmful 
effects.  Soaring  educational  costs  are  now 
a  given.  About  the  only  thing  we  can  do, 
aside  from  scholarships,  grants  and  edu- 
cational loans — all  of  which  are  becoming 
more  and  more  difficult  to  obtain — is  to 
optimize  the  transfusion  so  the  actual  cost 
to  us  (parents)  is  as  little  as  possible. 

In  other  words,  pay  the  educational  costs 
with  after-tax  dollars.  Failure  to  do  this 
can  as  much  as  double  the  cost,  depending 
on  your  tax  bracket. 

Simply  illustrated,  tuition  payments  are 
not  deductible,  so  a  parent  in  the  5()-per- 
cent  tax  bracket  must  "use"  $1(),(){)()  of 
earnings  to  make  a  $5 ,()()()  tuition  pay- 
ment. The  trick  is  to  let  someone  else — 
the  child — produce  the  funds  in  his  own, 
lower  tax  bracket,  so  the  overall  cost  is 
less.  There  are  several  ways  to  do  this. 

The   approach   to   tax-favored   educa- 


tional costs  can  be  divided  into  two  basic 
taxpayer  groups:  those  who  plan  and  the 
rest  of  us.  If  you  fall  into  the  first  category 
and  you  are  planning  now  for  the  edu- 
cation of  children  who  will  be  going  to 
college  five,  10  or  15  years  from  now, 
congratulations.  (You  probably  also  have 
perfect  teeth  and  always  file  your  tax  re- 
turns on  time.)  In  this  case,  you  can  con- 
sider such  things  as  yearly  gifts  (under  the 
Uniform  Gifts  to  Minors  Act),  "10-year" 
or  short-term  trusts,  interest-free  loans, 
gifts  and  lease-back  arrangements  and 
other  maneuvers.  In  general,  the  more 
time  you  have  to  accumulate  funds  in  a 
lower  tax  bracket,  the  better  off  you  (and 
the  child)  obviously  will  be.  If  your  child 
(or  any  other  college  candidate  under  your 
charge)  needs  the  funds  now,  it  is  a  bit 
more  difficult — but  not  impossible.  Let's 
start  with  minors. 

Under  the  Uniform  Gifts  to  Minors  Act, 
gifts  may  be  made  tax-free  (not  tax-de- 
ductible) to  anyone  under  the  age  of  ma- 
jority (18  in  Massachusetts)  in  the  name 
of  a  custodian  for  the  minor.  During  the 
period  of  minority,  all  income  earned  in 
the  account  will  be  taxed  to  the  minor  (no 
federal  tax  unless  income  exceeds  $1,000), 
and  the  accumulated  amount  may  then  be 
used  for  college  after  the  minor  reaches 
the  age  of  majority,  with  no  tax  conse- 
quences to  the  parent  or  other  donor. 

The  account  can  be  funded  with  cash 
gifts  or  gifts  of  securities.  If  the  securities 
have  appreciated,  the  capital  gain  will  be 
taxed  to  the  minor. 


WPl   JOURNAL 


For  gift- tax  purposes,  each  year  you  may 
give  $10,000  and  if  you  have  a  spouse  you 
(or  she)  may  give  an  additional  $10,000 
per  child  (or  other  donee).  If  the  gifts  are 
to  be  presently  used  for  tuition  or  other 
college  costs,  you  may  exceed  the  $10,000. 
Even  if  you  do  exceed  the  $10,000  tax- 
free  gift  limitation  for  things  other  than 
tuition,  the  sky  will  not  fall.  You  then 
merely  begin  to  use  up  a  part  of  the  al- 
lowable gift-tax  credit. 

The  problem  or  potential  risk  with  the 
Gifts  to  Minors  Act  is  that  you  have  no 
control  over  the  use  of  the  money.  Once 
the  child  is  an  adult,  he  has  a  right  to  use 
the  funds  any  way  he  likes.  Of  course  you 
could  remind  him  of  the  old  quotation: 
"spare  the  rod.  ..." 

Clifford  Trust 
Helps  You  Save 

The  Uniform  Gifts  to  Minors  Act  is  a  rel- 
atively easy  way  to  make  gifts  to  children 
for  the  purpose  of  ultimately  funding  a 
college  education  .  .  .  easy,  that  is,  in  the 
sense  that  the  transfer  of  money  or  prop- 
erty to  the  child  is  relatively  uncompli- 
cated and  you  avoid  such  "costly"  things 
as  lawyers'  fees. 

The  problem,  however,  with  the  Gifts 
to  Minors  Act  is  that  not  everyone  can 
afford  to  give  away  after-tax-dollars  of 
$3,000  (now  up  to  $10,000)  per  child  p^r 
year. 

Among  the  many  alternatives  we  will 
discuss  is  a  method  which,  although  a  bit 
more  complicated,  allows  you  to  give  up 
only  the  use  of  the  money  or  property  to 
the  child,  so  that  after  this  period  of  use 
(it  must  be  for  at  least  10  years),  the  prop- 
erty is  returned  to  you.  This  is  done  through 
a  trust,  often  called  a  Clifford  trust,  a  short- 
term  trust,  a  10-year  trust  or  a  reversion- 
ary trust. 

During  this  period  of  at  least  10  years, 
all  of  the  income  from  the  property  you 
have  placed  in  your  Clifford  trust  will  be 
taxed  either  to  the  trust  or  to  the  child, 
depending  upon  whether  it  is  held  in  the 
trust  or  paid  out  to  the  child,  respectively. 
This  can  result  in  substantial  income-tax 
savings,  depending,  of  course,  on  the 
property  transferred  and  the  amount  of 
income  generated.  For  example,  say  that 
Dad  transfers  500  shares  of  Electric  Light 
&  Power  Co.  stock  to  a  Clifford  trust  for 
daughter,  who  is  age  8.  The  stock  is  selling 
for  $45  per  share  and  pays  a  dividend  of 
$6  per  share,  or  $3,000  per  year  on  the 
500  shares. 


Tuition  pays,  in  part, 
for  faculty  excel' 
lence  in  teaching  and 
research,  advanced 
laboratory  equip- 
ment and  up-to-date 
project  facilities. 


In  Dad's  tax  bracket,  he  would  pay  a 
federal  tax  of  $1,500  on  these  dividends, 
but  in  daughter's  lower  bracket,  a  tax  of 
only  $280  will  be  paid.  Over  the  10-year 
period.  Dad  will  save  about  $12,000  in 
taxes,  not  counting  the  compound  interest 
on  the  savings.  After  the  10  years,  daugh- 
ter will  have  nearly  $20,000  to  use  for  her 
college  education  and  Dad  can  then  get 
his  stock  back,  with  no  adverse  tax  con- 
sequences to  him. 

There  are  some  points  that  must  be 
considered  with  the  Clifford  trust  to  qual- 
ify for  the  desired  tax  savings.  For  one, 
the  trust  must  last  for  at  least  10  years 
after  the  transfer  of  property  to  it,  and 
generally  it  may  not  be  changed  or  ter- 
minated during  this  period. 

Further,  if  any  of  the  trust  property  is 
sold  at  a  gain  during  the  10-year  period, 
the  person  creating  the  trust  (the  grantor) 
will  be  taxed  on  the  gain  even  though  he 
does  not,  at  that  point,  have  the  property 
or  the  proceeds. 

Finally,  the  transfer  of  property  to  this 
type  of  trust  constitutes  a  gift  (even  though 


the  property  will  eventually  return  to  the 
grantor).  Therefore,  consideration  must 
be  given  to  the  amount  placed  in  the  trust. 

For  gift- tax  purposes,  as  a  rule  of  thumb 
only,  the  amount  of  the  gift  on  a  transfer 
to  a  Clifford  trust  will  be  about  44  percent 
of  the  value  of  the  property  transferred. 
In  our  example  above.  Dad  transferred 
$22,500  of  stock,  and  44  percent  of  this 
would  be  about  $9,900.  Since  this  is  less 
than  the  allowable  annual  tax-free  gift  of 
$10,000,  Dad  will  have  no  gift-tax  prob- 
lems. Of  course,  there  are  many  other 
technical  requirements  that  your  Clifford 
trust  must  meet,  but  these  are  the  high- 
lights. 

One  of  the  primary  advantages  of  the 
Clifford  trust  is  that  income  from  specific 
property  other  than  cash  can  be  shifted 
to  the  child  without  selling  or  giving  away 
the  property.  There  is  no  other  way  this 
can  be  accomplished,  and  to  a  parent  who 
cannot  afford  to  give  the  underlying  prop- 
erty away,  the  Clifford  trust  can  be  a  tax- 
wise  way  of  producing  funds  for  educa- 
tion. 


APRIL  1983 


Gift'Leaseback 
Not  for  Everyone 

When  some  payment  or  expense  is  con- 
verted to  a  tax  deduction,  it  can  actually 
reduce  the  cost,  since  the  effect  of  the  tax 
deduction  is  to  cause  Uncle  Sam  to  bear 
a  part  of  the  expense. 

Educational  expenses  are  no  exception. 
There  are  some  maneuvers  which  can  have 
the  indirect  effect  of  rendering  these  costs 
tax-deductible.  One  of  them  is  the  "gift- 
leaseback"  arrangement. 

This  arrangement  does  not  apply  to 
everyone.  Generally,  it  is  limited  to  par- 
ents (or  other  benefactors)  who  use  some 
form  of  property  such  as  real  estate  or 
equipment  in  a  business  or  profession.  But 
given  the  huge  number  of  small  businesses 
and  professional  practices,  it  happens  to 
be  a  very  popular  approach  to  financing 
children's  college  education  on  a  tax- 
favored  basis. 

It  works  this  way.  The  businessman  or 
professional  (the  grantor)  transfers  his 
business  real  estate  or  equipment  to  an 
irrevocable  trust  for  the  benefit  of  his  chil- 
dren. Very  often,  a  Clifford  trust  is  used. 
After  the  property  is  transferred  to  the 
trust,  the  trustee  then  leases  the  property 
back  to  the  grantor  so  he  can  use  it  in  his 
business  or  professional  practice. 

TTie  trust  realizes  income  from  the  rental 
payments,  and  a  tax  is  paid  either  at  the 
trust  tax  bracket  (if  it  is  not  paid  out  to 
the  children)  or  at  the  children's  tax 
bracket,  if  the  funds  are  paid  out  to  them 
or  for  their  benefit  (for  instance,  college 
tuition  and  expenses).  Meanwhile,  the 
grantor  gets  a  tax  deduction  for  the  rental 
payments  to  the  trust. 

For  example.  Dr.  Forcible  Pullemout 
transfers  the  equipment  in  his  dental  prac- 
tice to  a  Clifford  trust  for  the  benefit  of 
his  two  children,  ages  19  and  18.  The 
trustee  is  required  to  pay  all  the  net  in- 
come out  to  the  children  each  year.  After 
the  transfer,  the  trustee  leases  the  equip- 
ment to  Dr.  Pullemout  for  $6, (XX)  per  year, 
which  in  turn  is  used  for  the  children's 
educations. 

Simply  stated,  the  doctor  gets  a  $6,000 
deduction,  and  the  children  each  pay  taxes 
on  S3,fXX),  or  about  $300.  For  the  two 
children,  the  doctor  would  save  about 
$2.4(K)  per  year  in  federal  income  taxes. 
In  other  words,  through  this  arrange- 
ment, the  government  is  contributing  a 
clean  $2,4(XJ  each  year  toward  the  chil- 
dren's educations. 

Of  course,  there  are  some  highly  tech- 


WPl  JOURNAL 


nical  considerations  with  this  arrange- 
ment which  could  affect  the  tax  conse- 
quences in  either  direction.  For  example, 
because  of  the  abihty  to  depreciate  busi- 
ness property,  it  could  be  that  the  trust 
or  the  children  would  pay  httle  or  no  tax 
on  the  rental  income.  On  the  other  hand, 
if  the  business  property  is  subject  to  a 
mortgage  on  which  the  grantor  is  Hable, 
some  or  all  of  the  tax  deductions  could 
be  lost.  It  is  an  area  where  expert  advice 
is  essential  to  success. 

To  make  matters  a  Httle  more  delicate, 
gift-leasebacks  are  frequently  examined 
very  closely  by  the  IRS,  and  if  the  ar- 
rangement fails  to  meet  the  accepted  cri- 
teria, it  will  be  challenged.  Those  who 
have  gone  before  you  have,  as  a  result  of 
many  tax  court  cases,  left  a  set  of  four 
basic  ingredients  necessary  to  survive  an 
IRS  attack. 

First,  the  trustee  of  the  irrevocable  trust 
must  be  an  independent  party.  This  could 
include  a  bank,  an  accountant  or  a  lawyer, 
but  definitely  should  not  be  a  grantor  or 
any  member  of  his  family. 

Second,  the  leaseback  should  not  be 
prearranged;  it  should  be  in  writing  and 
should  call  for  a  reasonable  rent  after  ne- 
gotiations with  the  independent  trustee. 

Third,  the  leaseback  must  have  a  "bona 


fide"  business  purpose.  (Use  of  the  prop- 
erty in  the  business  seems  to  satisfy  this 
requirement.) 

Fourth,  the  grantor  (the  person  who  is 
giving  the  property)  miist  not  retain  any 
interest  in  the  property.  In  some  cases, 
this  rule  could  preclude  a  reversionary  in- 
terest, such  as  the  return  of  the  property 
to  the  grantor  after  10  years.  If  this  is  the 
case,  the  problem  can  easily  be  avoided 
by  having  the  property  return  to  the  spouse 
of  the  grantor. 

Remember,  these  are  only  the  basics. 
In  addition  to  these  tests,  the  documents 
necessary  to  carry  out  the  plan  must  meet 
other  technical  and  practical  require- 
ments. It  is,  nevertheless,  quite  do-able, 
and  if  the  circumstances  lend  themselves 
to  such  an  arrangement,  thousands  of  tax 
dollars  can  be  saved. 

College  "Gifts" 
and  Borrow-back 

Few  things  have  been  more  dramatically 
affected  by  inflation  than  college  ex- 
penses. And  for  those  of  us  who  have  to 
pay  those  expenses,  it  hurts. 

Where  not  too  many  years  ago  a  year 


Computer  facilities 
play  an  increasingly 
key  role  in  a  WPI 
education.  More 
than  100  terminals 
across  campus  link 
students  to  the 
school's  three  main- 
frame computers. 


of  college  was  $1,000  or  $2,000,  the  same 
year  now  costs  $4,000  to  $8,000,  and  more. 
Every  dime  of  these  costs  we  can  save, 
therefore,  is  precious. 

One  way  to  ease  the  bite  is  to  have  the 
student  "earn"  the  college  money  at  his 
lower  tax  bracket.  The  problem  often  is 
that  the  student  has  little  or  no  money  on 
which  to  earn  anything.  Then  how  about 
gifts  to  the  student? 

This  could  be  helpful,  but  gifts,  in 
themselves,  are  not  tax-deductible,  and 
once  the  money  is  used  up,  more  gifts 
must  be  made.  Repeating  these  gifts  on 
a  non-deductible  basis  each  year  can  get 
pretty  expensive. 

Here's  another  idea.  Make  a  gift  (or 
gifts)  to  the  student,  then  borrow  your  gift 
back  with  interest.  The  interest  is  taxable 
income  to  the  student  but  also  deductible 
to  you.  Like  any  tax  maneuver,  it  must 
be  carried  out  with  attention  to  detail,  but 
if  properly  done,  it  can  work. 

For  example,  say  that  Mr.  and  Mrs.  B. 
want  to  help  put  their  daughter  through 
school,  and  the  costs  are  $4,000  to  $5,000 
per  year.  Daughter  has  a  little  money 
saved,  but  not  enough  to  cover  all  the 
costs.  Mr.  and  Mrs.  B.  make  a  gift  to 
daughter  of  $20,000  ($10,000  from  each, 
so  gift  taxes  are  not  a  concern).  Later, 
they  borrow  the  $20,000  back  from 
daughter  at  an  interest  rate  of  2  points 
over  the  prime  rate.  At  a  prime  of  16 
percent,  they  will  pay  18  percent  per  year 
interest,  or  a  total  of  $3,600  per  year;  al- 
most enough  to  cover  daughter's  college 
costs. 

The  interest  is  fully  deductible  to  the 
parents  and  is  taxable  income  to  the 
daughter.  If  she  has  no  other  income,  she'll 
pay  a  federal  tax  of  about  $400,  leaving 
nearly  enough  to  cover  her  costs.  Parents 
get  a  $3,600  tax  deduction,  saving  them 
as  much  as  $1,800  per  year  in  federal  taxes. 

At  the  end  of  her  college  education, 
daughter's  parents  could  pay  back  the  debt 
and  the  money  could  be  used  by  daughter 
in  starting  her  career.  Of  course,  there  is 
also  the  possibility  that  daughter,  instead, 
would  forgive  the  debt.  That  is,  she  could 
make  a  gift  of  $10,000  to  each  parent,  but 
this  would  be  extremely  risky,  as  the  IRS 
could  then  claim  that  the  whole  deal  was 
prearranged  and  there  never  was  a  debt. 
If  this  happened,  the  parents'  deduction 
would  be  disallowed  (for  at  least  the  pre- 
vious three  years). 

Under  this  gift  and  borrow-back  ar- 
rangement, there  should  be  an  actual 
transfer  of  funds  to  the  student,  or  to  a 
trust  for  the  benefit  of  the  student  if  the 
student  is  a  minor.  Subsequent  to  the  gift. 


the  borrowing  should  be  evidenced  by  a 
valid,  enforceable  promissory  note  exe- 
cuted by  either  or  both  of  the  parents, 
providing  for  payment  of  interest  and  a 
date  for  payment  of  the  principal  (ma- 
turity). At  maturity,  the  note  should  ac- 
tually be  paid  by  the  parent  or  parents. 
What  the  student  then  does  with  the  funds 
is  the  student's  business. 

Shifting  Income 
Can  Save  on  Taxes 

It  seems  clear  that  a  student's  college  ed- 
ucation can  be  cheaper  for  the  parent  if 
the  student,  rather  than  the  parent,  earns 
the  funds  necessary  for  the  college  costs. 


ducing  fashion.  The  income  from  the  in- 
vestment will  be  taxed  to  either  the  stu- 
dent or  the  trust,  depending  upon  whether 
it  is  kept  in  the  trust  or  distributed  to  the 
student. 

For  example.  Dad  cashes  in  his  $50,000 
certificate  of  deposit  and  loans  the  cash 
at  no  interest  to  his  spouse,  as  trustee  of 
a  trust  for  the  benefit  of  their  son,  James, 
age  18.  Spouse  (as  trustee)  then  purchases 
some  four-year  government  agency  bonds 
paying  15-percent  interest,  or  $7,500  per 
year.  The  same  year  James  enters  college, 
with  total  costs  of  $6,500  per  year,  and 
Mom  as  trustee  distributes  the  income  to 
James,  who  pays  an  income  tax  of  about 
$700  and  uses  the  balance  to  pay  his  tu- 
ition. In  the  50-percent  tax  bracket.  Dad 
would  have  had  to  earn  $15,000  to  give 


In  just  ten  years,  the 
rate  of  student  ath- 
letic  participation  at 
all  levels — whether 
in  the  nine  men's  and 
seven  women's  var- 
sity sports  or  in  the 
42  intramural  and 
club  sports — has 
nearly  doubled. 


This  can  often  be  accomplished  by  a  shift- 
ing of  income  from  the  parent  to  the  stu- 
dent. 

Shifting  income  from  a  high-bracket 
taxpayer  to  a  lower-bracket  taxpayer  is  a 
sure-fire  way  of  saving  taxes,  provided  you 
can  find  a  sure-fire  way  of  shifting  the 
income.  The  Clifford  trust,  or  10-year  trust, 
has  been  tried  and  tested  for  shifting  in- 
come, but  not  everyone  wants  or  needs 
to  shift  income  for  10  years. 

There  is  another  tried  and  often  tested 
way  to  shift  income  for  any  period  you 
like — whether  it  be  10  days,  10  months  or 
10  years.  It's  called  the  interest-free  de- 
mand loan. 

The  concept  is  simple.  You,  the  parent 
(or  grandparent,  or  other  benefactor),  will 
make  a  loan  to  the  student,  or  to  a  trust 
for  the  benefit  of  the  student  at  no  inter- 
est, and  the  student  or  the  trust  will  invest 
the  loan  proceeds  in  some  income-pro- 


James  the  same  amount — a  savings  of 
about  $7,000  (after  considering  James's 
federal  income  tax). 

This  pattern  repeats  for  three  more  years 
until  James  finishes  college,  at  which  time 
Dad  calls  the  loan  and  receives  his  $50,000 
back.  Total  savings:  more  than  $20,000. 

There  are  a  couple  of  important  re- 
quirements to  the  interest-free  loan  ar- 
rangement. For  one,  the  loan  must  be  a 
"demand"  loan  rather  than  for  a  specified 
period  of  time.  That  is,  the  lender  (Dad 
in  our  example)  should  have  the  right  to 
"call"  the  note  (demand  payment)  at  any 
time.  Secondly,  an  actual  written,  en- 
forceable promissory  note  should  be  signed 
by  the  borrower  (Mom,  as  trustee,  in  our 
example)  and  the  note  should  actually  be 
given  to  the  lender  and  held  until  paid. 
Further,  only  money  should  be  loaned. 
Loaning  property  other  than  money,  par- 
ticularly income-producing  property  such 


APRIL  1983        5 


A  thematic  se- 
quence of  courses  in 
areas  ranging  from 
art  history  and  mu- 
sic to  philosophy, 
literature  and  the 
history  of  technol- 
ogy and  science, 
round  out  the  edu- 
cation of  the  tech- 
nological humanist. 


as  stocks  or  bonds,  will  almost  assuredly 
produce  tax  problems  for  the  lender  and 
may  defeat  the  entire  purpose  of  the  ar- 
rangement. 

There  are  several  very  important  ad- 
vantages to  the  interest-free  loan,  partic- 
ularly if  done  through  a  trust.  For  ex- 
ample, if  James  does  poorly  and  drops  out 
of  college.  Dad  could  call  the  loan  and 
get  his  money  back  immediately.  On  the 
other  hand,  if  James  went  on  to  graduate 
school  Dad  could  simply  let  the  loan  con- 
tinue. If  at  any  time  Dad  needed  a  portion 
of  the  funds  he  could  call  a  portion  of  the 
loan.  It  is  this  tremendous  flexibility  of 
time  and  control  that  can  make  the  in- 
terest-free loan  much  more  desirable  than 
the  10-year  Clifford  trust. 

There  is  one  other  pomt  that  you  should 
realize  about  mterest-free  loans.  The  IRS 
hates  them.  The  reason  is  partly  that  it  is 
such  an  easy,  straightforward  way  to  save 
taxes,  and  partly  because  it  is  such  a  "low- 
profile"  arrangement  (it  is  difficult  for  them 


even  to  be  aware  of  it  since  there  is  no 
requirement  to  report  such  a  loan),  but 
mainly  because  almost  every  time  they 
have  challenged  the  arrangement  in  court, 
the  IRS  has  lost.  For  this  reason,  advisers 
generally  recommend  the  use  of  interest- 
free  loans  as  an  effective  way  to  shift  in- 
come, especially  for  college  educational 
expenses. 

A  Court  Decision 
for  the  Big  Guys 

In  a  recent  U.S.  Court  of  Appeals  decision, 
however,  the  court  held,  in  effect,  that  giv- 
ing someone  the  use  of  money  for  no  in- 
terest, or  for  a  rate  less  than  the  market 
rate,  is  a  gift,  tiixable  to  the  donor/lender. 
After  having  lost  every  one  of  the  many 
court  battles  on  interest-free  loans  of  this 
sort,  the  IRS  is  justifiably  jubilant  over 
the  victory  and  no  doubt  still  partying  in 


celebration  of  this  landmark  decision, 
which  involved  more  than  $80,000  in  de- 
linquent gift  taxes  and  additional  charges 
to  the  Dickman  family  of  Florida,  which 
made  the  loans. 

Involved  were  hundreds  of  thousands 
of  dollars'  worth  of  interest-free  demand 
notes  from  Mr.  and  Mrs.  Dickman  to  their 
son,  Lyle,  and  to  a  closely  held  corpo- 
ration. As  in  their  past  attacks  on  these 
loans,  the  IRS  contended  that  the  use  of 
the  money  at  no  charge  was  no  different 
than  a  transfer  of  property  by  gift.  The 
gift-tax  laws,  they  argued,  impose  a  tax 
on  the  transfer  of  property  by  an  individ- 
ual for  less  than  adequate  and  full  con- 
sideration. The  value  of  the  gift  for  gift- 
tax  purposes  will  be  the  value  of  the 
"property"  transferred  less  the  amount 
received  by  the  donor. 

Remember  that  in  this  case  we  are  not 
talking  about  the  amount  of  the  loan,  but 
rather  the  value  of  the  use  of  the  amount 
of  the  loan  for  a  period  of  time.  This,  the 
IRS  said — and  the  court  agreed — was  the 
property  right  that  was  given,  and  it  has 
a  clear  and  definite  value.  In  other  words, 
what  would  it  cost  if  you  were  to  ask  a 
bank  or  other  lending  institution  to  let 
you  use,  say,  $10,000  for  a  year?  Clearly 
it  does  have  a  value  and  in  the  Dickman 
case,  since  nothing  was  charged  for  the 
use  of  the  money,  a  gift  was  considered 
made  at  the  end  of  each  taxable  period. 

The  fact  that  the  interest-free  notes  were 
"demand"  notes,  a  point  which  most 
professionals  (including  myself)  heavily 
stressed  to  counter  IRS  attacks,  made  no 
difference,  said  the  court.  The  only  dis- 
tinction there  was  the  period  involved  for 
valuing  the  amount  of  the  gift.  In  its  hold- 
ing, the  court  overruled  the  famous  Crown 
case,  which  was  the  recent  frontrunner  for 
interest-free  loan  guidelines  and  which 
helped  establish  the  importance  of  the  de- 
mand note,  as  opposed  to  a  note  for  a 
term  of  years. 

But  does  all  this  mean  that  you  should 
now  steer  clear  of  interest-free  loans?  Are 
they  now  a  lost  ark  for  tax  savings?  I  say 
no  on  both  counts.  Here's  why. 

In  the  Dickman  case  the  IRS  made  a 
sound  argument  that  the  free  use  of  money 
can  be  a  gift,  but  they  also  told  us  how 
they  intend  to  value  it  for  gift-tax  pur- 
poses. It  appears  they  intend  to  apply  the 
same  interest  rate,  as  a  measure  of  the 
value  of  the  gift  (not  as  a  tax),  that  they 
apply  to  underpayment  of  taxes  and  tax 
refunds.  This  interest  rate  is  set  in  Oc- 
tober of  each  year  according  to  the 
(rounded-off)  prime  interest  rate  in  Sep- 
tember. 


WPI   JOURNAL 


In  other  words,  [using  a  hypothetical 
12-percent  prime]  at  this  rate  as  a  measure 
of  the  use  of  the  funds,  an  interest-free 
loan  of  $83,000  for  one  year  would  con- 
stitute a  gift  of  $9,960.  Because  of  the 
$10,000  tax-free  gifts  which  may  now  be 
given  each  year  to  a  donee,  there  would 
be  no  gift-tax  problem  with  this  interest- 
free  loan.  And  if  a  spouse  consents  to  the 
use  of  his  or  her  additional  gift-tax  exclu- 
sion, an  interest-free  loan  of  $166,000  may 
be  made  (if  the  12-percent  rate  applies) — 
more  if  the  rate  goes  down,  less  if  it  goes 
up. 

Furthermore,  even  if  you  exceed  the 
gift-tax-free  amount,  you  can  still  apply  a 
part  of  your  unified  tax  credit,  so  the  pos- 
sibility of  having  to  pay  a  gift  tax  under 
current  law  is  quite  remote,  unless  you 
want  to  loan,  say,  a  million  or  more,  in 
which  case  I'd  like  you  to  know  that  my 
credit  is  very  good. 

So  you  can  see,  despite  the  import  of 
the  Dickman  case  and  the  fanfare  it  will 
no  doubt  be  given,  for  us  small-to-me- 
dium-sized shooters,  the  interest-free  loan 
is  still  very  fair  game. 

Deductions  Now 
Lead  to  Cash  Later 

As  we  have  seen,  tax-wise  financing  of 
college  education  is  limited  by  only  three 
things:  the  expert  advice  available  to  you., 
your  personal  and  family  circumstances, 
and  the  amount  of  money  you  have. 

Generally,  those  persons  who  have  more 
money  will  benefit  more  than  the  rest  of 
us  from  creative  tax  planning  for  other- 
wise non-deductible  educational  ex- 
penses. One  effective  maneuver  fre- 
quently used  by  the  heavy  hitters  is 
converting  a  tax  shelter  into  a  scholarship 
fund. 

During  the  past  few  years,  so-called  tax 
shelters  have  become  extremely  popular 
as  a  tax-deferring  device. 

Basically,  shelters  allow  the  investor  to 
defer  taxes  by  providing  a  current  tax  de- 
duction which  comes  back  to  the  investor 
later  (called  a  "recapture"),  sometimes  in 
the  form  of  a  cash  flow.  For  the  most  part, 
these  shelters  are  in  real  estate  or  oil  and 
gas  ventures.  By  taking  advantage  of  the 
current  deduction,  then  shifting  the  re- 
capture income  to  the  child  in  a  lower 
bracket,  the  tax  payer  makes  an  effective 
double-play  against  the  IRS. 

For  example,  say  that  Mr.  B.  invests 
$25,000  in  a  conservative  oil  and  gas  in- 
vestment for  1981 ,  which  in  turn  gives  him 


a  tax  deduction  of  a  like  amount.  In  the 
60-percent  tax  bracket,  this  saves  him 
$15,000  in  taxes.  In  1982,  while  the  ven- 
ture is  still  developing  its  prospects,  Mr. 
B.  transfers  his  entire  share  in  the  venture 
to  a  trust  for  the  benefit  of  his  daughter, 
who  expects  to  begin  college  in  1983.  Be- 
cause of  the  uncertainty  of  value  and  re- 
stricted marketability  of  the  share,  in  all 
probability  the  value  of  the  gift  in  1982 
for  gift-tax  purposes  will  be  nominal,  and 
Mr.  B.  will  not  have  to  use  any  of  his 
allowable  gift-tax  credit.  Further,  the  trust 
could  be  a  Clifford  trust,  where  Mr.  B. 
would  recover  his  interest  in  the  venture 
after  10  years,  if  he  wished. 

In  1983  the  venture  begins  to  produce 
a  cash  flow  of  $500  per  month  to  daugh- 
ter's trust.  If  the  payments  are  eligible  for 
the  current  oil-depletion  allowance  ben- 
efit, simply  stated  (it  can  be  unbelievably 
complicated)  this  is  22  percent  of  the  $500 
monthly  payments,  and  that  amount  would 


be  tax-free.  The  balance  would  produce 
an  annual  tax  of  about  $200,  leaving  $5,800 
after  taxes  to  finance  daughter's  educa- 
tion. (Even  if  daughter's  income  were  not 
eligible  for  the  oil-depletion  allowance, 
which  is  quite  possible,  there  would  only 
be  a  tax  of  about  $600  for  the  year.) 
Hopefully,  this  income  would  continue 
over  a  period  of  years,  at  a  cost  to  Dad 
of  only  $10,000  ($25,000  investment  less 
$15,000  tax  benefit). 

Now  for  the  warnings.  (You  knew  they 
had  to  come!)  Tax  shelters  are  by  nature 
speculative;  this  is  one  of  the  reasons 
Congress  gives  them  favorable  tax  treat- 
ment. Therefore,  there  is  no  guarantee  of 
the  projected  cash  flow.  And  even  if  there 
is  a  cash  flow,  it  may  not  be  consistent. 

Second,  the  complicated  nature  of  the 
tax  laws  make  this  maneuver  a  very  del- 
icate one;  consequently,  it  should  only  be 
considered  after  obtaining  the  best  expert 
advice  available.  Nevertheless,  there  are 


#•#*•#• 


The  end.  And  a  new 
beginning.

many  such  ventures  designed  to  produce 
current  deductions  with  future  cash  flow, 
which  lend  themselves  neatly  to  this  tax- 
savings  plan. 

When  a  Gift 
Means  Savings 

I  should  be  ashamed  of  myself,  one  reader 
told  me.  It  is  just  not  fair  that  she  had 
already  paid  thousands  for  college  edu- 
cation of  children  without  any  tax  advan- 
tage, and  here  I  was  telling  others  how 
they  could  finance  education  through  tax- 
saving  schemes.  What  can  I  say?  I  guess 
some  horses  are  never  led  to  the  water. 

Unfortunately,  it  is  a  fiercely  compet- 
itive world,  particularly  from  the  stand- 
point of  tax  planning.  One  must  often  take 
an  aggressive  position  and  should  always 
ask,  "Is  there  any  way  this  expense/in- 
vestment/transaction can  be  structured  to 
give  me  a  tax  advantage?"  College  costs 
are  no  exception. 

We  have  explored  several  ways  of  re- 
ducing educational  cost  through  tax  ben- 
efits, and  the  main  thrust  has  been  the 


shifting  of  taxable  income  to  the  child,  so 
that  the  child  can  use  this  income  (in  a 
lower  tax  bracket)  to  pay  his  own  college 
expenses  at  a  lower  overall  outlay  of  cash 
to  the  parent  or  other  sponsor. 

For  a  quick  recap,  the  several  ways  that 
seem  to  work  include: 

•  Outright  gift  to  the  child  (or  to  a 
custodian  under  the  Uniform  Gifts  to 
Minors  Act)  where,  although  the 
gifts  themselves  are  not  tax-deducti- 
ble, the  income  from  the  gifts  is 
taxed  to  the  child,  thereby  saving  the 
parent  tax  dollars  each  year. 

•  Gifts  of  income-producing  prop- 
erty to  a  10-year  trust,  so  that  the 
child  (or  the  trust)  collects  the  in- 
come in  a  lower  tax  bracket  for  the 
10-year  period,  and  after  that  the 
property  returns  to  the  parent  or 
someone  designated  by  the  parent. 

•  Gift  (or  sale)  of  business  or 
professional  property  to  the  child  or 
to  a  trust  for  the  benefit  of  the  child, 
which  is  then  rented  back  to  the  par- 
ent or  other  sponsor.  The  rental  pay- 
ments are  deductible  to  the  parent 
and  taxed  to  the  child  or  the  trust  at 
a  lower  bracket. 


•  Gifts  of  money  to  the  child  or  to 
a  trust  and  a  subsequent  borrowing 
back  of  the  money  by  the  parent,  at 
a  substantial  (but  fair)  interest  rate. 
The  interest  paid  is  deductible  to  the 
parent  and  taxable  to  the  child. 

•  Interest-free  loans  from  the  par- 
ent to  the  child  or  to  a  trust  for  the 
child.  The  child  or  trust  then  invests 
the  borrowed  funds  to  generate  in- 
come at  a  lower  tax  bracket,  while 
the  parent/lender  may  recall  the  loan 
at  any  time.  The  funds  used  to  make 
the  interest-free  loans  could  be  sav- 
ings, or  funds  borrowed  from  your 
own  retirement  plan  or  insurance 
policy,  or  even  from  a  second  mort- 
gage on  your  home. 

•  Gifts  of  a  tax-shelter  interest 
after  the  deductions  have  been  taken 
but  before  the  income  is  generated, 
so  the  parent  receives  the  tax  bene- 
fits on  both  sides,  but  this  can  only 
be  done  with  certain  types  of  shel- 
ters. 

•  Employing  your  child  in  your 
business  and  paying  his  wages.  The 
wages  are  deductible  to  you  and  only 
taxed  to  the  child  if  they  exceed 
$3,300  for  the  year  (assuming  he  has 
no  other  income).  Of  course,  the 
child  should  actually  render  services 
to  your  company. 

•  Any  other  legitimate  maneuvers 
that  you  and  your  tax  advisors  can 
dream  up. 

As  a  general  rule,  you  should  be  sure 
that  none  of  the  income  earned  by  your 
child  or  your  child's  trust  is  to  be  used  to 
satisfy  any  of  your  legal  obligations.  To 
the  extent  that  it  is,  then  that  amount  will 
be  taxed  to  you,  the  parent.  For  example, 
if  you  sign  an  agreement  to  pay  $3,000 
for  the  first  year  of  schoQl  and  it  is  paid 
out  of  the  child's  trust,  then  the  $3,000 
will  be  income  to  you,  regardless  of  how 
carefully  the  trust  was  set  up. 

Unlike  many  other,  more  sophisticated 
tax  schemes,  financing  of  college  educa- 
tion through  one  or  more  of  the  ap- 
proaches suggested  in  this  article  is  avail- 
able to  everyone,  not  just  the  big  shooters. 
Consequently,  if  you  are  to  survive,  you 
must  revise  your  attitude  and  think  of  these 
tax  plans  as  fruits  on  a  tall  tree,  then  make 
arrangements  to  pick  those  within  your 
reach. 

Alexander  A.  Bove,  Jr.,  is  a  Boston 
lawyer  whose  column,  "The  Family 
Money,"  appears  weekly  in  the  Boston 
Globe.  Reprinted  with  permission  of  the 
author  and  the  Boston  Globe.

ANIMALS 

IN  THE 

ARTS 


Art  by  Clinton  Anowood 

These  anomalous  creatures  are  in  the 
tradition  of  Arthur  Rackham  and  Er- 
nest Shepard.  They  are  animals,  yes. 
The  frogs  show  a  froggy  leaping  bra- 
vura. The  sheep  are  dumb  and  digni- 
fied. The  alhgators  are  sleepily  ro- 
coco. But  they  also  display  all  the 
traits  of  the  singers,  dancers,  and  mu- 
sicians they  portray. 

At  right  is  the  lovely  Cho-Cho-San, 
Puccini's  Madame  Butterfly,  who 
commits  hara-kiri  for  the  sake  of  one 
Lieutenant  Pinkerton.  Notice  her  air 
of  operatic  self-possession:  she  is  sure 
she  belongs  at  center  stage.- 


he  corps  de  ballet,  at 
the  back  of  the  stage 
(abo%'e),  has  marsh  grass  as 
a  headdress.  Downstage, 
this  frog  executes  a  grand 
jete  (a  great  leap),  while  the 
harlequin  performs  a  ca- 
briole (literally,  a  caper) 
and  the  couple  a  pas  de 
deux.  "The  frog  possesses 
an  essential  quality  that  a 
lot  of  real  live  dancers 
should  envy."  Arrowood 
comments. 


ALUMNI  MAGAZINE  CONSORTIUM 


A, 


rrowood  believes  he  got  the  idea  of 
dancing  frogs  from  the  Royal  Ballet  of 
London,  which  performed  a  version  of 
Beatrix  Potter  tales  some  years  ago.  He 
was  transfixed  by  the  image  of  Jeremy 
Fisher  leaping  across  the  stage. 

Arrowood  tries  to  make  his  frogs  ana- 
tomically correct,  which  is  why  their  torsos 
bulge  and  their  feet  sickle — these  are,  after 
all,  frogs.  But  he  has  endowed  the  female 
dancers  with  mammaries  and  eyelashes, 
"just  to  give  them  a  little  more  feminine 
charm."  The  dancing,  however,  so  far  as 
the  frogginess  of  the  models  allows,  is  cor- 
rectly performed. 

When  he  draws,  he  starts  with  the  eyes. 
"I  don't  really  feel  comfortable  until  I've 
got  the  eyes.  But  once  the  eyes  are  done, 
I  feel  I've  established  some  contact  with 
the  drawing  and  the  rest  of  it  flows."

he  alligators  were  the  first  of  the  cultured  creatures  to 
be  drawn,  back  in  1975,  and  Arrowood  has  since  moved  to  a 
simpler  type  of  rendition.  He  admires  the  clear,  simple  lines 
with  which  Ernest  Shepard  illustrated  The  Wind  in  the  Wil- 
lows, and  is  striving  toward  a  less  self-conscious  style.  The 
baroque  elegance  of  these  drawings,  however,  seems  to  suit 
some  quality  in  alligators.  Their  teeth,  perhaps. 

From  the  left:  a  pianist.  The  piano  is  copied  from  an  in-

tures human  faces.

ith  the  foxes  and  the  hunting  horns  on 
her  costume,  this  French  horn  player  honors  the 
hunting  ancestry  of  her  instrument.  She  has  a 
self-satisifed  look,  as  if  her  fellow  horn-players 
were  softly  shuffling  their  feet  to  applaud  a  solo 
passage.  "That's  the  ultimate  compliment,"  Ar- 
rowood  says — he  is  a  flutist — "because  your 
peers  really  know  whether  you  played  well  or 
not." 

Above,  a  noble  trombone  player  sports  a  fur- 
lined  coat,  proud  that  his  is  the  oldest  instrument 
in  the  orchestra.

be  ordered  from  Gambit  Press,  27  North  Main 
Street,  Meeting  House  Green,  Ipswich,  Mass. 
01938.  Ask  for  Lambs'  Tales  from  Great  Operas, 
ators  and  Music,  and  Frogs  and  the  Ballet, 
by  Donald  Elliott  and  Clinton  Arrowood.

opening,  as  will  the  Brooklyn  Bridge  this 
May  24.  On  tap  are  a  parade,  a  sound  and 
Hght  show,  a  harbor  salute,  and  a  musical 
composition  commissioned  in  its  honor. 
There  are  longer  suspension  bridges  than 
the  Brooklyn;  the  Williamsburg  is  one  of 
them,  by  five  feet.  And  there  were  other 
suspension  bridges  that  preceded  it.  though 
at  the  time,  none  so  long.  But  no  bridge 
in  history,  it  seems  fair  to  say,  has  cap- 


practical  engineering  was  more  his  speed. 
Such  as  maintaining  bridges. 

The  bearded,  44-year-old  engineer  is 
cordial,  and  he  displays  a  knowledge  of 
the  bridge's  history  far  more  extensive  than 
his  job  demands;  it's  plain  he  doesn't  re- 
sent the  attention  the'Brooklyn  Bridge  is 
getting.  Yet  something  galls  him  about  all 
the  centennial  goings-on:  "The  city  has 
been  ignoring  the  bridge  for  a  hundred 


1877:  Seven  years  into  construction,  the 
four  massive  cables  that  support  the 
bridge,  each  consisting  of  6,000  individual 
wires,  are  scarcely  begun.  Posing  on  the 
footbridge  is  a  group  of  company  trustees. 


tured  the  imagination  of  so  many  people 
as  the  Brooklyn  Bridge.  It  is  a  one-of-a- 
kind  cultural  symbol.  It  is  the  quintessen- 
tial American  bridge. 

For  Robert  Gough,  the  centennial 
hoopla  has  been  a  bit  of  a  bother.  As 
Chief  Engineer  for  Bridge  Operations  of 
the  New  York  City  Department  of  Trans- 
portation, Gough  has  all  the  many 
hundreds  of  the  city  bridges  in  his  care. 
So  numerous  are  the  writers  and  photog- 
raphers who  want  to  explore  the  Brooklyn 
Bridge  as  the  centennial  draws  near,  he 
says,  that  it  amounts  to  "a  regular  tour  of 
the  thing;  you've  got  people  bumping  into 
each  other  on  the  cables."  And  actually 
climbing  them:  A  photographer  wishing 
to  take  pictures  from  atop  one  of  the 
bridge's  276-foot  granite  and  limestone 
towers  must  reimburse  the  city,  at  $27  per 
hour  each,  for  the  two  ironworkers  needed 
to  help  him  navigate  a  steeply-pitched,  15- 
inch  diameter  cable,  with  only  guy  wires 
and  safety  harness  for  support.  People  do 
it,  eagerly.  It's  the  only  way  up. 

Gough  has  a  bachelor's  degree  in  civil 
engineering  from  City  College.  Some  years 
ago,  he  registered  for  a  master's  program 
at  Columbia  University.  But  after  a  taste 
of  all  that  theoretical  math,  he  says,  he 
decided  he  didn't  need  it;  down  to  earth. 


years,"  he  says.  "Now  it's  basically  one 
year  of  activity,  and  then  they'll  go  back 
to  ignoring  it." 

In  the  shadow  of  the  bridge,  along  Pearl 
Street  in  Manhattan,  an  old  Chinese 
man  limps  wearily  along.  Vehicles 
stream  by  in  infinite  variety.  Next  to  a 
bridge  pier,  squared-off  piles  of  new  brick 
stand  beside  scraps  of  stone  and  virgin 
trees:  The  city  is  making  a  small  brick- 
paved  park  to  coincide  with  the  centen- 
nial. A  group  of  boys  on  BMX  bikes  take 
turns  rolling  down  a  steep  brick  embank- 
ment that's  part  of  it.  A  woman  drags  an 
unhappy-looking  little  girl  behind  her.  A 
dead  car  reduced  to  a  hulk,  denuded  even 
of  its  wheels,  lies  sprawled  alongside  the 
curb.  "The  single  most  beautiful  object 
in  New  York,"  writer  Nicholas  King  has 
called  the  Brooklyn  Bridge.  But  it  is  from 
the  ragged  clutter  of  a  sometimes  ugly  city 
that  the  bridge  rises. 

It  begins  in  a  great  bowl,  the  rim  of 
which  is  formed  by  the  surrounding  office 
towers.  In  the  middle  of  the  bowl,  as  con- 
verging highway  ramps  wrap  around  it, 
the  bridge  gashes  up  through  the  bottom. 
For  a  Brooklyn-bound  pedestrian,  the 
bridge  begins  inauspiciously:  You  arc 
confined  to  a  raw,  concrete  path,  pressed 


in  on  either  side  by  speeding  auto  traffic. 
At  this  point  in  your  trek,  it's  a  matter  of 
semantics  whether  you're  on  the  bridge 
proper  or  on  the  Manhattan  street  system 
that  feeds  it. 

In  time,  you  reach  a  stairway,  two  sets 
of  seven  steps  each,  and  abruptly  it's  as 
if  the  city  dropped  away:  Ahead,  sup- 
planting the  bare  concrete,  stretch  a  wood- 
slatted  promenade,  the  soaring  cables,  the 
great  gothic  towers.  The  roar  of  traffic 
still  strikes  the  ear,  but  now  it's  more  dis- 
tant to  the  eye.  Now  the  cars  speed  below 
you,  hedged  in  by  steel  trusses.  Up  here, 
everything  seems  neater,  more  ordered, 
more  spacious.  The  array  of  wood  slats 
stretching  out  before  you  in  a  wide  ribbon 
is  somehow  welcoming.  Walkers  and  cy- 
clists seem  unhurried — a  rarity  in  this 
nervous  city.  An  occasional  wood  bench 
recalls  a  boardwalk,  or  a  quiet  park. 

After  maybe  50  yards,  the  bridge's  four 
great  cables  begin  their  ascent  from  be- 
neath the  roadway,  bound  for  the  tops  of 
the  towers  looming  ahead.  Wire  rope  sus- 
penders plunge  from  the  cables,  holding 
up  the  roadway.  At  the  Manhattan  tower, 
well  out  over  the  East  River,  another  short 
stairway  brings  you  yet  further  above  the 
traffic.  And  here  a  steel  web  of  vertical 
suspenders  and  diagonal  stays,  tied  to- 
gether at  knot-like  junctures  with  loops 
of  metal,  trembling  a  little  as  the  traffic 
roars  past,  irresistibly  compels  attention. 
There  are  four  vertical  planes  of  criss- 
crossing stays  and  suspenders,  one  for  each 
cable,  and  the  lines  of  the  foreground  (or 
inboard)  planes  form  diagonal  windows 
for  viewing  the  outboard  ones.  Trape- 
zoids and  triangles  are  everywhere. 

These,  suspenders  and  stays  form  fili- 
gree walls  flanking  you  on  either  side;  only 
a  narrow  slot  of  unobstructed  sky  re- 
mains, directly  above.  But  near  midspan, 
as  the  cables  dip  down  again  to  the  road- 
way, the  slot  grows  wider,  the  walls  smaller, 
until  finally  they  vanish.  And  it  is  here, 
out  in  the  middle  of  the  river,  that  the 
designer's  vision  seems  most  perfectly  re- 
alized: Almost  800  feet  from  either  tower, 
you're  half  a  mile  from  either  shore  and 
135  feet  above  the  river,  and  the  city  streets 
in  all  their  angry  commotion  are  far  out 
of  mind  and  sight.  Here,  out  over  the 
water,  as  a  stiff  wind  blows  up  the  East 
River,  it  is  just  you,  and  the  steel,  and 
the  sky,  and  the  perfect  catenary  curve  of 
Mr.  Roebling's  cables. 

Dear  Father,"  Washington  Roeb- 
ling  wrote  from  the  Royal  Hotel 
in  London  on  July  19,  1867.  "Last 
Sunday,  I  paid  a  visit  to  the  two  Suspen-

sion  Bridges  over  the  Thames — the  Lam- 
beth and  the  Chelsea  bridges.  The  former 
is  a  wire  rope  bridge  with  two  cables  on 
each  side  each  consisting  of  7  ropes  in  6 
X  6;  each  cable  is  nearly  6"  in  diam.  They 
are  painted  with  a  black  varnish  and  have 
the  cracks  in  the  upper  side  filled  up  with 
cement  to  shed  the  water  ..." 

Washington's  father  was  John  Roeb- 
ling.  who  planned  to  soon  build  the  long- 
est suspension  bridge  in  the  world.  It  would 
be  some  500  feet  longer  than  the  current 
recordholder.  one  he'd  also  built,  across 
the  Ohio  River  at  Cincinnati.  The  Brook- 
lyn Bridge  was  to  use,  for  the  first  time, 
cables  made  of  steel,  which  still  bordered 
on  being  an  experimental  material.  Its 
towers  would  be,  as  someone  would  put 
it  years  later,  "about  the  largest,  most 
massive  things  ever  built  on  the  entire 
North  American  continent."  They  were 
to  rest  on  immense  foundations,  102  feet 
by  162  feet  in  section,  built  using  a  new 
technique  involving  pneumatic  caissons — 
submerged  boxes  filled  with  compressed 
air  in  which  men  could  work  underwater. 
There  was  much,  though,  that  John 
Roebling  didn't  yet  know  about  caissons. 
So  in  the  spring  of  1867,  he  sent  his  son 
Washington  to  Europe,  with  his  new  wife 
Emily,  to  report  back  on  bridges  in  gen- 
eral and  on  caissons  in  particular. 

For  nine  months,  Washington's  long, 
detailed,  neatly  scribed  letters  arrived  at 
his  father's  big  house  in  Trenton,  New 
Jersey.  The  Roeblings,  apparently,  were 
neither  of  them  much  for  long  preambles 
or  elaborate  expressions  of  fond  feeling: 
Dear  father.  Washington's  letters  would 
invariably  begin.  Then  he'd  launch  di- 
rectly into  an  elaborate  treatment  of  Tel- 
ford's bridge  over  the  Menai  Strait,  or 
Brunei's  bridge  at  Clifton.  .  .  Kircaldy's 
materials  testing  machine  was  big  enough 
to  take  a  specimen  15  feet  long,  boasted 
a  theoretical  capacity  of  one  million  pounds 
.  .  .  The  caissons  he  saw  in  Koenigsberg, 
in  Germany,  each  had  a  large  dredging 
cylinder  in  the  middle  and  two  main  shafts 
...  At  the  Paris  Exposition,  he'd  seen 
12-inch  rolled  beams  55  feet  long  ...  He 
wrote  about  steel  works  he'd  seen  in  Man- 
chester and  in  Essen;  about  wiremaking 
and  the  latest  developments  in  metal- 
lurgy. And  he  reported  all  this  in  elabo- 
rate, no-nonsense  detail,  down  to  the  nuts 
and  bolts,  supplying  dimensions  and 
weights,  accompanying  the  text  with  little 
sketches  and  diagrams.  Yours  affection- 
ately, V/ash.  A.  Roebling,  he'd  sign  them. 

It  was  during  this  period  that  his  father 
drew  up  plans  for  the  great  bridge.  John 
Roebling  had  come  over  from  Muhlhau-

sen,  Germany  in  1831,  at  the  age  of  24, 
armed  with  a  diploma  from  Berlin's  pres- 
tigious Royal  Polytechnic  Institute.  His 
mind  burned  with  ideas.  He  was  im- 
mensely ambitious.  He  had  no  use  for  the 
bureaucracy  of  the  Old  World.  Back  there, 
he  wrote  in  his  diary,  nothing  could  be 
accomplished  "without  first  having  an  army 
of  government  councilors,  ministers,  and 
other  functionaries  deliberate  about  it  for 
ten  years,  make  numerous  expensive 
journeys  by  post,  and  write  so  many  long 
reports  about  it,  that  for  the  amount  ex- 
pended for  all  this,  reckoning  compound 
interest  for  ten  years,  the  work  could  have 
been  completed." 

With  other  German  immigrants,  Roeb- 
ling had  founded  Saxonburg,  a  kind  of 
Utopian  farming  community  in  western 
Pennsylvania.  The  first  house  to  go  up 
was  his  own  and,  as  one  biographer  has 
noted,  "like  everything  he  ever  built,  it 
was  built  to  last."  Saxonburg  was  mod- 
estly successful,  but  Roebling  himself  was 
an  engineer,  a  builder,  more  than  a  farmer, 
and  pretty  soon  he  was  building:  He  built 


dams  and  locks.  He  built  aqueducts.  He 
started  manufacturing  wire  rope,  using  a 
process  then  known  only  in  Europe;  ul- 
timately., he  moved  both  the  business  and 
his  family  back  east  to  Trenton.  He  built 
bridges,  invariably  of  the  suspension  va- 
riety. "Suspension  bridges  kind  of  tickled 
his  fancy  as  being  somehow  'natural'  in 
the  way  they  expressed  function  through 
form,"  says  Bob  Gough  of  Roebling.  He 
was,  by  all  accounts,  something  of  a  ge- 
nius, and  before  long  he'd  built  a  name 
for  himself  as  perhaps  the  pre-eminent 
bridge  engineer  in  America.  And  in  1867, 
in  the  space  of  three  months,  he  designed 
the  Brooklyn  Bridge. 

The  Brooklyn  Bridge  today  looks  just 
about  as  John  Roebling  thought  it  ought 
to  look.  It  is  built  where  he  said  it  should 
be  built.  And  it  stands  today  in  conform- 
ity to  calculations  he  performed  that  said 
it  ought  to  stand.  But  in  1869,  while  he 
and  son  Washington  were  at  a  ferry  slip 
inspecting  the  site  for  the  Brooklyn  tower, 
an  incoming  boat  caught  him  unawares 
and  crushed  his  foot.  Tetanus  set  in.  He 


X        ALUMNI  MA(3AZINE  CONSORTIUM 


died.  He  never  built  the  bridge  it  was  his 
dream  to  build. 

His  son  Washington — 32  at  the  time,  a 
Civil  War  hero,  one  of  the  earliest  grad- 
uates of  Rensselaer  Polytechnic  Institute 
and  an  accompHshed  bridge  engineer  in 
his  own  right,  fresh  from  his  "post-grad- 
uate" engineering  vacation  in  Europe — 
did  it  instead. 

From  the  time  in  late  1869  when  work- 
ers began  preparing  the  site  for  the 
Brooklyn  caisson  to  the  time  in  the  spring 
of  1883  when  the  bridge  was  opened,  14 
years  passed.  It  took  a  year  just  to  sink 
each  3,000-ton  caisson  into  the  bed  of  the 
river;  six  years  to  finish  the  towers,  each 
of  which  consumed  some  40,000  cubic 
yards  of  masonry  mined  from  no  less  than 
20  quarries;  two  years  to  spin  14,000  miles 
of  three-sixteenth-inch  diameter  wire  into 
the  four  great  cables. 

The  period  saw  the  revelation  of  scan- 
dal after  scandal,  with  many  more  whis- 
pered for  each  one  proved;  the  notorious 
Tweed  Ring  had,  after  all,  played  a  major 
role  in  gaining  a  go-ahead  for  the  bridge, 


and  commercial  interests  had  supplied  the 
prime  impetus  for  its  construction  all  along. 
To  this  day,  its  cables  contain  221  tons  of 
wire  that  had  been  rejected  by  inspec- 
tors— "brittle  as  glass,"  Washington 
Roebling  called  a  sample  of  the  stuff 
brought  to  his  attention — yet  surrepti- 
tiously diverted  to  bridge  use.  The  cost  of 
the  bridge  soared  from  the  $7  miUion  John 
Roebling  had  forecast  to  more  than  twice 
that  figure,  $15  miUion. 

During  construction,  a  fire  broke  out 
deep  in  the  Brooklyn  caisson  and,  fed  by 
air  under  pressure,  ate  into  the  caisson's 
wood  beam  roof  for  two  weeks  after  it 
was  thought  to  be  extinguished;  ulti- 
mately, the  fire  ate  almost  a  third  of  the 
way  through  the  15-foot  thick  roof.  The 
burned-out  sections  were  cleaned  out  and 
buttressed  with  wood  and  cement. 

A  mysterious  ailment,  "caisson  dis- 
ease," took  the  hves  of  three  workers  and 
caused  awful  cramps  and  convulsions  in 
many  others.  "The  bends,"  we  know  now, 
are  caused  by  nitrogen  boihng  up  from 
the  blood  upon  too  sudden  a  release  of 


pressure — which  is  just  what  the  men  ex- 
perienced upon  emerging  from  the  pres- 
surized caisson.  Eighteen  months  into  the 
project,  Washington  Roebling  was  him- 
self afflicted.  It  brought  him  close  to  death 
and  apparently  contributed  to  a  mysteri- 
ous nervous  affliction  which  turned  him 
into  a  recluse. 

All  of  Roebhng's  engineering  staff  but 
one  remained  on  the  job  the  entire  14 
years  it  took  to  finish  it.  During  most  of 
that  time,  the  Chief  Engineer  never  so 
much  as  set  foot  at  the  construction  site; 
he  worked  out  of  his  home  in  Brooklyn 
Heights.  His  wife  Emily  therefore  came 
to  play  a  key  role  in  the  day-to-day  con- 
duct of  the  work,  as  private  secretary  and 
the  relayer  of  his  directives,  as  interme- 
diary and  fence-builder,  deftly  parrying 
attacks  on  her  husband  and  his  plans. 
Along  the  way,  she  apparently  amassed 
considerable  engineering  knowledge.  "She 
became,"  as  bridge  scholar  David  Mc- 
CuUough  told  a  gathering  of  the  Rens- 
selaer Alumni  Association  a  few  years  ago, 
"as  accomplished  a  civil  engineer  as  any- 
body on  the  job.  And  in  fact,  toward  the 
end  she  was  actually  making  decisions — 
quite  good  decisions."  Today,  a  plaque 
on  the  bridge  acknowledges  her  impor- 
tant role. 

All  told,  20  men,  out  of  the  thousands 
who  at  various  times  worked  on  it,  were 
lost  to  the  bridge:  Three  men  died  in  falls 
from  towers.  A  man  named  Cope  died 
when  his  leg  got  wrapped  around  the  drum 
of  a  hoisting  engine.  A  carpenter  was  killed 
by  a  falling  stone  .  .  . 

But  in  the  end,  the  bridge  got  built. 

A  nineteenth-century  engineer  once 
calculated — he  was,  plainly,  trying 
b.to  make  a  point — that  to  cross  a 
hundred  foot  gorge,  you  could  step  out 
across  a  wrought  iron  bar  nine  inches  wide, 
27  inches  deep,  and  weighing  44  tons;  or 
else  you  could  shimmy  across  along  a  thin 
steel  wire  weighing  13  pounds.  A  suspen- 
sion bridge  is  little  more  than  many  such 
wires  from  which  a  roadway  is  slung. 

Indeed,  the  thing  about  a  suspension 
bridge  apt  to  leave  the  most  forcible 
impression  is  that  there's  not  much  to  it: 
So  little  does  so  much.  A  cantilever  bridge, 
like  that  spanning  the  Firth  of  Forth  in 
Scotland,  say,  is  a  grand  affair  with  trusses 
and  girders  and  beams  enough  to  darken 
the  sky.  The  same  goes  for  an  arch  bridge, 
like  the  Bayonne  Bridge  linking  Staten 
Island  to  New  Jersey:  They  look  substan- 
tial, crowded  with  strength.  A  suspension 
bridge,  on  the  other  hand,  especially  at  a 
distance,  seems  ethereal,  ephemeral,  Ut-

ALUMNI  MAGAZINE  CONSORTIUM 


tie  more  than  the  vine  suspended  across 
the  stream  that  bridge  historians  guess  was 
its  original  form. 

Now  consider  crossing  that  flimsy  trail 
of  vine  hand  over  hand:  The  dead  weight 
of  your  body  is  not  the  only  load  the  vine 
must  endure.  Because,  unlike  a  log  thrown 
across  a  stream,  the  vine  lacks  rigidity. 
Conceivably,  even  if  your  weight  would 
not  break  it,  by  slinging  yourself  across 
you  could  set  it  to  twisting  and  shaking 
and  thrashing  so  much  that  finally  it  would 
break. 

Which  is  just  about  what  can  happen 
with  a  suspension  bridge.  In  The  Great 
Bridge,  David  McCuUough  has  written 
what  is  arguably  the  definitive  work  on 
the  subject  of  the  Brooklyn  Bridge.  And 
in  it  he  notes  that  before  about  1855,  when 
John  Roebling  finished  a  suspension  bridge 
for  heavy  railroad  trains  across  the  821- 
foot  Niagara  Gorge,  "suspension  bridges 
had  a  dubious  reputation  ...  So  many  of 
them  had  come  crashing  down  over  the 
years,  and  frequently  with  tragic  conse- 
quences. In  England  in  1831  a  suspension 
bridge  had  collapsed  under  the  feet  of 
marching  troops  ...  In  France  in  1850 
another  wire  bridge  had  failed  under  al- 
most identical  circumstances,  killing  two 
hundred  men.  In  America  a  number  of 
small  suspension  bridges  had  collapsed 
under  droves  of  cattle."  What  was  more, 
McCullough  added,  "nobody  understood 
quite  why  these  things  happened." 

Indeed,  even  70  years  and  more  after 
John  Roebling  drew  up  plans  for  his  East 
River  masterpiece,  the  design  of  suspen- 
sion bridges  apparently  remained  tricky 
enough  that  one  across  the  Tacoma  Nar- 
rows in  Washington  state  thrashed  itself 
to  death  in  a  high  wind.  "Galloping  Ger- 
tie" set  bridge  engineers  scurrying  back 
to  their  drawing  boards  to  review  their 
designs,  subjecting  bridge  models  to  wind 
tunnel  tests,  and  even  reinforcing  existing 
bridges  against  wind-bred  oscillations.  The 
2300-foot  Bronx-Whitestone  Bridge  in 
New  York  was  one  suspension  span  that 
got  structural  reinforcements.  The  Golden 
Gate  Bridge  in  San  Francisco  was  an- 
other. By  this  time,  of  course,  the  Brook- 
lyn Bridge  had  already  weathered  a  half- 
century's  worth  of  gales  with  scarcely  a 
shiver  of  protest. 

Back  in  1854,  John  RoebHng  had  stud- 
ied the  collapse  of  a  suspension  bridge  in 
West  Virginia,  ascribing  it  to  a  lack  of 
structural  stiffness,  just  the  sort  on  which 
the  Tacoma  Narrows  disaster  would  later 
be  blamed.  Accordingly,  when  it  came 
time  to  plan  the  Brooklyn  Bridge  he  was 
not,  as  he  wrote  in  his  proposal  for  it, 


"disposed  to  underrate  the  great  force  of 
a  severe  gale,  when  directed  toward  the 
suspended  work,  and  I  am  also  fully  aware 
that  herein  lies  the  great  danger  to  all 
Suspension  Bridges  of  ordinary  design." 
His  design,  he  hastened  to  add,  was  not 
ordinary. 

"To  guard  against  vertical  and  horizon- 
tal oscillations,"  he  wrote,  "and  to  insure 
that  degree  of  Stiffness  in  the  flooring 
which  is  absolutely  necessary  to  meet  the 
effects  of  violent  gales  in  such  an  exposed 
situation,  I  have  provided  six  Hnes  of  trusses 
.  .  ."In  fact,  he  declared,  the  whole  bridge 
could  be  regarded  as  "a  huge  truss  of  1600 
feet  span,  and  of  a  depth  of  150  ft.  The 
Towers  form  one  supporting  element,  the 
framing  of  the  floor  forms  the  second,  and 
the  extensive  system  of  stays  forms  the 
third." 

The  diagonal  stays  to  which  he  referred 
radiate  out  from  the  top  of  each  tower 
and  help  support  the  roadway.  In  a  bold 
exercise  of  conceptual  salesmanship  in- 
tended to  point  up  the  strength  built  into 
his  design,  he  asked  his  readers  to  imagine 
the  four  supporting  cables  removed  en- 
tirely! Thanks  to  the  stays,  he  claimed, 
"the  bridge  would  sink  in  the  centre,  but 
would  not  fall." 

In  an  1898  article  in  Railroad  Gazette, 
Roebling's  son  Washington  would  cast 
doubt  on  the  merits  of  stay  construction. 


neighbors,  the  Manhattan  and  the  Wil- 
liamsburg bridges:  "The  thing  just  sits 
there." 

In  his  proposal  for  the  Brooklyn 
Bridge,  John  Roebling  predicted  that 
"its  most  conspicuous  features,  the 
great  Towers,  will  serve  as  landmarks  to 
the  adjoining  cities,  &  they  will  be  enti- 
tled to  be  ranked  as  national  monuments. 
As  a  great  work  of  art,  &  as  a  successful 
specimen  of  advanced  Bridge  Engineer- 
ing, this  structure  will  forever  testify  to 
the  energy,  enterprise  &  wealth  of  that 
Community  which  shall  secure  its  erec- 
tion." 

Not  only  that,  he  might  have  forecast 
with  equal  confidence,  the  bridge  would 
be  easy  to  keep  clean. 

Roebling  was  "very  smart  about  de- 
tails," says  Bob  Gough.  "And  as  Mies  van 
der  Rohe  once  said,  'God  is  in  the  details.' 
Every  detail  of  the  Brooklyn  Bridge  is 
really  well  thought  out  and  executed." 
Rain,  he  reports,  "hits  it  and  rolls  off  .  .  . 
There  are  no  pools  of  sitting  water,  no 
drains  to  clog  and  back  up  and  cause  rot 
in  the  structure."  The  bridge  is  easy  to 
maintain.  "There's  nothing  in  it  that  is 
complex.  It's  just  nuts  and  bolts.  I  think 
that's  why  people  have  an  affinity  for  the 
bridge.  Everything  is  so  simple."  So  spare. 
And  despite  the  bridge's  structural  rigid-

old  photo  today,  you'd

always  has  been. 


"The  theoretic  complications  introduced 
by  stays  are  so  numerous,"  he  wrote,  "that 
probably  no  engineer  would  venture  to 
use  them,  even  for  the  sake  of  stiffness." 
Of  course  that  was  years  later.  And  the 
fact  is,  whatever  the  scientific  shortcom- 
ings of  his  father's  reasoning,  the  strength 
and  solidity  of  the  structure  are  incon- 
testable. As  Bob  Gough  says  in  com- 
paring it,  favorably,  to  its  East  River 


ity,  reports  Gough,  it  is  exceedingly  light — 
just  four  tons  per  linear  foot,  as  compared 
to  14  tons  per  foot  for  the  "clunky"  Wil- 
Hamsburg  Bridge,  and  12  for  the  Man- 
hattan. Which  means  that  much  less  to 
paint  and  keep  up. 

Gough  occupies  a  large  corner  office  in 
an  old  office  building  on  Worth  Street,  in 
lower  Manhattan.  With  its  banks  of  big 
windows,  it  ought  to  be  bathed  in  light. 


APRIL  1983 


XIII 


But  the  walls  are  painted  brown,  and  the 
windows  are  covered  with  drawn  Vene- 
tian blinds,  and  there's  a  big  bulletin  board 
that  obstructs  one  of  them  almost  en- 
tirely. For  two  hours  this  morning  Gough 
has  patiently  answered  questions  about 
the  bridge,  supplying  a  capsule  history, 
giving  details  of  its  construction.  And  now 
it  seems  he's  had  enough:  The  bridge  awaits 
us. 

A  ten-story  elevator  descent  and  a  ten- 
minute  walk  through  lunch-hour  streets, 
and  we're  out  on  the  bridge,  walking  up 
the  incUned  concrete  approach  to  the 
bridge  proper.  Gough,  in  his  sky-blue 
mountain  parka  and  his  tweed  cap,  at  last 
freed  from  the  constraints  of  the  office, 
seems  visibly  looser,  more  at  home.  "You 
want  to  see  the  cable?"  he  asks  as  we 
approach  the  steps  leading  up  to  the 
promenade. 

Abruptly,  he  swings  a  leg  over  the  rail- 
ing that  separates  the  promenade  from 
the  swarms  of  cars  barreling  down  the 
roadway.  I  follow.  We  have  to  inch  along 
a  narrow  curb,  maybe  ten  or  twelve  inches 
wide,  as  three  lanes  of  cars  whiz  by  beside 
us.  They  think  we're  nuts,  I  think,  a  pair 
of  New  York  crazies  playing  in  the  traffic. 
But  then  we  scurry  off  the  curb  and  under 
the  promenade  into  the  underbelly  of  the 
bridge.  "Watch  your  head!"  says  Gough 
as  we  clamber  under  and  around  the  steel- 
work. Under  trusses,  down  ladders,  along 
catwalks,  and  then  we  are  there — that 
magic  junction,  that  place  of  focused 
power,  where  the  mathematical  abstrac- 
tion that  is  the  catenary  curve  dissolves 
into  spaghetti  bundles  of  wires.  Here,  out 
of  the  wire-wrapped  sheathing  of  the  ca- 
ble emerge  19  distinct  strands,  each  con- 
sisting of  278  individual  wires.  Here  in  this 
narrow,  confining,  limestone-lined  vault — 
"the  cable  pit,"  Gough  calls  it — the  lines, 
of  force  of  some  engineering  diagram  are 
transmuted  into  steel. 

Later,  coming  back  out  of  the  cable  pit, 
I  inspect  the  underside  of  the  bridge.  It 
is  impossibly  straightforward,  transparent 
in  its  workings.  The  whole  thing  is  put 
together  like  a  child's  erector  set,  its  nuts 
and  bolts  scavenged  from  some  Brob- 
dingnagian  mechanic.  The  main  cables,  I 
notice,  are  attached  to  the  wire  rope  sus- 
penders with  a  coupling  that  looks  just  as 
it  might  in  a  quick,  offhand  sketch  in- 
tended to  show  the  concept  of  the  coup- 
ling: a  rounded  band  of  metal  with  a  hole 
in  it.  Another  piece  into  which  the  sus- 
penders fit,  also  with  a  hole  through  it. 
The  whole  assembly  is  fastened  together 
with  a  big  nut  and  a  big  bolt:  What  you 
see  is  what  you  get. 


Simple,  straightforward  design  of- 
fers no  guarantee  of  perfection, 
however:  In  1901, 18  years  after  the 
bridge's  completion,  Washington  Roeb- 
Ung  wrote  to  John  Shea,  then  Commis- 
sioner of  Bridges  for  the  Borough  of  Man- 
hattan, "Two  adjacent  suspender  rods 
seem  to  have  been  broken  for  some  time 
past,"  he  said.  "I  learn  also  that  single 
suspension  rods  have  broken  on  previous 
occasions." 

In  the  Brooklyn  Bridge,  the  cables  sup- 
port the  bridge  roadway  by  three  distinct 
means.  Along  most  of  its  length,  the  road- 
way hangs  from  wire  rope  suspenders.  For 
a  brief  stretch  where  the  cables  dip  under 
the  road  near  each  anchorage,  it  actually 
sits  on  the  cables.  Finally,  around  mid- 
span,  the  roadway  hangs  from  solid  steel 
rods.  And  these  suspender  rods,  as  Bob 
Gough  says,  "have  been  cracking  since 
the  bridge  was  built.  We've  got  reports 
going  back  to  1890."  (His  men  keep  re- 
placement blanks  on  hand  that  can  be  cut 
to  size  when  a  cracked  one  is  found.) 

In  his  letter  to  the  bridge  commissioner, 
Washington  Roebling  wrote  that  "the  true 
cause  of  their  fracture  [is  not]  far  to  seek." 
The  roadway  trusses  to  which  they  attach 
at  a  slip  joint  move  as  the  bridge  expands 
and  contracts  with  the  weather.  Appar- 
ently the  freedom  of  motion  built  into  the 
joint  was  insufficient.  Roebhng  had  a  fix 
in  mind,  he  told  the  commissioner.  But 
no  one,  it  seems,  ever  acted  upon  it. 

In  any  event,  this  nagging  Uttle  problem 
is  at  last  being  treated.  In  1978,  the  firm 
of  Steinman,  Boynton,  Gronquist  &  Bird- 
sail  was  hired  to  inspect  the  bridge  and 
draw  up  any  rehabilitation  plans  neces- 
sary. A  Steinman  engineer  who  wishes 
not  to  give  his  name  said  that  the  inspec- 
tion, carried  out  in  the  summer  of  1979, 
did  reveal  three  or  four  broken  suspender 
rods.  Some  thought  was  given  to  replacing 
the  solid  suspenders  with  the  kind  of  wire 
rope  suspenders  used  elsewhere  on  the 
bridge.  But  that,  he  said,  would  allow  the 
structure  too  much  flex.  What's  being  done 
instead  is  to  replace  the  existing  slip  joints, 
or  trunnions — which  permit  only  longi- 
tudinal swing  of  the  rods — with  spherical 
bearings  that  can  accommodate  to  motion 
in  any  direction. 

Other  rehabilitation  measures  are  in 
store  for  the  bridge,  among  them: 

•  The  cable  pit  will  be  opened  up  to 
permit  easier  maintenance  of  the  cable. 
Broken  wires,  in  what  the  Steinman  en- 
gineer terms  "a  delicate  operation,"  will 
be  replaced. 

•  The  New  York  and  Brooklyn  towers 
will  be  protected  with  cofferdams. 


•  Wire  rope  suspenders  and  stays  will 
be  replaced. 

•  The  bridge  will  get  a  paint  job. 
After  that,  said  the  Steinman  man,  the 

bridge  may  be  good  for  another  100  years. 

Maybe.  Anthony  Atheling,  for 
one,  is  not  so  sanguine. 
For  years,  Atheling  worked 
mostly  abroad — in  Thailand,  Brazil,  Li- 
beria. Saudi  Arabia,  and  elsewhere — on 
big  construction  projects.  He  worked  on 
harbors,  bridges,  roads.  Then,  he  says,  "I 
got  tired  of  working  overseas.  I  thought 
I  would  sort  of  redomesticate  myself."  So 
three  years  ago,  he  took  a  job  as  Acting 
Engineer-in-Charge  in  the  Division  of 
Bridge  Operations  of  the  City  of  New 
York's  Department  of  Transportation, 
East  River  Bridges  Section.  His  boss  is 
Bob  Gough.  And  while  Gough  is  respon- 
sible for  all  the  city's  bridges,  Atheling 
cares  solely  for  the  four  big  bridges,  in- 
cluding the  Brooklyn,  that  span  the  East 
River. 

But  now,  at  age  55,  all  he's  looking  for 
is  the  chance  to  get  out. 

He  was  born  in  Youngstown,  Ohio, 
raised  in  Brooklyn.  Brooklyn  is  where  he 
is  now,  at  390  Kent  Avenue,  the  home  of 
East  River  Bridges;  you  can  see  three  of 
them  from  his  window,  the  Williamsburg 
looming  just  outside.  He  has  brown,  al- 
most Oriental  eyes,  set  deep  into  a  gaunt, 
high  cheekboned  face.  It  is  an  unforget- 
table face,  and  right  now  it  is  angry.  Over 
the  telephone,  he'd  seemed  merely  tired, 
resigned.  But  seeing  him  in  person,  you 
realize  his  feelings  go  much  deeper. 

At  first,  he  is  coolly  official.  His  job  is 
to  maintain  the  four  East  River  bridges, 
he  says,  adding:  "In  theory."  Because  the 
fact  is,  he  says  reluctantly,  his  department 
has  been  reduced  to  "a  reaction  squad." 
His  work  force  is  "pretty  well  decimated. 
It  used  to  be  about  120,  but  I  don't  sup- 
pose there  are  more  than  70  now."  Now, 
all  he  and  his  thinned-out  staff  can  do  is 
react  to  citizen  complaints  as  they  arise. 
Regular  maintenance  of  the  bridges,  any 
of  the  bridges,  is  out.  "Now,"  he  says, 
"we're  not  much  more  than  janitors." 

Yes,  he  admits,  the  city  has  been  faced 
with  the  most  serious  budget  crisis  in  its 
crisis-ridden  history,  with  every  depart- 
ment forced  to  endure  brutal  cuts.  But 
don't  they  realize,  he  asks,  that  while 
"you're  saving  maintenance  dollars,  sooner 
or  later  you  have  to  make  it  up  with  a 
capital  outlay?" 

But  money  and  manpower  are  not  the 
whole  problem.  There  is  a  crisis  of  mo- 
rale.  Bureaucratic  and  political  factors
