make  it  impossible,  he  claims,  for  his  men 
to  do  their  jobs.  An  organizational  re- 
shuffling some  years  back  pulled  Bridges 
out  of  Public  Works  and  into  Highways — 
which  is  run,  he  says  with  thinly  veiled 
contempt,  by  mere  "laborers."  Taking  care 
of  a  complex  structure  like  a  bridge,  John 
RoebUng's  or  any  other,  demands  elec- 
tricians, oilers,  carpenters — skilled  crafts- 
men. But  in  the  new  set-up,  AtheUng  says. 


City-organized  bridges  maintenance  sec- 
tion is  not  to  be  provided  with  such  easily 
accessible  machine  shop  facilities,"  he'd 
written  at  the  time,  "then  the  time  might 
have  arrived,  at  last,  for  the  city  to  sell 
the  Brooklyn  Bridge  to  someone  who  can 
properly  care  for  it." 

He  looks  up,  angrily:  "The  administra- 
tors make  the  decisions  here,  not  the  en- 
gineers, not  the  craftsmen  .  .  .  What  suits 


Suddenly,  on  May  24,  1883,  Brooklyn 
was  just  a  five'tninute  train  ride  away. 
But  even  in  1925  shown  here,  the  ferries  still  ran. 


he's  got  master  carpenters  who  have  to 
report  to  people  who  are  little  more  than 
pothole  fillers  risen  up  through  the  ranks. 
"They  don't  know  a  freaking  thing  about 
bridges!"  It  leaves  his  men  angry,  de- 
moralized. One  of  them,  in  the  office  while 
Atheling  is  talking,  nods  his  head  in 
agreement. 

Why.  he  can't  so  much  as  order  the 
logbooks  his  foremen  need,  he  laments — 
though  they  form  part  of  his  department's 
legal  record;  they  were  deleted  from  his 
budget  request.  Loose  slats  on  the  Brook- 
lyn Bridge  promenade  went  undetected, 
he'd  written  in  an  intradepartmental  memo 
just  a  few  days  before,  "because  of  end- 
less diversion  of  the  East  River  Bridges 
carpenters  to  non-bridge  work,  such  as 
.  .  .  remodeling  of  40  Worth  Street  offices 
and  erecting  of  temporary  fencing  around 
an  archeological  "dig.'  "  Oh,  and  what 
about  that  wire  rope  stay  that  snapped  a 
while  back,  killing  a  Japanese  photogra- 
pher? "Nobody  is  accountable!" 

Me  wants  to  do  his  job,  Atheling  insists, 
"but  I  have  neither  the  people  nor  the 
equipment  to  do  it."  He  shuffles  amongst 
his  papers  for  a  memo  he'd  written  a  cou- 
ple of  years  before,  complaining  that  his 
department  was  having  trouble  gaining 
access  to  basic  machine  shop  equipment 
traditionally  under  its  jurisdiction.  "If  the 


them  in  their  management  is  what  decides 
the  lifeblood  of  the  whole  freaking  city 
.  .  .  The  engineers  are  handcuffed." 

But  1883  was  a  different  age.  It  was 
the  age  of  the  engineer.  It  was  a 
time  when,  as  David  McCullough 
has  written,  to  Americans  "a  bridge 
seemed  such  a  magnificent  example  of 
man's  capacity  to  master  the  forces  of  na- 
ture .  .  .  Building  a  bridge  seemed  such 
a  clean,  heroic  thing  for  a  man  to  do." 

The  day  after  John  Roebling's  funeral 
in  1869,  the  Brooklyn  Eagle  commented: 
"The  great  boast  of  this  land  is  twofold — 
the  political  works  of  the  [Founding] 
Fathers,  and  the  material  triumphs  of  sci- 
ence, of  which  Roebling  was,  with  scarcely 
any  exception,  the  greatest  hero  .  .  .  One 
such  life  as  Roebling's  was  worth  more 
than  those  of  a  whole  convention  full  of 
jabbering  and  wrangling  politicians." 

John  Roebling  was  the  kind  of  man  who, 
when  he  came  over  on  the  boat  from  Ger- 
many, was  up  early  every  morning,  learn- 
ing "the  name  of  every  sail,  every  stay, 
brace,  bowline,  halyard,  every  rope  and 
how  each  one  worked.  And  he  made  dia- 
grams to  be  sure  he  understood."  That 
was  how  David  McCullough  saw  him  re- 
vealed in  his  diary  of  the  voyage.  He  was 
the  kind  of  man  for  whom,  as  his  son 


would  write  after  his  death,  "the  question 
.  .  .  was  never  how  to  do  a  thing,  but 
which  method  to  select  of  the  number  that 
occurred  to  him  .  .  .  The  originality  of  his 
mind  was  such  as  to  forbid  it  from  imi- 
tating even  itself."  He  was  a  dreamer,  a 
thinker,  a  builder,  a  doer. 

Not  that  he  was  an  innocent.  He  knew 
how  power  worked.  He'd  managed  to 
make  plenty  of  money.  And  he  knew  how 
to  sell.  Indeed,  he  was  the  first  person  to 
"sell"  the  Brooklyn  Bridge. 

In  his  1867  proposal,  he  appealed  to  the 
innate  conservativeness  of  businessmen 
skeptical  of  a  potentially  risky  engineer- 
ing feat:  The  cable  cars  he  planned  to  run 
across  the  bridge  represented  a  technol- 
ogy "perfectly  simple  and  perfectly  well 
understood.  There  is  no  novel  feature  and 
no  experiment  involved  in  this  arrange- 
ment." He  appealed  to  their  ideals.  He 
appealed  to  their  imaginations.  He  ap- 
pealed to  their  pocketbooks.  The  ferries 
then  plying  the  East  River  between 
Brooklyn  and  New  York  would  be  placed 
in  financial  jeopardy  by  the  bridge?  Not 
at  all,  he  said,  there'd  be  plenty  of  busi- 
ness for  everybody.  The  bridge  would  "not 
set  up  a  dangerous  rivalry  to  the  existing 
Ferry  interest."  Rather,  it  would  create 
business.  Even  aesthetic,  or  what  we'd 
call  today  urban  renewal,  concerns  did 
not  escape  his  attention.  "This  work  will 
greatly  beautify  and  improve  [the  area 
around  the  New  York  terminal]  which  ap- 
pears to  need  it  more  than  any  other." 

It  was  a  model  of  high-minded  sales- 
manship, at  once  practical,  thorough, 
confident,  technically  imaginative,  and 
frankly  commercial.  It  was  a  sales  pro- 
posal, yes.  But  it  was  the  sales  proposal 
of  a  visionary,  a  man  of  values  and  ideals, 
rather  than  that  of  a  door-to-door  sales- 
man. There  is  a  powerful  imagination  at 
work  in  it,  one  single-mindedly  intent  on 
having  other  eyes  see  what  his  did,  other 
minds  grasp  his  vision. 

And  the  fact  is,  he  was  right  in  his 
promises  and  predictions,  over  and  over 
again.  The  cable  cars  worked  fine.  The 
ferries  did  indeed  prosper;  the  last  of  them 
wasn't  withdrawn  from  service  until  1942. 
He  predicted  New  York  would  overtake 
London  and  Liverpool  as  a  commercial 
center.  And  it  did.  He  said  tunnels  would 
one  day  be  sunk  under  the  East  River. 
And  they  were. 

"The  contemplated  Work,  when  con- 
structed in  accordance  with  my  designs, 
will  not  only  be  the  greatest  Bridge  in 
existence,  but  it  will  be  the  great  Engi- 
neering Work  of  this  Continent  &  of  the 
Age."  And  it  was. 


XVI        ALUMNI  MAGAZINE  CONSORTIUM 


JacksonL.  Dur/cee,  '43: 
His  Cables  Bridge  the  World 


-A 


t  company  meetings  and 
public  hearings,  I'm  what's 
known  as  the  elbow  man. 
When  someone  turns  to  the  guy  at  his 
elbow  for  counsel  on  a  sticky  technical 
question,  I'm  that  guy." 

As  a  consulting  structural  engineer, 
Jackson  L.  Durkee,  '43  CE,  '51  PCE,  has 
served  as  elbow  man  scores  of  times,  from 
hearings  on  the  Kansas  City  Hyatt  Re- 
gency Hotel  skywalk  failure,  to  the  cur- 
rent rehabilitation  of  New  York  City's  four 
East  River  bridges,  including  the  century- 
old  Brooklyn  Bridge. 

In  public  works  projects,  he  says,  it's 
common  to  bring  in  experts  to  advise  on 
technical  questions.  In  the  case  of  the  NYC 
bridge  rehabilitation,  he  counsels  the  NYC 
Department  of  Transportation,  reviewing 
the  feasibility  studies,  tests,  specifications 
and  reports  of  the  bridge  rehabilitation 
engineers  and  contractors. 

"Engineering  decisions,"  he  says,  "are 
not  always  cut-and-dried.  For  example, 
three  consulting  engineers  will  render  three 
distinct  opinions  on  a  given  question.  All 
this  is  quite  legitimate  and  necessary." 
After  all,  he  adds,  when  your  dentist  rec- 
ommends bridgework  (the  pun  was  irre- 
sistible), you'd  be  wise  to  get  the  opinions 
of  a  couple  of  other  specialists  before  pro- 
ceeding. 

Normally,  consultants'  reputations  are 
decades  in  the  making.  Durkee's  28  years 
as  chief  construction  engineer  at  Bethle- 
hem Steel,  the  last  ten  of  them  as  chief 
bridge  engineer,  and  his  participation  on 
many  of  the  world's  most  ambitious  con- 
struction projects  are  why  his  counsel  is 
today  in  such  great  demand.  He  has  been 
"totally  immersed"  in  seven  suspension 
and  scores  of  other  bridge  projects,  in- 
cluding the  Tacoma  Narrows  Bridge  sec- 
ond span,  Philadelphia's  Walt  Whitman 
Bridge,  Florida's  Sunshine  Skyway,  and 
the  gargantuan  maintenance  hangars  at' 
Jan  Smuts  airport  in  Johannesburg.  He  is 
also  consulting  on  the  projected  Messina 
Strait  suspension  bridge  linking  the  Ital- 
ian mainland  with  Sicily.  "Under  consid- 
eration for  more  than  20  years,"  he  re- 


The  1982  winner  of  ASCE's  gold  medal 
for  structural  engineering. 

ports,  "the  Messina  project — consisting 
of  a  single  span  of  10,827  feet,  and  1,250- 
foot  towers  to  be  built  on  earthquake- 
prone  strata — is  my  greatest  challenge  to 
date." 

But  Durkee  is  best  known  for  devel- 
oping and  applying  a  revolutionary  sus- 
pension-bridge cable  system  that  uses  par- 
allel-wire strands  (PWS)  rather  than  the 
traditional  helical  configuration.  In  fact, 
lifelong  advancement  of  bridge  design  and 
his  PWS  breakthrough  have  won  him  the 
1982  Earnest  E.  Howard  Award,  the  gold 
medal  of  structural  engineering,  which  is 
given  by  the  American  Society  of  Civil 
Engineers  (ASCE).  In  winning  the  How- 
ard, Durkee  joins  a  host  of  distinguished 
bridge  designers,  including  David  Stein- 
man  and  O.H.  Ammann.  "I  feel  out- 
classed by  the  past  recipients,"  he  says, 
adding,  "but  I  decided  not  to  turn  down 
the  award." 

It  was  in  1959,  while  standing  atop  a 
bridge  tower,  gazing  at  a  suspension  ca- 
ble, that  Durkee  had  the  idea  of  making 
strands  having  parallel  (rather  than  heli- 


cal) alignment  for  use  in  construction  of 
suspension-bridge  cables. 

This — a  breakthrough?  Certainly,  when 
you  consider  that  the  strength-to-mass 
characteristics  of  PWS  cable  are  consid- 
erably greater  than  in  helical-strand  ca- 
bles. PWS  cables  also  combine  the  erec- 
tion advantages  of  helical-strand  with  the 
superior  in-place  characteristics  of  par- 
allel-wire cables.  Developed  in  the  late 
1960s,  PWS  cable  suddenly  rendered  ob- 
solescent the  traditional  "aerial  spinning" 
method  of  bridge  cable  construction  de- 
veloped 125  years  earlier  by  John  Roe- 
bling,  designer  of  the  Brooklyn  and  other 
bridges  of  the  day. 

Completed  in  1969,  the  Newport  (RI) 
Bridge  was  the  first  suspension  bridge  to 
use  Durkee's  PWS  cables.  Others  fol- 
lowed; for  example,  the  second  Chesa- 
peake Bay  Bridge  and  the  many-spanned 
suspension  bridges  of  Japan's  vast  Hon- 
shu-Shikoku  crossing  project. 

In  spite  of  his  global  reputation,  Dur- 
kee's consulting  engineering  firm  is  a  one- 
man  show  run  from  his  home  in  Bethle- 
hem, PA.  Naturally,  he  calls  upon  spe- 
cialty sub-consultants  as  his  commissions 
dictate.  "It  keeps  the  perspective  fresh," 
he  says. 

"Engineering  is  not  merely  grinding 
numbers  and  coming  up  with  textbook  an- 
swers to  physical  problems."  More  im- 
portant, he  contends,  " — and  far  more 
difficult — we  must  apply  our  accumulated 
judgment  to  the  practical  implementation 
of  these  numerical  solutions." 

Though  he  recently  turned  60,  Durkee 
believes  his  best  years  lie  ahead.  "I'm 
shooting  to  be  a  scratch  golfer,  for  one 
thing."  (When  his  work  takes  him  to  the 
British  Isles,  he  plays  the  famous  Old 
Course  at  St.  Andrews,  Scotland.)  Ca- 
reer-wise, until  now,  he  says  with  char- 
acteristic understatement,  "  'the  past  is 
prologue.'  The  next  20  years  should  be  a 
bit  more  productive." 

Durkee's  '43  classmates  will  be  back  on 
campus  in  force  for  their  40th  reunion  June 
2-5.  ASCE's  gold  medal  winner  plans  to 
be  among  them.  Says  he,  "Save  me  a  seat!"

cently he  retired  as  senior  engineer  from  Lib-

gineer. Last  spring,  the  Healys  and  the  Fred 
Swans  took  a  jungle  cruise  up  the  Orinoco  River 
in  Venezuela.

retired  to  Florida,  but  spend  the  summers  in 
North  Conway,  NH.

Maggiacomo  Tapped 
By  Two  Halls  of  Fame 

Often  the  crowning  achievement  of  an 
athlete's  career  is  induction  into  a  hall  of 
fame.  Last  fall,  Antonio  ("Jock")  Mag- 
giacomo, '32,  of  Cranston,  RI,  was  in- 
ducted into  two!  Not  only  was  he  tapped 
.for  the  Cranston  Hall  of  Fame,  but  he  was 
also  named  to  the  Providence  Gridiron 
Hall  of  Fame. 

Jock  Maggiacomo  has  earned  his  lau- 
rels. As  head  football  line  coach  at  Cran- 
ston High  School  from  1936  to  1953,  he 
helped  his  team  win  seven  divisional  titles 
and  three  state  championships.  The  base- 
ball team  he  coached  for  16  years  at  Cran- 
ston High  won  four  state  championships. 

A  founder  and  treasurer  of  the  People 
to  People  Sports  Committee  of  Rhode  Is- 
land, he  has  directed  the  activities  of  two 
groups  of  Rhode  Island  young  people  in 
several  Latin  American  countries.  Two 
years  ago,  Maggiacomo  was  designated 
by  former  president  Ford  as  "Sports  Am- 
bassador" of  the  U.S.  in  appreciation  of 
his  20  years  of  participation  in  interna- 
tional sports  competition,  contributing  to 
international  friendship,  goodwill  and 
understanding  through  sports. 

A  faculty  member  at  Cranston  High 
School  East  for  25  years  and  science  de- 
partment chairman  at  Cranston  West  for 
six  years,  Maggiacomo  holds  a  master's 
degree  in  education  from  Rhode  Island 
College.  From  1936  to  1978  he  was  treas- 
urer and  vice  president  of  the  Greater 
Providence  Deposit  and  Trust  (including 
12  years  in  active  service  after  he  had  re- 


Hall  of  Fame  president  honors 
Maggiacomo  (right). 

tired  from  the  Cranston  school  system). 

He  also  has  been  active  in  community 
affairs,  serving  with  the  Cranston  Mental 
Health  Association,  the  Cranston  Broth- 
erhood Committee,  the  Cranston  Rotary 
and  the  American  Cancer  Society. 

During  the  hall  of  fame  induction  cer- 
emonies, Maggiacomo  was  cited  as  being 
"respected  and  admired  by  all  as  an  ex- 
emplar of  the  scholar-athlete,  who  has  also 
had  a  distinguished  business  career."

since  1980.  Previously  he  taught  at  St.  John's 
Preparatorv'  School  in  Danvers  and  Central  New

His  Camera  Captures 
The  Beauty  of  Nature 

Award-winning  photographer  Lincoln 
Nutting,  '43.  of  Grand  Island,  NY,  is  for 
the  birds — for  the  flowers  and  for  the  en- 
vironment. 

An  accomplished  naturalist  as  well  as 
photographer,  Nutting  knows  how  to  cap- 
ture the  heart  of  Mother  Nature.  The  Buf- 
falo News  Magazine  found  his  brilliant 
spring  flower  photos  so  realistic  that  it  ran 
a  three-page  spread.  And  the  publishers 
of  Wildflowers  of  Eastern  America  (Gal- 
ahad Books,  New  York  City,  1981)  se- 
lected his  wildflower  photographs  for  their 
book  jackets. 

How  did  a  former  chemist  with  Hooker 
Chemical  Company  get  so  involved  with 
flora  and  fauna?  Says  Line,  "Nature  study 
and  photography  have  been  part  of  my 
life  since  I  was  a  boy.  When  I  retired  in 
1970,  it  seemed  logical  to  develop  my  other 
talents." 

Nutting  has  developed  his  outside  in- 
terests into  a  fine  art.  Photo  Researchers, 
a  company  which  sells  slides  to  publishers, 
holds  600  of  his  nature  slides.  To  date, 
his  biggest  artistic-commercial  success  has 
been  a  slide  of  a  puffin  taken  on  Machias 
Seal  Island,  ME. 

Besides  participating  in  winter  bird 
counts  and  nature  tours.  Line  has  pho- 
tographed bats  in  an  abandoned  mine,  has 
made  a  publicity  film  for  the  Tifft  Farm 
Nature  Preserve  and  has  given  illustrated 


Getting  ready  to  shoot. 

nature  talks  to  garden  clubs.  Scouts  and 
school  groups.  Last  summer  he  entered  a 
photo  entitled  "Two  Bridges"  in  the  Grand 
Island  Art  Society  Exhibition.  It  took  first 
prize. 

Line  is  active  in  many  nature,  orni- 
thological and  conservation  groups  in  the 
Buffalo  area.  "My  wife,  Barbara,  shares 
my  interests,"  he  reports.  "We  have  served 
as  co-directors  of  Audubon  Wildlife  Films. 
Recently  she  helped  me  gather  material 
for  the  Lillian  Fairchild  Travel  Talks." 

Daughter  Mary  Lyn  appears  to  have 
inherited  at  least  one  of  Dad's  talents.  She 
is  majoring  in  art  at  Mt.  Holyoke  College.

field,  MA.  Formerly  he  ran  the  power  trans- 
former product  section  for  Canadian  General

versity.

opment Corporation  in  Bradenton,  FL,  a  firm

Dick  Bran  and  his  warm-up  bats. 

Bratt's  Bat 

Is  Making  a  Hit! 

In  1982  the  American  League  (AL)  hit 
nearly  40  percent  more  home  runs  than 
the  National  League,  climaxing  a  six-year 
upward  trend  in  AL  home  run  superior- 
ity. A  fluke?  Dick  Bratt,  '59,  doesn't  think 
so. 

Dick  is  president  of  Bratt  Corp.  of  Hol- 
den,  MA,  a  firm  which  makes  weighted 
warm-up  bats.  Since  1979  he  has  been  giv- 


ing the  American  League  the  exclusive 
use  of  his  bats,  and  the  upsurge  in  AL 
home  runs  has  been  astonishing.  In  one 
year  alone,  the  AL  home  run  total  went 
from  1,140  to  2,007! 

Who  uses  Bratt's  bats?  Jim  Rice  of  the 
Boston  Red  Sox,  for  one.  And  George 
Foster,  formerly  of  the  Cincinnati  Reds, 
for  another.  Before  1979  both  leagues  used 
the  bats,  and  both  players  were  home  run 
champions  for  their  respective  leagues  in 
1977  and  1978.  Rice  is  quoted  as  saying, 
"I  might  have  hit  a  dozen  fewer  home 
runs  and  20  to  30  [percentage]  points  lower 
if  I  hadn't  discovered  this  bat."  He  says 
he  likes  it  because  it's  balanced. 

Bratt,  a  product  engineer  at  Norton  Co., 
Worcester,  contends  that  his  balanced, 
weighted  bat  is  better  than  the  conven- 
tional hard-rubber  doughnut  model  for 
building  strength  and  loosening  muscles. 

"If  you  use  a  doughnut,  the  end- 
weighted  bat  swings  itself,"  he  says.  "With 
the  Bratt  bat,  you  have  to  move  the  bat 
yourself." 

The  bat,  developed  by  Bratt's  late  father, 
Leonard,  has  an  ash  handle.  Its  plastic 
barrel  is  filled  with  sand  or  alumina  zir- 
conia.  Foam  and  a  wooden  plug  hold  in 
the  sand.  Used  for  exercise — not  hitting — 
the  bat  comes  in  nine  weights,  from  45  to 
90  ounces. 

Bratt,  a  die-hard  Red  Sox  fan,  has  great 
confidence  in  his  company's  product,  be- 
cause he  feels  it  produces  results.  He  re- 
ports, "We  were  watching  the  '75  World 
Series  on  TV,  when  Bernie  Carbo  hit  a 
pair  of  pinch-hit  homers  for  the  Red  Sox. 
Both  times  they  showed  him  using  our 
warm-up  bat  before  he  went  to  the  plate. 
We  celebrated  until  four  in  the  morning!"

space. Recently  promoted  to  first  lieutenant  in 
the  U.S.  Air  Force,  he  is  stationed  in  Albu- 
querque, NM. 

Paula  Mesite  is  studying  for  her  MSEE  in 
communications  at  MIT.

COMPLETED  CAREERS 


Frank  Forsberg,  '15,  a  retired  chief  metallurg- 
ist and  consultant  to  the  Gillette  Co.  in  Boston, 
died  on  October  29,  1982,  in  Worcester.  He 
was  89. 

A  Worcester  native,  he  graduated  with  a 
BSCE.  He  belonged  to  Sigma  Xi  and  Tau  Beta 
Pi. 

During  his  early  career,  he  worked  for  the 
American  Steel  &  Wire  Division  of  U.S.  Steel. 
From  1943  to  1958,  he  was  chief  metallurgist 
for  Gillette  Safety  Razor  Co. 

Mr.  Forsberg  belonged  to  the  American  So- 
ciety for  Metals,  the  American  Institute  of 
Mining  and  Metallurgical  Engineers,  the 
American  Society  of  Swedish  Engineers,  the 
Masons  and  the  Tech  Old-Timers.  He  was  a 
registered  professional  engineer  in  Massachu- 
setts. 

Earl  R.  Smiddy,  Sr.,  '26,  died  December  19, 
1982,  in  Framingham  (MA)  Union  Hospital  at 
the  age  of  80.  He  was  born  on  September  16, 
1902,  in  Southborough,  MA. 

After  receiving  his  BSEE,  he  was  for  many 
years  with  Gamewell  Co.  and  Honeywell,  both 
in  Newton.  A  registered  professional  engineer 
in  Massachusetts.  New  Hampshire  and  Con- 
necticut, he  retired  in  1970. 

Besides  being  a  member  of  Theta  Chi.  Mr. 
Smiddy  belonged  to  St.  Matthew's  Church  and 
the  American  Legion  of  Southborough.  the  New 
England  Fire  Chiefs'  Association,  the  Profes- 
sional Engineers  Association  and  the  Inter- 
national Municipal  Signal  Association.  In  World 


War  II,  he  served  as  a  lieutenant  commander 
in  the  Navy. 

Howard  B.  Thomson,  '26,  passed  away  on  No- 
vember 13,  1982,  in  Clearwater,  FL.  He  was 
born  on  June  9,  1902,  in  Worcester. 

Following  his  graduation  as  an  electrical  en- 
gineer, he  joined  New  York  Telephone.  In  1966, 
he  retired  as  a  supervisor  for  the  firm  in  Syr- 
acuse. In  1968,  he  retired  permanently  from 
Thomson  Associates  (building  engineering). 

A  professional  engineer  in  New  York  State, 
Mr.  Thomson  was  active  with  the  Boys'  Club, 
the  Masons  and  various  church  choirs.  He  be- 
longed to  Theta  Chi,  Skull  and  the  Poly  Club. 

Edward  F.  Cahalen,  '27,  a  retired  chemist,  died 
on  November  27,  1982.  in  Livermore,  CA.  A 
native  of  Lenoxdale,  MA,  he  was  born  on  May 
27,  1901. 

During  his  career,  he  was  with  Seamless 
Rubber  Co.,  New  Haven,  CT.  Ross  &  Roberts 
Co.,  Stratford,  and  Van  Lew  Rubber  Co.,  New 
Haven,  from  which  he  retired  in  the  1960s. 

A  member  of  ATO,  Mr.  Cahalen  had  served 
as  president  of  the  New  Haven  chapter  of  the 
WPI  Alumni  Association,  as  council  repre- 
sentative and  as  class  treasurer.  He  was  a  past 
president  of  the  Fathers'  Club  of  Notre  Dame 
High  School  in  New  Haven  and  a  member  of 
St.  Peter's  Church.  He  was  the  father  of  Paul 
Cahalen,  '63,  and  John  Cahalen,  '67. 

Frank  S.  Finlayson,  '31,  of  Brewster,  MA,  a 


32        WPI  JOURNAL 


retired  professor  of  mechanical  engineering  at 
WPI,  died  on  November  29,  1982,  in  New  Eng- 
land Deaconess  Hospital,  Boston,  MA.  He  was 
born  in  Worcester  on  November  6,  1908. 

He  received  his  BSME  in  1931  and  his  MSME 
in  1945.  Joining  the  WPI  staff  as  an  instructor 
in  1937,  he  was  named  assistant  professor  in 
1943,  associate  professor  in  1947  and  professor 
in  1958.  He  retired  in  1974. 

A  research  analyst,  consultant,  and  author, 
during  his  career  he  was  associated  with  Har- 
rington &  Richardson,  Inc.,  Alden  Hydraulic 
Laboratory,  Harvard  University,  Whitin  Ma- 
chine Works,  L.  J.  Barrett  Co.,  Rice  Barton 
Corp.  and  Allis  Chalmers  Corp. 

In  1960  Prof.  Finlayson  participated  in  the 
first  conference  on  engineering  design  educa- 
tion at  Case  Institute.  He  belonged  to  the 
ASME,  the  American  Society  for  Engineering 
Education,  the  Tech  Old-Timers  and  Sigma  Xi. 
During  World  War  II,  he  taught  flying  at  Graf- 
ton Airport.  He  was  the  father  of  David  Fin- 
layson, '61,  and  Dana  Finlayson,  '67. 

Walter  Rutman,  '30,  a  Rhode  Island  publisher, 
died  December  8,  1982,  in  Providence  at  the 
age  of  74. 

A  native  of  Worcester,  he  received  his  BS 
in  chemistry.  He  belonged  to  AEP. 

Mr.  Rutman  owned  the  Herald  Press,  pub- 
lisher of  the  Rhode  Island  Herald,  and  Ondine 
Publishing  Company.  Formerly  he  was  the 
owner  and  publisher  of  The  East  Providence 
Post  and  The  Seekonk  Star. 

He  was  a  member  of  the  New  England  Press 
Association,  the  National  Association  of  Photo 
Lithographers  and  the  Ledgemont  Country 
Club.  An  honorary  board  member  of  Sandy 
Lane  Country  Club  in  Barbados,  he  also  was 
an  honorary  director  of  Trinity  Square  Rep- 
ertory Company. 

Edward  H.  Rouse,  '31,  of  Saint  Louis  Park, 
MN,  died  on  October  5,  1982.  He  was  born  in 
Bristol,  CT,  on  July  18,  1907. 

Graduated  as  an  electrical  engineer  from  WPI, 
he  belonged  to  Sigma  Xi. 

During  his  career,  Mr.  Rouse  was  with  Fac- 
tory Insurance  Association  and  Automatic 
Sprinkler  Company  of  America,  from  which 
he  retired  in  1958. 

Mr.  Rouse,  a  registered  mechanical  engi- 
neer, was  a  member  of  several  professional  so- 
cieties, as  well  as  the  Masons  and  of  Shriners. 
He  was  the  brother  of  Wesley  Rouse,  '36. 

Donald  S.  Clough,  '35,  of  Andover,  CT,  passed 
away  on  November  23,  1982,  a  few  days  before 
his  71st  birthday. 

For  many  years,  he  was  with  Pratt  &  Whit- 
ney Aircraft,  East  Hartford,  from  which  he  was 
retired  as  assistant  design  project  engineer. 

A  member  of  Theta  Chi,  he  also  was  active 
in  the  PTA  and  the  Boy  Scouts. 

Adam  Z.  Pizan,  '36,  a  retired  civil  engineer 
from  the  Massachusetts  Department  of  Public 
Works  (DPW),  died  at  his  home  in  West  Boyl- 
ston,  MA,  in  December.  He  was  69. 

With  the  exception  of  military  service  in  World 
War  II,  Mr.  Pizan  had  been  with  the  DPW 
since  1937.  He  retired  in  1978. 

A  Navy  veteran,  he  served  as  a  chief  car- 
penter's mate  in  the  war,  holding  the  post  of 
construction  inspector  on  the  Panama  Canal 
during  major  lock  construction. 


FEEDBACK 


Editor: 

I  enjoyed  the  article  "The  Plan: 
Is  It  Working?"  in  your  October  1982  is- 
sue. As  a  member  of  the  class  of  1976  I 
can  look  back  on  the  advantage  of  having 
project  work  as  an  integral  part  of  my 
undergraduate  education. 

I  would  like  to  point  out  an  error  on  p. 
12  in  the  article  which  perhaps  dilutes  the 
impact  and  innovation  of  what  an  IQP 
[Interactive  Qualifying  Project]  can  be. 
The  picture  of  the  "computer  science  ma- 
jor" with  the  "fourth  graders"  at  a  com- 
puter terminal  was  taken  on  a  field  trip 
to  the  computer  center  at  WPI  as  part  of 
an  IQP  under  the  guidance  of  Prof.  L.  H. 
Berka  of  the  Chemistry  Department.  The 
photograph,  however,  was  taken  in  1975. 
Those  fourth  graders  are  now  seniors  in 
high  school  with  some  of  their  college  ap- 
plications potentially  now  in  at  WPI. 


Home  computers  were  virtually  non- 
existent when  this  photograph  was  taken. 
In  the  December  1982  issue  of  Scientific 
American,  an  article  on  personal  com- 
puters states,  "In  1975  a  device  flexible 
enough  to  be  considered  the  first  com- 
mercially available  personal  computer  was 
developed  by  MITS,  Inc."  This  year  the 
personal  computer  market  is  expected  to 
approach  $3  billion  in  sales.  Through  an 
IQP,  the  students  in  that  picture  had  their 
first  experience  with  a  programmable  ma- 
chine in  a  world  which  is  now  surrounding 
them  with  such  devices.  I  hope  and  be- 
lieve they  are  the  better  for  it. 

I  feel  that  the  IQP  is  an  important  part 
of  the  Plan  and  enjoyed  the  freedom  I 
encountered  with  mine. 

Yours  truly, 

the  Chemistry  major  in  the  picture, 

Waher  X.  Zukas,  '76 


Mr.  Pizan,  a  Worcester  native,  belonged  to 
the  Massachusetts  Society  of  Professional  En- 
gineers and  Registered  Surveyors. 

Ravindra  L.  Kirloskar,  '38,  a  well-known  in- 
dustrial leader  in  India,  died  in  Kuala  Lumpur 
on  November  4,  1982. 

He  was  born  on  January  14,  1917,  in  Kir- 
loskarvadi,  India.  After  receiving  his  BSEE, 
he  spent  two  years  as  a  graduate  student  ap- 
prentice at  Westinghouse  Electric,  East  Pitts- 
burgh, PA.  He  then  joined  Kirloskar  Brothers 
Ltd.  in  Kirloskarvadi.  In  1947,  he  became  the 
founder  and  general  manager  of  Kirloskar 
Electric  Co.,  a  large  manufacturer  of  electric 
motors  in  Bangalore,  India.  At  the  time  of  his 
death,  he  was  chairman  and  managing  director. 

In  1974,  Mr.  Kirloskar  received  the  Robert 
H.  Goddard  award  from  WPI.  He  had  been 
an  associate  member  of  the  AIEE  and  presi- 
dent of  the  Institute  of  Management,  as  well 
as  president  of  the  Quality  Control  Associa- 
tion. He  was  the  father  of  Vijay  Kirloskar,  '74. 

Dr.  Amet  "Larry"  Powell,  '38,  died  at  his  home 
in  Wayland,  MA,  on  November  23,  1982.  He 
was  67  years  old. 

A  native  of  Worcester,  he  graduated  as  a 
chemist.  In  1940  he  earned  his  MS  from  WPI 
and  in  1956  his  PhD  from  Clark  University.  He 
completed  postdoctoral  work  at  Oxford  Uni- 
versity in  England. 

During  his  career,  he  was  with  Dow  Chem- 
ical Co.,  Sylvania  Electric  Products,  Inc.,  and 
the  Office  of  Naval  Research  in  Boston,  where 
he  served  as  deputy  director  and  chief  scientist. 
He  retired  from  the  Office  of  Naval  Research 
in  1979,  following  32  years  of  federal  service. 

A  member  of  Sigma  Xi,  Dr.  Powell  was  also 
a  former  officer  and  national  committee  mem- 
ber for  the  ACS.  He  was  affiliated  with  the 
American  Academy  of  Sciences,  the  New  York 
Academy  of  Sciences,  the  American  Associa- 
tion for  the  Advancement  of  Science,  the  Ki- 


wanis  Club,  the  Masons  and  the  Methodist 
Church. 

Dr.  Irving  A.  Breger,  '41,  a  research  chemist 
with  the  U.S.  Geological  Survey  for  28  years, 
died  in  Washington,  DC,  on  October  13,  1982. 
He  was  62  years  old. 

He  was  an  authority  in  geochemistry,  espe- 
cially in  the  study  of  the  relationship  between 
fossil  fuels  and  uranium.  He  retired  from  the 
Geological  Survey  in  1980.  Since  1975,  he  had 
been  a  consultant  to  the  Los  Alamos  scientific 
laboratory.  At  the  time  of  his  death,  he  was 
editor  of  Organic  Geochemistry ,  a  technical 
publication. 

A  native  of  Boston,  he  received  his  BS  in 
chemistry.  Later,  he  received  his  master's  de- 
gree and  PhD  from  MIT.  A  member  of  Sigma 
Xi,  he  had  also  been  awarded  Fulbright  and 
Guggenheim  fellowships.  He  belonged  to  AEP. 

A  fellow  of  the  American  Institute  of  Chem- 
ists, he  had  also  belonged  to  the  American  Oil 
Chemists  Society  and  the  International  Asso- 
ciation of  Sedimentologists.  He  helped  to  es- 
tablish the  geochemistry  division  of  the  Amer- 
ican Chemical  Society. 

J.  Lawrence  Krause,  '41,  a  lifelong  resident  of 
Millville,  NJ,  died  on  October  26,  1982,  at  the 
age  of  64. 

Graduated  as  a  mechanical  engineer  from 
WPI,  he  belonged  to  Lambda  Chi  Alpha. 

In  January  of  1982,  Mr.  Krause,  a  utility 
maintenance  engineer,  retired  from  Wheaton 
Glass  Co.,  Millville,  where  he  had  been  em- 
ployed for  38  years. 

Mr.  Krause  had  previously  belonged  to  the 
ASME  and  ASLE.  Active  with  the  Methodist 
Church,  he  was  a  charter  member  and  past 
president  of  the  Y's  Men's  Club,  as  well  as  a 
charter  member  and  assistant  director  of  the 
local  American  Legion  chorus. 

During  World  War  II,  he  held  the  rank  of 
captain  with  the  Army  Air  Corps.
